{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Commercial Vehicle Safety Alliance (CVSA) appreciates this opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, titled “Clarification of Regional Emergency Declaration Rules.” As an international not-for-profit organization representing state, provincial, and federal commercial motor vehicle (CMV) safety enforcement agencies and industry in Canada, Mexico, and the United States, CVSA is dedicated to improving CMV safety across North America. Our expertise lies in developing and promoting uniform CMV safety enforcement standards, policies, and procedures through our North American Standard Inspection Program.

CVSA strongly supports FMCSA’s proposal to extend the automatic emergency relief period following a regional declaration from 14 days to 30 days. This restoration is crucial for ensuring uniform enforcement standards, managing the operational burden on state inspection workforces, and facilitating the integration of technology-enabled roadside safety systems during critical emergency response operations.

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA’s NPRM proposes to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the duration of automatic regulatory relief following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days. This change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA, and specifically seeks to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023. The proposed rule would revert the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to October 2023, typically under 49 CFR 390.23.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
CVSA’s support for the 30-day automatic relief period is grounded in the real-world operational challenges and inconsistencies experienced by our state and federal enforcement members under the current 14-day limit. These impacts directly affect uniform enforcement standards, strain state inspection resources, and highlight the need for enhanced technological support.

**Impacts on Uniform Enforcement Standards:**
The 14-day automatic relief window creates significant operational inconsistencies within the North American Standard Inspection Program. CVSA coordinates more than three million roadside inspections annually across North American jurisdictions [9]. When the automatic relief period was shortened, inspectors conducting Level I through Level VI inspections faced a compressed and often unclear enforcement window during regional emergencies. Regional disasters—such as hurricanes, floods, and wildfires—routinely extend well beyond 14 days in their acute operational phase, meaning inspectors were required to resume full FMCSR enforcement while emergency supply chains were still actively operating under crisis conditions [1]. This created a patchwork enforcement environment: some jurisdictions interpreted the 14-day cutoff strictly while others applied discretion, directly undermining the cross-jurisdictional consistency that is the foundational purpose of the North American Standard Inspection Program [4], [5].

Under the current 14-day limit, state enforcement officers conducting roadside inspections during major regional emergencies face a compressed and unpredictable enforcement window. When emergency conditions persist beyond 14 days, as they frequently do in large-scale disasters, inspectors must abruptly revert to full FMCSR compliance checks on carriers still actively engaged in relief operations [1]. This creates confusion at the roadside: officers must determine in real time whether a carrier’s relief activities fall within an extended FMCSA national declaration or have reverted to standard enforcement, a distinction that is difficult to make consistently across dozens of state jurisdictions simultaneously. The result is uneven enforcement, potential friction with relief carriers, and erosion of the inspection program’s credibility and consistency—core values of the North American Standard Inspection Program.

Furthermore, the 14-day limit creates enforcement coordination chaos at the state level, forcing inspectors to apply inconsistent standards mid-emergency when operational conditions have not materially changed. When a 14-day automatic relief window expires mid-disaster—as occurred during Hurricanes Helene and Milton in October 2024, where FMCSA was compelled to issue multiple sequential declarations (Nos. 2024-008 and 2024-010) and then extend relief through November 26, 2024—state enforcement officers face an untenable situation [2], [3], [7]. They must either resume full enforcement against carriers still actively engaged in disaster relief operations, or await a new federal extension declaration before resuming consistent cross-jurisdictional standards. This patchwork creates a window of enforcement ambiguity that undermines the foundational purpose of the North American Standard Inspection Program, which depends on predictable, harmonized rules across all participating jurisdictions. Officers in affected states cannot efficiently coordinate with neighboring states when the legal status of relief exemptions differs by days or declaration sequence.

CVSA's Operational Policy 5 and the North American Standard Inspection Procedures establish clear protocols for when inspectors must place a vehicle or driver out of service [4], [5]. However, when an emergency declaration suspends specific FMCSRs, inspectors face an unresolved question: if a driver is found in violation of a suspended regulation during the relief period, does the inspector have authority—or an obligation—to issue an out-of-service order? Restoring the 30-day window without addressing this gap will perpetuate inconsistent enforcement outcomes across jurisdictions, with some inspectors issuing out-of-service orders for suspended violations and others not, undermining both safety and the uniformity of the inspection program. This inconsistency also affects the integrity of FMCSA's safety data, as out-of-service violations recorded during emergency relief periods may not accurately reflect carrier safety performance.

**Impacts on State Inspection Workforce Limitations and Funding:**
The 14-day automatic relief period imposes a disproportionate administrative burden on state-level inspection workforces that are already resource-constrained [1], [8]. When the automatic relief period expires at 14 days and FMCSA has not yet issued a formal extension, state inspection supervisors must rapidly communicate updated enforcement postures to field officers—many of whom are simultaneously managing elevated inspection volumes due to the emergency itself [1]. During Hurricane Helene and Milton response operations, FMCSA was compelled to issue multiple extension declarations [7], meaning state agencies had to track and communicate at least three separate declaration documents to field personnel. Each transition point between relief and non-relief status introduces a window of enforcement inconsistency. A single, predictable 30-day window eliminates at least one of these transition points, reducing the administrative overhead on state agencies and lowering the risk of an inspector inadvertently citing a relief-eligible carrier.

Under CVSA’s North American Standard Inspection Program, state-level inspection personnel must track which carriers and drivers are operating under emergency relief exemptions and which are subject to full FMCSR enforcement at any given time. The 14-day window compresses this administrative tracking burden into the most chaotic phase of disaster response, when inspectors are simultaneously managing surge traffic volumes, damaged infrastructure, and reduced staffing due to inspectors themselves being affected by regional emergencies [8]. With major disasters now being declared at a rate of approximately every four days in 2024 per FEMA data, state enforcement agencies face near-continuous cycles of exemption activation and expiration, creating a persistent administrative burden that the 14-day window exacerbates rather than alleviates. A 30-day window aligns relief duration with the operational reality of disaster response timelines, reducing the frequency of mid-operation enforcement transitions that consume limited inspector time and create jurisdictional inconsistencies across state lines. The 14-day limit creates repeated, disruptive enforcement recalibration cycles that undermine the consistency and reliability of our North American Standard Inspection Program during extended regional disasters [6], [7].

**Impacts on Technology-Enabled Roadside Safety Systems:**
While restoring the 30-day window is a critical step, CVSA believes that its effectiveness can be significantly enhanced through the integration of technology-enabled roadside safety systems. Under the current 14-day automatic exemption, CVSA member inspectors at roadside enforcement points must rapidly shift enforcement posture—often mid-disaster—when the automatic relief expires and carriers must seek extensions or return to full compliance. This inconsistency directly undermines the uniformity that is the foundation of the North American Standard Inspection Program. A fully digital system requires coordination across an entire operation [6].

When carriers operate under ambiguous exemption status because a 14-day window has lapsed but an extension has not yet been formally issued, enforcement officers face impossible choices: cite carriers who may be legally exempt, or waive enforcement against carriers who may not be. This ambiguity exposes state agencies to legal challenges and undermines the credibility of the inspection program. The mismatch between state emergency management timelines and the federal 14-day automatic relief window creates a structural misalignment that the proposed 30-day restoration would correct. CVSA supports restoring the 30-day period and simultaneously committing to publishing a centralized, machine-readable emergency declaration registry—referenced in existing FMCSA guidance [6]—that state enforcement agencies can integrate into their dispatch and inspection management systems. This would allow real-time verification of relief eligibility at the roadside, reducing reliance on paper declarations carried by drivers and minimizing enforcement errors during the transition back to standard compliance at day 30.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
CVSA urges FMCSA to finalize the proposed rule to restore the 30-day automatic emergency relief period and to consider the following additional recommendations to enhance safety, ensure consistent enforcement, and support state inspection workforces:

**To Achieve Uniform Enforcement Standards:**
1.  **Finalize the 30-Day Automatic Relief Period:** FMCSA should finalize the proposed restoration of the 30-day automatic relief period without modification [1].
2.  **Issue Standardized Guidance and Notification Protocols:** FMCSA should issue standardized guidance documents and real-time notification protocols to state enforcement agencies at the time of any emergency declaration, clearly specifying the relief period start and end dates, covered commodities, and geographic scope [2]. FMCSA should also consider codifying a standardized notification protocol to CVSA as part of the final rule to eliminate the current information lag that compounds coordination difficulties.
3.  **Clarify Out-of-Service Criteria During Relief Periods:** FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period AND simultaneously issue a companion guidance document—developed in coordination with CVSA—that explicitly addresses inspector authority to issue out-of-service orders during the relief period. This guidance should clarify: (1) which out-of-service criteria remain applicable when underlying regulations are suspended; (2) how inspectors should document emergency-period inspections in FMCSA's data systems to flag them for appropriate analytical treatment; and (3) whether carriers operating under emergency relief are subject to the same post-inspection follow-up requirements [4], [5]. This guidance should be incorporated into the North American Standard Inspection Procedures before the rule takes effect.

**To Support Adequate State Funding and Workforce Management for Inspections:**
1.  **Acknowledge Administrative Burden:** FMCSA should explicitly acknowledge in the final rule's preamble that the 14-day period established in October 2023 was inconsistent with observed disaster duration patterns and created a disproportionate administrative burden on resource-constrained state enforcement agencies [1].
2.  **Conduct Post-Implementation Review:** FMCSA should commit to publishing a post-implementation review within 24 months of the final rule's effective date, examining whether 30 days remains appropriate given climate trends toward longer-duration weather emergencies, and whether a tiered automatic relief period—calibrated to the geographic scope of the declaration—should be considered in future rulemaking [2].

**To Facilitate Technology-Enabled Roadside Safety Systems:**
1.  **Publish Real-Time Exemption Status Data:** FMCSA should commit to publishing real-time, machine-readable exemption status data through its existing enforcement technology platforms (e.g., the FMCSA Portal and systems integrated with PrePass and roadside inspection databases) so that inspectors can instantly verify a carrier's exemption status without manual lookups [6], [7].
2.  **Develop Electronic Notification Protocols:** FMCSA should develop a standardized electronic notification protocol that automatically alerts state enforcement agencies when a regional declaration is issued or expires, enabling proactive workforce preparation rather than reactive enforcement pivots [6], [7].

[CONCLUSION]
CVSA strongly supports FMCSA’s proposal to restore the automatic emergency relief period following a regional declaration to 30 days. This change is essential for enhancing public safety, ensuring consistent and uniform enforcement standards across jurisdictions, and alleviating the operational and administrative burdens placed on state CMV safety inspection workforces during critical emergency response operations. We appreciate FMCSA’s responsiveness to the real-world challenges created by the 14-day limit and stand ready to collaborate with the agency on implementation and future enhancements to emergency relief protocols.

Sincerely,

Commercial Vehicle Safety Alliance

### References
[1] https://public-inspection.federalregister.gov/2026-00268.pdf
[2] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/archive-emergency-declarations-2024
[3] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/ssc-extension-emergency-declarations-hurricanes-helene-and-milton-11-22-2024
[4] https://www.cvsa.org/wp-content/uploads/Operational-Policy-5.pdf
[5] https://csa.fmcsa.dot.gov/safetyplanner/documents/396%20Forms/Inspection%20Procedure.pdf
[6] https://jjkellercompliancenetwork.com/news/fmcsa-limits-scope-of-emergency-exemption
[7] https://nefi.com/news-publications/recent-news/federal-and-state-hours-of-service-waivers-december-2025---january-2026
[8] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/texas-emergency-declaration-2024

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Association of Motor Vehicle Administrators (AAMVA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, Clarification to the Applicability of Emergency Exemptions; Response to Petitions for Reconsideration. AAMVA is a nonprofit, tax-exempt educational association representing state and provincial officials in the United States and Canada responsible for the administration and enforcement of motor vehicle laws. Our members include state Driver Licensing Agencies (SDLAs) that issue Commercial Driver's Licenses (CDLs), manage driver records, and ensure compliance with federal CDL program standards, including interstate driver record sharing and medical certification integration.

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA's proposal seeks to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, by extending the automatic exemption period for regional emergency declarations from 14 days to 30 days. This change would restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, particularly concerning the provisions under 49 CFR 390.23 and 390.25. AAMVA strongly supports this proposed revision, as the current 14-day limit is demonstrably insufficient for state motor vehicle agencies to effectively manage CDL program requirements, maintain interstate data integrity, and integrate critical medical certification information during emergencies without creating significant operational burdens and compliance risks.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
AAMVA's support for restoring the 30-day automatic emergency relief period stems from several critical operational and compliance challenges our member states face under the current 14-day window:

1.  **Operational Burden on State DMVs for CDL Licensing and Testing Standards Coordination:** When a regional emergency is declared, AAMVA member state DMVs must rapidly reconfigure their CDL licensing workflows, testing schedules, and interoperability protocols with neighboring states. Our member agencies have consistently reported that 14 days is inadequate to complete even the most basic administrative adjustments—including suspending third-party testing site operations, notifying examiners, and updating the Commercial Driver's License Information System (CDLIS) to flag affected records [6, 7]. The compressed 14-day window forces DMV administrators to make rushed decisions about which CDL applicants and renewals to process under emergency conditions, increasing the risk of incomplete or inaccurate records entering the national driver database. As demonstrated by the Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse in Maryland (March 2024), FMCSA itself was compelled to issue multiple extension declarations under 49 CFR 390.25—effectively acknowledging that 14 days is operationally inadequate [1]. Each extension declaration requires AAMVA member agencies to re-process system updates, re-notify affected licensees, and re-synchronize interstate records, multiplying administrative burden rather than reducing it. A single 30-day automatic window would allow state DMV administrators to execute one coordinated compliance cycle rather than a series of reactive, resource-intensive extensions.

2.  **State DMV System Interoperability Challenges for Enhanced Interstate Driver Record Sharing:** The 14-day limit creates a cascading interoperability crisis for state DMV systems that cannot be resolved within the compressed timeframe, forcing states to implement patchwork manual workarounds that undermine CDL data integrity across jurisdictions. When a Governor declares a regional emergency, state DMV systems must propagate exemption status information across CDLIS and the Problem Driver Pointer System (PDPS) to ensure that CDL holders operating under emergency relief are not flagged as non-compliant in neighboring states. Under the 14-day window, state DMV IT teams face an immediate race against the clock to receive formal notification, update system parameters, coordinate with adjacent state DMVs whose drivers may be crossing into the affected region, and communicate exemption boundaries to enforcement personnel—all before the exemption lapses. As demonstrated by the Hurricane Helene and Milton declarations of October 2024 (Declaration Nos. 2024-008 and 2024-010), FMCSA itself was compelled to issue extension declarations because emergency conditions had not abated within the initial window [2, 3]. Each extension declaration requires state DMV systems to re-process the exemption, re-notify stakeholders, and re-synchronize interstate data feeds—multiplying administrative burden rather than reducing it. AAMVA's member agencies report that the first 7-10 days of any major regional emergency are consumed by disaster response coordination, leaving fewer than 7 days of functional administrative capacity under the 14-day rule to manage system updates before the exemption expires and manual override procedures must begin.

3.  **Compliance Cost and Risk for CDL Fraud and Masking Detection:** The current 14-day automatic relief window creates a dangerous 'mid-emergency cliff' in CDL fraud detection and masking prevention, as state DMV systems cannot safely re-engage full compliance screening within 14 days when emergency conditions are still ongoing. CDL fraud and masking—where drivers obtain licenses in one state while concealing disqualifying records from another—is a persistent threat that AAMVA's member agencies combat through continuous CDLIS data exchange. During an emergency, some states temporarily relax verification timelines or allow provisional CDL issuances. Under the 14-day rule, if an emergency persists beyond day 14 (as the archived FMCSA declarations show is common), states face an abrupt compliance deadline while emergency conditions remain active [3]. This forces DMV administrators to either prematurely reinstate full screening protocols in a chaotic environment—risking processing errors that create masking opportunities—or seek individual exemptions from FMCSA, which introduces administrative burden and inconsistency across state lines [8, 9]. Paradoxically, a 14-day relief window that expires before SDLAs can process backlogged medical certification data creates a window of ambiguity in which a driver's CDL record may not accurately reflect their current medical status—not due to fraud, but due to administrative lag [4, 5]. This ambiguity is more dangerous than a clearly defined 30-day relief period, because it creates inconsistent record states across the SDLA network that are difficult to audit and reconcile. The 30-day window provides a stable, predictable compliance horizon that allows AAMVA and its member states to maintain coordinated, uniform CDL data integrity standards throughout the emergency period rather than scrambling at an arbitrary 14-day cutoff.

4.  **Operational Burden for Medical Certification Data Exchange Requirements and Integration:** The 14-day window is demonstrably insufficient for state Driver Licensing Agencies (SDLAs) to complete the medical certification data exchange updates required during regional emergencies, creating compliance gaps that undermine CDL integrity. When a regional emergency is declared, AAMVA member SDLAs must simultaneously manage disaster-response staffing disruptions, system outages, and backlogs in processing medical certification data transmitted electronically through the National Registry II (NRII) infrastructure. Under the 14-day window, states face a hard deadline that does not account for the cascading delays in electronic data transmission from certified medical examiners to SDLAs—delays that are compounded during emergencies [4]. FMCSA's own experience with the NRII transition demonstrates this reality: the agency issued multiple exemptions and extensions, including a six-month exemption allowing paper medical certificates to remain valid for up to 60 days post-exam, precisely because the electronic data exchange pipeline cannot be compressed to meet arbitrary short deadlines [5]. A 30-day window aligns with the operational reality of SDLA data processing cycles and gives states adequate time to reconcile CDL medical certification records without creating artificial compliance violations for drivers who are actively supporting emergency relief operations.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on the analysis above, AAMVA offers the following recommendations to support CDL program improvements, enhance interstate driver record sharing, and integrate medical certification with state licensing systems:

1.  **Restore the 30-Day Automatic Relief Period:** FMCSA should finalize the proposed rule restoring the 30-day automatic relief period as proposed. This will reduce administrative burden on state DMVs, allow for more effective coordination of CDL licensing and testing standards, and provide adequate time for interstate data synchronization through CDLIS and PDPS.

2.  **Update CDLIS Technical Guidance for Regional Emergency Exemptions:** Concurrent with the final rule's implementation, FMCSA should direct that CDLIS technical guidance be updated to include a standardized 'regional emergency exemption' status flag that state systems can activate and deactivate in a single transaction, reducing the multi-step manual processing currently required during each extension cycle. AAMVA recommends that FMCSA convene a working group with state DMV administrators to develop these technical specifications.

3.  **Establish Standardized Electronic Notification Protocols for Emergency Declarations:** FMCSA should issue standardized electronic notification protocols to state DMV systems at the moment a regional declaration is triggered, so that CDLIS and PDPS records can be updated automatically rather than through manual state-by-state processes. AAMVA further recommends that FMCSA establish a formal data exchange standard specifying the machine-readable format and transmission timeline for emergency declaration notices to state DMV systems, reducing the lag between declaration issuance and system-level compliance status updates.

4.  **Clarify Medical Certification Data Exchange Obligations:** FMCSA should clarify in the rule's preamble that the 30-day automatic relief period applies to medical certification data exchange obligations under 49 CFR Part 383 and Part 391, so that SDLAs and drivers are not penalized for data transmission delays that are outside their control during declared emergencies. AAMVA further recommends that FMCSA coordinate with SDLAs through established AAMVA channels to issue standardized guidance on how states should document and flag CDL records during the relief period to prevent masking of disqualifying medical conditions once the exemption expires.

5.  **Develop Post-Emergency CDL Record Reconciliation Protocol:** FMCSA should adopt the 30-day restoration and simultaneously issue supplemental guidance requiring state DMVs to complete a standardized post-emergency CDL record reconciliation protocol within the final 10 days of the relief window (days 21-30). This protocol should include mandatory CDLIS cross-checks, medical certification status verification, and Clearinghouse query requirements for any driver who operated under emergency exemption. AAMVA offers to collaborate with FMCSA in developing this protocol as part of existing CDL program coordination mechanisms.

6.  **Maintain Continuous CDLIS Reporting Obligations:** FMCSA should codify a requirement that state jurisdictions maintain continuous CDLIS reporting obligations even when Hours of Service (HOS) and other operational regulations are waived, ensuring that the extended relief window does not create a corresponding gap in fraud detection capability. As a condition of the automatic relief, states should submit a CDL data integrity attestation to FMCSA by day 21 confirming that CDLIS records for all CDL holders operating under emergency relief remain current and free of unresolved masking flags.

[CONCLUSION]
AAMVA strongly supports FMCSA's proposal to extend the automatic regulatory relief period for regional emergency declarations from 14 days to 30 days. This change is essential for enabling state motor vehicle agencies to effectively manage CDL programs, enhance interstate driver record sharing, and integrate medical certification data with licensing systems, thereby reducing operational burdens, improving compliance, and safeguarding CDL data integrity during critical emergency response periods. We urge FMCSA to finalize this proposed rule and work collaboratively with AAMVA and our member states to develop the necessary technical guidance and protocols to ensure its successful implementation.

### References
[1] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008
[2] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/extension-emergency-declarations-nos-2024-008-and-2024-010-florida-georgia-north-carolina
[3] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/archive-emergency-declarations-2024
[4] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/sites/fmcsa.dot.gov/files/2024-01/Medical%20Examiner%27s%20Handbook%202024%20Edition_0.pdf
[5] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/newsroom/fmcsa-issues-temporary-exemption-support-nrii-transition
[6] https://www.apta.com/wp-content/uploads/APTA-Impact-of-CDL-Under-the-Hood-Testing-Requirement-Nov-2024.pdf
[7] https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dmv/driver-services/commercial-drivers/modernized-commercial-driver-license-skills-testing
[8] https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/01/09/2026-00268/clarification-to-the-applicability-of-emergency-exemptions-response-to-petitions-for-reconsideration
[9] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/regulations/federal-register-documents/2026-00268

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Owner-Operator Independent Drivers Association (OOIDA) submits these comments in response to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, proposing to extend the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from 14 days to 30 days. As the largest trade association representing the interests of independent professional truck drivers, OOIDA strongly opposes this proposed extension. While we advocate for driver flexibility, we believe that an overly broad, one-size-fits-all 30-day automatic waiver period, as proposed, creates unintended safety consequences and disproportionately harms experienced owner-operators by enabling large carriers to pressure small businesses into unsafe and uncompensated extended operations under the guise of emergency relief. Our members, who are self-employed small business owners, require targeted, justifiable flexibility that does not undermine their safety, livelihoods, or the integrity of hours-of-service (HOS) regulations.

[BACKGROUND]
The NPRM seeks to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs), specifically relating to the duration of automatic regulatory relief following a regional emergency declaration. The core proposal is to extend this automatic exemption period from the current 14 days to 30 days, applying to regional declarations issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA itself. This rulemaking is being proposed to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions to the 30-day limit that existed prior to that rule's issuance. While the NPRM does not explicitly cite specific CFR sections, these regulations typically fall under 49 CFR 390.23, which governs relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
OOIDA's opposition to the proposed 30-day automatic relief period is grounded in the operational realities faced by our members and the documented history of emergency declaration abuse, which creates unintended safety consequences and undermines genuine driver flexibility.

**Opposition to a One-Size-Fits-All, Blanket 30-Day Waiver Period:**
A blanket 30-day automatic waiver treats all regional emergencies identically, regardless of their scope, duration, or affected geography. This one-size-fits-all approach ignores the variability demonstrated by FMCSA's own emergency declaration archive [6]. For instance, the Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse declaration [6] affected a specific corridor, while Hurricane Helene declarations [2, 3] covered multiple states with different recovery timelines. Extending the automatic exemption to 30 days in all cases creates an operational hazard for owner-operators. Large carriers and brokers, equipped with legal teams, exploit the extended window to negotiate freight contracts that assume HOS flexibility, then pass enforcement risk downstream to the owner-operator who accepted the load. OOIDA members, operating under broker agreements, have limited bargaining power to refuse loads that implicitly depend on emergency exemption availability, particularly when the exemption window is broad and geographic boundaries are ambiguous. This structural asymmetry effectively transfers emergency freight revenue from small operators to large carriers at the exact moment when independent drivers are most needed and most vulnerable [6].

**Unintended Safety Consequences of Prolonged, Blanket Waivers:**
OOIDA members' livelihoods depend on HOS rules functioning as a genuine floor on working conditions, not a variable that large shippers and carriers can effectively suspend for month-long periods by invoking emergency declarations tied to foreseeable, recurring supply conditions. When HOS rules are waived for 30 days, the competitive pressure on owner-operators to match the extended availability of large-fleet drivers—who are salaried or on guaranteed minimums—is acute. Per-mile operators who decline extended hours during a 30-day waiver window risk losing broker relationships and load access to competitors willing to run longer [7]. The 2023 rule's narrowed definition of 'emergency' and shortened automatic period were intended to close exactly this loophole, and reversing the duration component while retaining the narrowed definition creates an inconsistent regulatory framework that disproportionately harms self-employed owner-operators. Extended blanket HOS waivers disproportionately harm self-employed owner-operators who compete against large fleets that exploit prolonged relief periods to pressure independent drivers into unsafe, uncompensated extended operations [7]. This constitutes an unintended safety consequence of prolonged, blanket waivers that prioritize supply chain management over driver well-being.

Furthermore, the historical record of FMCSA emergency declarations demonstrates that the agency has routinely issued and extended declarations for conditions—including seasonal fuel supply transitions and pipeline maintenance—that do not constitute the acute, life-threatening emergencies that HOS relief was designed to address [5]. A longer automatic window will further normalize the use of HOS waivers as a supply chain management tool at the expense of driver safety and fair compensation. When HOS rules are waived for 30 days, large carriers and brokers gain leverage to demand extended driving windows that small operators cannot refuse without losing freight. Unlike large fleets with dedicated safety departments and legal counsel, OOIDA members have no institutional buffer against broker or shipper pressure to maximize miles during relief windows. A 30-day automatic period—double the current 14-day limit—effectively doubles the window during which owner-operators face this coercive dynamic, with no guaranteed compensation increase for the added risk and fatigue. The 2023 final rule's reduction to 14 days was a direct acknowledgment that prolonged automatic relief creates systemic safety and labor pressure problems that fall hardest on the smallest operators [5].

**Administrative Burden and Lack of Genuine Driver Flexibility:**
OOIDA opposes the proposed 30-day extension as the primary mechanism for emergency relief because it fails to address the real administrative burden that owner-operators face when navigating the extension request process during the current 14-day window — a burden the agency itself has acknowledged creates 'uncertainty' for drivers in the field [7]. Owner-operators — who are self-employed small business owners without dedicated compliance staff, legal counsel, or regulatory affairs departments — bear a disproportionate share of the administrative overhead associated with monitoring extension request timelines, determining applicability of declarations to their specific routes and cargo, and making real-time operational decisions under regulatory uncertainty. Unlike large carriers with compliance teams, an OOIDA member driving solo through a disaster-affected region must simultaneously manage safety, dispatch, and regulatory compliance. When FMCSA's own records show that extension decisions have come 'close to when the automatic emergency exemption expired' [7], it is the small operator — not the fleet manager — who absorbs that uncertainty directly into their driving decisions and income. Under a per-mile compensation model, regulatory ambiguity that forces a driver to stop and wait for clarification translates immediately into lost revenue with no administrative backstop [7]. This is not genuine flexibility, but a compliance trap.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address the unintended safety consequences of prolonged blanket waivers, uphold the integrity of HOS regulations, and provide genuine, targeted flexibility to experienced owner-operators, OOIDA makes the following recommendations:

1.  **Retain the 14-Day Automatic Relief Period:** FMCSA should retain the 14-day automatic relief period established by the October 2023 final rule. This period provides sufficient initial response capability while limiting the window for potential abuse [7].
2.  **Develop a Structured Extension Mechanism:** If the agency believes longer emergencies require extended relief, it should instead create a structured extension mechanism requiring affirmative agency action—with public notice and a brief comment window—after the initial 14-day period expires. This would preserve rapid initial response capability while ensuring that extensions beyond 14 days are subject to documented justification, preventing routine or economically motivated declarations from becoming de facto deregulation of HOS rules for 30-day stretches [7].
3.  **Establish a Streamlined Notification System:** Rather than simply extending the automatic window to 30 days and reducing the number of extension requests, FMCSA should establish a streamlined, owner-operator-accessible notification system — such as a mobile-optimized dashboard or SMS/email alert — that provides real-time, plain-language confirmation of which declarations are active, which routes are covered, and when relief expires [4, 7]. This would reduce administrative burden and uncertainty for drivers in the field.
4.  **Create a Simplified Self-Certification Process for Small Carriers:** FMCSA should create a simplified, single-page self-certification process for small carriers (those with fewer than 5 power units) to request extensions without the same administrative filing burden imposed on large carriers [7, 8]. This tiered approach would preserve the agency's ability to monitor emergency scope while reducing the compliance asymmetry between large and small carriers.
5.  **Clarify Emergency Definition:** FMCSA should explicitly clarify in the final rule that recurring, foreseeable supply chain stress events—such as annual refinery seasonal transitions or predictable agricultural transport surges—do not qualify as emergencies under the revised 49 CFR § 390.5 definition, regardless of declaration duration [5].
6.  **Prohibit Contractual Requirements for Emergency Operations:** FMCSA should issue explicit guidance clarifying that no carrier — large or small — may contractually require a driver to operate under an emergency exemption as a condition of load acceptance. This would protect owner-operators from coercive pressure during waiver periods [8].

[CONCLUSION]
OOIDA emphasizes that true driver flexibility for experienced owner-operators must be balanced with robust safety standards and protections against exploitation. The proposed 30-day automatic waiver period, while seemingly offering flexibility, risks creating a one-size-fits-all regulation that invites abuse, pressures independent drivers into unsafe practices, and ultimately undermines the very safety goals of HOS rules. We urge FMCSA to reconsider the proposed extension and instead adopt a more targeted, accountable approach to emergency relief that protects the safety and livelihoods of all professional drivers.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
J.B. Hunt Transport Services Inc. ("J.B. Hunt") appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124. As one of North America's largest and most diversified transportation and logistics companies, J.B. Hunt operates a vast network of dedicated contract, truckload, and intermodal freight services across all 50 states. Our fleet, comprising over 12,000 company trucks and 25,000 trailers, routinely deploys significant capacity in direct support of emergency relief efforts, often involving multi-state operations and complex coordination with rail partners.

We conditionally support FMCSA's proposal to extend the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from 14 days to 30 days. This extension aligns more realistically with the operational realities of major regional emergencies, which frequently require sustained "direct assistance" well beyond a two-week window. However, J.B. Hunt is concerned that simply extending the duration without accompanying regulatory frameworks that address the unique complexities of intermodal operations, the role of advanced safety technology, and the potential future contributions of autonomous vehicles, could inadvertently create compliance ambiguities, operational inefficiencies, and safety risks.

[BACKGROUND]
The NPRM proposes a revision to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the automatic emergency exemption period from 14 days to 30 days, applicable to regional declarations issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA. This proposal specifically seeks to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the 30-day limit that existed prior to that rule. While the NPRM does not explicitly cite CFR sections, these regulations typically fall under **49 CFR 390.23** regarding relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
J.B. Hunt's experience during recent major emergencies, such as Hurricane Helene in September 2024 and the Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse in March 2024, demonstrates that the 14-day automatic relief window is demonstrably insufficient for managing complex, multi-state, or prolonged disruptions. These events underscored the need for a more robust and responsive regulatory framework for emergency relief.

**Operational Burden and Planning Uncertainty:**
Under the current 14-day automatic exemption, J.B. Hunt's operations teams face a compressed and unpredictable planning horizon. When FMCSA decisions on extension requests cannot be issued until close to the expiration of the automatic exemption – as FMCSA itself acknowledged in its rulemaking record [3] – our dispatchers, safety compliance officers, and driver managers are forced to make real-time decisions about whether to continue relief operations or stand down drivers to avoid potential Hours of Service (HOS) violations. This uncertainty is operationally costly: it requires us to maintain dedicated compliance staff on standby, creates scheduling conflicts with our intermodal rail-truck coordination commitments, and introduces liability exposure if relief operations inadvertently extend beyond the exemption window. The 30-day window would materially reduce this administrative burden and allow our dedicated fleet managers to plan driver rotations, rest cycles, and equipment repositioning over a more realistic operational horizon consistent with the actual duration of major disasters. For example, during Tropical Storm Francine in September 2024, Louisiana issued an emergency declaration effective September 10, expiring September 23, 2024 [1], demonstrating the typical limited window of automatic relief.

**Intermodal Operations Challenges:**
A single J.B. Hunt intermodal move from, for example, a Southeast rail ramp to a Midwest destination may originate in a state under a Governor's emergency declaration, transit through one or more additional states also under separate declarations issued on different dates, and terminate in a state outside any declaration zone. During Hurricane Helene, seven states issued declarations between September 23–27, 2024 [2], meaning under a 30-day automatic relief rule, the expiration dates would be staggered across a five-day window. For a driver operating an intermodal drayage move that crosses state lines, it is operationally and legally unclear which declaration governs HOS compliance at any given point, whether the most permissive or most restrictive state's relief terms apply, and how ELD systems should record hours when the regulatory regime changes mid-trip. This ambiguity is not theoretical: our Southeast intermodal operations regularly involve multi-state drayage moves, and our ELD and compliance systems must reflect a single, deterministic regulatory state for each driver at each moment.

Furthermore, intermodal drayage drivers operating under HOS relief face an immediate operational paradox: the relief exemption applies to the truck leg, but the rail leg remains on a fixed schedule that cannot be unilaterally extended. During Hurricane Helene, emergency declarations were issued across seven states simultaneously (FL, GA, NC, SC, TN, VA, WV) [2], disrupting intermodal flows across the entire Southeast corridor. Our drayage drivers operating under relief exemptions were legally permitted to extend their hours, but rail terminal dwell times, container availability, and interchange agreements with railroads remained governed by separate contractual and operational timelines that do not flex with FMCSA declarations [5]. The result is operational incoherence: drivers with extended HOS relief sitting at terminals waiting for rail assets that are metered and repositioned on railroad-controlled schedules, or conversely, drivers unable to complete legally-permitted extended runs because rail terminals close on fixed windows regardless of emergency status. The Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse response in March 2024 further demonstrated this, requiring FMCSA to issue multiple sequential extensions through at least May 8, 2024 [6], yet rail terminal operations and port access remained constrained by separate factors.

**Safety Technology & Data Integrity:**
J.B. Hunt operates a fleet of more than 12,000 company trucks equipped with integrated telematics systems that continuously collect ELD, GPS, speed, braking, and driver-behavior data. Our safety management infrastructure is built around 24/7 automated flagging of HOS violations and near-violations. When a regional emergency declaration activates automatic regulatory relief, our telematics platforms must be reconfigured – or exception logic must be manually coded – to avoid generating thousands of false-positive violation alerts for drivers operating lawfully under the exemption. Under the prior 14-day window, this reconfiguration cycle was compressed and frequently misaligned with actual emergency durations, forcing our safety operations center to issue manual overrides and creating data integrity gaps in our safety scoring records [4, 5]. Restoring the 30-day window reduces the frequency of mid-emergency reconfiguration events, but without clear agency guidance on how telematics records generated during the exemption period should be treated for SMS (Safety Measurement System) scoring purposes, J.B. Hunt faces litigation and audit exposure even when our drivers are fully compliant with the applicable relief provisions.

**Driver Fatigue & Retention:**
J.B. Hunt has made substantial investments in driver training, retention, and safety technology – including collision mitigation systems and electronic logging devices across our entire fleet [7]. Our safety culture and our ongoing autonomous vehicle technology development program are premised on the principle that driver fatigue is a primary risk factor in commercial motor vehicle accidents. Emergency relief operations are inherently high-stress, high-demand environments: drivers face unfamiliar routes, debris-laden roads, and compressed delivery schedules [1]. Extending the automatic exemption to 30 days without clarifying the boundaries of HOS relief could expose our drivers – and the public – to elevated fatigue risk over a prolonged period. It could also create competitive pressure from less safety-conscious carriers to push drivers harder during the exemption window, undermining the level playing field that responsible carriers like J.B. Hunt depend on. Our driver retention investment is also at risk: drivers who are overextended during emergency deployments are more likely to leave the industry, exacerbating the structural driver shortage.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure that the extension of automatic emergency relief to 30 days truly enhances safety, efficiency, and preparedness for future crises, J.B. Hunt urges FMCSA to adopt the following comprehensive regulatory frameworks and clarifications:

1.  **Intermodal-Specific Regulatory Frameworks:**
    *   **Standardized Multi-State Harmonization:** FMCSA should issue guidance clarifying that carriers operating under multi-state emergency declarations receive a single harmonized 30-day window rather than staggered, state-by-state timelines that create compliance complexity for carriers crossing state lines during a single relief mission. The agency should adopt a 'most permissive applicable declaration' standard for interstate intermodal moves, under which a driver operating under any valid state or FMCSA emergency declaration for any portion of a trip is covered for the full trip duration, provided the trip originates or terminates within a declared zone [2, 4].
    *   **FMCSA-STB Coordination:** Establish a formal coordination protocol between FMCSA and the Surface Transportation Board (STB) to ensure that rail terminal operating hour flexibility and intermodal yard access are considered in tandem with truck-side relief. This should include mechanisms to prevent intermodal carriers from being penalized for rail-caused delays that fall outside the HOS relief window.
    *   **Clear Intermodal Scope:** Any automatic relief declaration should include explicit language addressing whether the relief applies to the drayage leg only, the full intermodal move (including rail coordination), or both, so that carriers can make legally defensible operational decisions without ambiguity [2, 6].

2.  **Safety Technology Adoption Incentives & Data Integrity Standards:**
    *   **Telematics Data Treatment:** Accompany the final rule with a companion guidance document – or regulatory text amendment – that explicitly states that telematics and ELD records reflecting HOS activity during a validly declared emergency exemption period shall not be counted as violations in the SMS DataQ system or in carrier safety fitness determinations [4, 5].
    *   **Standardized ELD Flagging:** Establish a standardized electronic flag or annotation field within ELD data schemas that carriers can use to tag records generated under emergency relief, enabling automated downstream filtering. FMCSA's Motor Carrier Management Information System (MCMIS) should suppress or separately categorize flagged records for the duration of the declared exemption [4, 5].
    *   **Telematics Monitoring Clarity:** Clarify that HOS regulatory relief does not suspend ELD recording obligations or voluntary telematics safety monitoring commitments. FMCSA should issue a best-practices advisory encouraging carriers to maintain active collision, speed, and fatigue monitoring throughout the emergency period, and consider a post-emergency data review mechanism within CSA to identify carriers with elevated safety event rates during exemption windows [4, 5].

3.  **Autonomous Vehicle Testing Provisions:**
    *   **Pilot Programs for Emergency Response:** Given the significant investments in autonomous vehicle technology by J.B. Hunt and other industry leaders (as evidenced by our own autonomous vehicle technology development program [6]), FMCSA should explore regulatory frameworks that facilitate AV testing and pilot programs specifically for emergency relief operations. Autonomous vehicles, once mature, could provide fatigue-free relief capacity, allowing human drivers to rest and recover. FMCSA should consider special permits or exemptions for AV testing in controlled emergency scenarios to assess their efficacy in high-stress, degraded environments.

4.  **Digital Notification Protocols:**
    *   **Real-time API Access:** FMCSA should require – or at minimum establish as a best practice – that all emergency declarations and any subsequent extensions be published simultaneously in a structured, machine-readable data format (e.g., a public API or standardized XML/JSON feed) accessible to carriers and their technology systems. This would allow platforms like J.B. Hunt 360 to automatically update compliance parameters without manual intervention, reducing compliance risk and administrative burden [6, 7].
    *   **Public Dashboard:** Establish a standardized, publicly accessible real-time dashboard that clearly displays the effective dates, geographic scope, and applicable regulatory relief provisions for all active state and federal emergency declarations [1].

5.  **Driver Fatigue Management:**
    *   **Direct Relief Operations Only:** Clarify that the HOS exemption applies to direct emergency relief operations only and does not permit carriers to use the exemption window for non-relief commercial freight [1, 6].
    *   **Internal Rotation & Rest:** Emphasize that carriers are expected to implement internal driver rotation and rest protocols consistent with fatigue science, even when formal HOS rules are suspended. FMCSA should conduct post-emergency compliance reviews to identify carriers that may have misused the exemption window [1, 6].

[CONCLUSION]
J.B. Hunt supports FMCSA's intent to provide more realistic automatic emergency relief duration. However, to truly optimize the industry's ability to respond to future crises while maintaining high safety standards, a simple extension of the exemption period is insufficient. We urge FMCSA to adopt comprehensive regulatory frameworks that explicitly address the unique challenges of intermodal operations, leverage the power of safety technology, and pave the way for future autonomous vehicle contributions in emergency response. J.B. Hunt remains committed to collaborating with FMCSA to develop practical, data-driven solutions that ensure the safety and efficiency of our nation's transportation network during times of emergency.

[Signature removed for privacy]
J.B. Hunt Transport Services Inc.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Truck Safety Coalition (TSC) submits this comment in response to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) regarding the extension of the automatic emergency relief period for Hours of Service (HOS) regulations from 14 days to 30 days, Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124.

TSC is a national organization representing crash victims and their families, dedicated to reducing truck crashes and improving truck safety through evidence-based policy advocacy, public education, and support for crash victim families. Our members and supporters have firsthand experience with the devastating impact of commercial vehicle crashes, many of which are linked to driver fatigue, excessive speeds, and systemic failures in safety oversight. We recognize the importance of emergency response and the need for flexibility during crises, but this flexibility must never come at the expense of public safety.

While we understand the administrative arguments for extending the automatic relief window, the Truck Safety Coalition cannot support the proposed 30-day extension without the simultaneous implementation of robust safety oversight, mandatory data collection, and enhanced public transparency requirements. As currently proposed, this rulemaking restores a longer waiver period without addressing the fundamental safety risks and accountability gaps that make extended HOS suspensions dangerous for all road users. Our conditional support is contingent upon the adoption of the comprehensive safeguards detailed below, which are essential to ensure that emergency relief does not inadvertently increase crash frequency or severity.

[BACKGROUND]
The current regulatory framework, established by a final rule published in October 2023, limits the automatic emergency relief period following a regional declaration to 14 days. This NPRM proposes to reverse that specific modification, extending the automatic exemption period back to 30 days, as it existed prior to the October 2023 rule. The proposed change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA, and implicates regulations typically found under 49 CFR 390.23.

The rationale provided for this extension includes the argument that the current 14-day limit may be insufficient for addressing the logistical and safety needs inherent in regional emergencies, which often require longer periods of "direct assistance." However, the October 2023 rule’s 14-day limit was not arbitrary; it reflected a deliberate, evidence-based safety policy judgment that longer automatic HOS waivers increase crash risk by prolonging exposure to fatigued driving. Any reversal of that judgment must be supported by compelling crash outcome data from the intervening period, which FMCSA has not provided in this proposal.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The Truck Safety Coalition’s conditional support for the 30-day extension is predicated on FMCSA addressing several critical safety impacts:

**Impact 1: Increased Crash Risk without Data and Oversight**
When HOS rules are suspended for extended periods, the risk of driver fatigue increases, which directly correlates with a higher likelihood of crashes. The Truck Safety Coalition exists because fatigued commercial truck drivers kill and injure thousands of Americans every year, and HOS regulations are among the most evidence-supported tools for reducing that toll. When FMCSA proposes to automatically suspend those rules for 30 days — rather than 14 — across entire regions, the agency is making a consequential safety tradeoff.

The proposal's rulemaking record does not include any analysis of crash rates during the 14-day versus 30-day exemption periods since the 2023 rule took effect, nor does it cite any study demonstrating that extended HOS waivers during emergencies do not increase crash risk. FMCSA is asking the public to accept a rollback of a safety rule on the basis of administrative convenience and industry preference, not safety evidence. Furthermore, the agency has not demonstrated how the current 14-day limit poses an operational burden that cannot be addressed through an expedited extension request process rather than an automatic 30-day waiver.

**Impact 2: Safety Oversight Gaps and Accountability Issues**
As an organization representing crash victims and their families, the Truck Safety Coalition regularly encounters situations where FMCSA's emergency exemption data is incomplete, inaccessible, or unavailable through FOIA requests. When automatic relief is triggered for 30 days — suspending hours-of-service rules that exist specifically to prevent fatigued driving — our ability to hold carriers and the agency accountable for crashes that occur during those windows is severely compromised.

Families of victims killed in truck crashes during emergency exemption periods have no reliable mechanism to determine whether the driver involved was operating under waived HOS rules, for how many consecutive hours, or whether the carrier had a prior safety history that should have disqualified them from relief. Extending the window from 14 to 30 days doubles the exposure period without any corresponding increase in oversight. This lack of transparency and data directly impacts FMCSA accountability and data transparency.

**Impact 3: Misuse of Exemptions and Lack of Clear Criteria**
Our operational experience tracking FMCSA emergency declarations reveals that automatic relief provisions are frequently extended well beyond their original scope, as illustrated by the Maryland Port of Baltimore emergency, which was continued from April through at least June 2024 [3]. Each continuation represents an additional period during which fatigued driving risks are elevated without individualized safety review. If the automatic trigger is reset to 30 days, the baseline period before any agency review doubles, increasing the window during which crash victim families have no recourse to challenge the appropriateness of the exemption. This is not a theoretical concern — it is a pattern we have documented through FOIA requests and public declaration tracking.

The October 2023 rule’s 14-day limit was specifically designed to curtail this misuse pattern, which was recognized by commenters during the previous rulemaking process [4]. Without clear, evidence-based criteria for what constitutes a qualifying emergency, the 30-day automatic exemption risks being triggered for situations that do not genuinely impair a carrier's ability to comply with HOS rules, thereby increasing operational burden on safety stakeholders and FMCSA accountability.

**Impact 4: Crash Data Collection Gaps and Evidentiary Issues for Victims**
The Truck Safety Coalition works directly with families of crash victims who rely on FMCSA crash data, inspection records, and enforcement histories to understand the circumstances of fatal crashes. When emergency relief periods suspend HOS requirements, carriers are no longer required to maintain the same level of electronic logging device (ELD) records, and FMCSA's enforcement data becomes incomplete. Extending the relief window from 14 to 30 days doubles the period during which this data gap exists.

For families navigating FOIA requests for crash-related records — already a slow and often incomplete process — a longer relief window means a longer period of diminished data quality. Our organization has documented repeated instances where FMCSA's DataQs system and crash data repositories contain incomplete records from emergency periods, making it nearly impossible to reconstruct the regulatory environment at the time of a fatal crash. This creates a dangerous accountability gap and impacts FMCSA crash data FOIA limitations.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To conditionally support the proposed 30-day extension, the Truck Safety Coalition urges FMCSA to adopt the following concurrent safeguards:

1.  **Recommendation on Data-Driven Decision Making:** Before finalizing any extension of the automatic relief window beyond 14 days, FMCSA should:
    *   Publish a retrospective analysis of crash data from emergency exemption periods under both the pre-2023 (30-day) and post-2023 (14-day) frameworks. This analysis should be disaggregated by exemption day (i.e., crashes on days 1-7, 8-14, 15-21, 22-30) to identify whether crash risk increases as exemption duration extends. (Addresses Impact 1)
    *   Consult the ongoing HOS crash risk research being conducted by Pulsar Informatics under FMCSA contract and delay finalization until preliminary findings are available. (Addresses Impact 1)
    *   If the 30-day window is ultimately adopted, include a mandatory sunset review at 3 years with a public report on crash outcomes during exemption periods. (Addresses Impact 1)

2.  **Recommendation on Accountability & Transparency:** FMCSA should condition the restoration of the 30-day automatic relief window on the concurrent adoption of mandatory real-time reporting requirements for carriers operating under emergency exemptions:
    *   Carriers invoking emergency relief must register with FMCSA within 24 hours of beginning operations under the exemption, identifying the specific declaration and duration. (Addresses Impact 2)
    *   Electronic logging device (ELD) data from exemption-period operations must be preserved and made available to FMCSA upon request for a minimum of 12 months post-exemption. (Addresses Impact 2)
    *   Any crash occurring during an active exemption period must be flagged in FMCSA's public Safety Measurement System (SMS) with notation of the exemption status. (Addresses Impact 2)
    *   A post-emergency public report must be published summarizing the number of carriers that operated under relief, the states and routes involved (e.g., specific states like Florida, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia during Hurricanes Helene and Milton [2, 5]), and any crashes or violations recorded during the exemption period. (Addresses Impact 2)

3.  **Recommendation on Clear Criteria for Exemptions:** FMCSA should amend the proposal to require that automatic 30-day relief be accompanied by a published, publicly accessible written finding by the Regional Administrator. This finding must specify:
    *   The clear operational nexus between the declared emergency and HOS compliance barriers.
    *   The precise geographic boundaries of relief.
    *   A safety monitoring plan for the duration of the exemption.
    Without these guardrails, the 30-day automatic trigger functions as a blanket deregulatory tool rather than a targeted emergency response, reversing the safety progress achieved by the October 2023 rule. (Addresses Impact 3)

4.  **Recommendation on Crash Data Collection:** FMCSA should condition the extended 30-day automatic relief period on a mandatory parallel data collection requirement:
    *   Carriers operating under emergency relief must still submit daily electronic trip logs or equivalent records to a centralized FMCSA repository, even if those logs are not used for enforcement purposes during the relief window. (Addresses Impact 4)
    *   FMCSA should be required to publish a public-facing dashboard within 72 hours of any emergency declaration that identifies which carriers are operating under relief, in which states, and for what duration. This dashboard should remain publicly accessible for no less than five years post-declaration to support litigation, FOIA requests, and retrospective safety analysis. (Addresses Impact 4)

[CONCLUSION]
The Truck Safety Coalition reiterates its conditional support for the proposed 30-day extension of the automatic emergency relief period. However, this support is inseparable from the urgent need for FMCSA to implement robust data collection, transparency, and accountability measures. As currently drafted, the proposal risks increasing crash frequency and severity by prolonging exposure to driver fatigue without adequate safety oversight.

FMCSA's primary mission is to save lives and prevent injuries on our nation's roadways. Extending HOS waivers without the recommended safeguards would be a dereliction of that duty. We urge the agency to prioritize safety outcomes for all road users by adopting these essential conditions before finalizing this rule.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The National Private Truck Council (NPTC) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) proposed rule, Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, which seeks to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days following regional emergency declarations. NPTC is the only national trade association exclusively representing the interests of the private truck fleet industry. Our members include some of the nation's most respected companies, operating dedicated fleets that transport goods vital to the U.S. economy. NPTC is committed to promoting safety, efficiency, and compliance within the trucking industry, particularly as it pertains to the unique operational and safety profiles of private fleets.

We conditionally support the operational flexibility that a 30-day automatic relief period could provide during prolonged or widespread emergencies, such as Hurricane Helene, which triggered declarations across seven states simultaneously [4]. However, NPTC is deeply concerned that a blanket extension, without corresponding safeguards and differentiated approaches for private fleets, risks undermining crucial driver qualification and training standards, creating disproportionate compliance burdens, and failing to recognize the superior safety performance and unique operational model of private fleets.

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA's proposed rule, Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, aims to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the automatic exemption period from 14 days to 30 days for regional emergency declarations issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA. This proposal specifically seeks to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, where the automatic limit was 30 days. The proposed rule suggests that the current 14-day limit may be insufficient for addressing the logistical and safety needs inherent in regional emergencies, which often require longer periods of "direct assistance." These regulations typically fall under **49 CFR 390.23** regarding relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Private Fleet Differentiation and Superior Safety Performance:**
NPTC member fleets operate under a fundamentally different economic and operational model than for-hire carriers. Private fleets maintain dedicated routes with known road conditions, predictable schedules, and driver familiarity with specific corridors — factors that materially reduce fatigue-related risk compared to irregular-route for-hire operations. Our members consistently outperform the broader trucking industry on safety metrics, in part because of rigorous internal compliance management systems and substantial investments in fatigue management training, including FMCSA-recognized curricula and electronic logging device (ELD) compliance programs [9]. If FMCSA relies on aggregated incident data from the 14-to-30-day emergency window without distinguishing private fleet performance, the agency may be attributing safety degradation that occurred in for-hire operations to a carrier segment that did not experience it. This would justify a blanket 30-day exemption that erodes the fatigue management standards our training programs are built around, without a commensurate safety benefit for private fleet drivers [2].

**2. Integrity of Driver Qualification and Training Standards:**
NPTC member fleets maintain driver qualification files (DQFs), medical certifications, road test records, and Entry-Level Driver Training (ELDT) compliance as core operational standards—not merely regulatory checkboxes. When a Governor's emergency declaration automatically suspends these requirements for up to 30 days, private fleets face pressure to onboard emergency-response drivers who may lack verified CDL credentials, current medical certificates, or completed ELDT curricula. Unlike for-hire carriers who may draw from a broad labor pool, private fleets operate dedicated routes with specialized cargo (often high-value or sensitive goods) and have invested heavily in driver qualification infrastructure. A 30-day suspension window creates a compliance gap that is difficult to close post-emergency: qualification files must be reconstructed, training records reconciled, and medical status re-verified—all at significant administrative cost. NPTC members report that driver qualification file maintenance and ELDT compliance represent meaningful per-driver investments (training programs range from approximately $75 to $750 per module per driver, per available commercial training vendors), and that onboarding drivers without these records creates downstream liability exposure that persists well beyond the emergency period itself [4]. Furthermore, driver qualification standards such as 49 CFR Part 391 medical examiner certificate requirements and annual driving record reviews are not operationally burdensome during emergencies and should never lapse [1].

**3. Disproportionate Recordkeeping and Audit Burdens:**
Private fleet safety managers maintain driver qualification files (DQFs) that must be continuously reconciled against the regulatory framework in effect on any given operating day. Under a 30-day automatic exemption, NPTC members must track which drivers operated under exemption conditions, for how long, and whether any qualification or hours-of-service documentation requirements were modified or suspended during that window. Because private fleets operate dedicated, recurring routes — often with the same drivers day after day — a 30-day ambiguity period creates compounding documentation complexity: each driver's file must reflect whether a given trip fell within or outside the exemption window, whether any training or qualification deferrals were permissible, and when normal recordkeeping obligations fully resumed. A single misclassified operating day can trigger a finding during a DOT compliance audit, which can result in immediate revocation of operating authority if corrective actions are not satisfactorily implemented. For private fleets — which are not for-hire carriers and cannot simply redirect freight — loss of operating authority is operationally catastrophic [6].

**4. Need for Disaggregated Safety Data:**
FMCSA's reliance on aggregated incident data from the 14-to-30-day emergency window without distinguishing private fleet performance obscures the demonstrably superior safety performance of private fleets and could result in overbroad relief that undermines fatigue management standards where they are most needed [2]. While we acknowledge the operational necessity of longer relief periods during multi-state emergencies, such as Hurricane Helene and Milton, which required relief extensions through late December 2024 [4], the agency must ensure that such extensions are justified by data relevant to all affected carrier types.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these concerns and ensure that any extension of automatic emergency relief recognizes the unique safety profile of private fleets, NPTC offers the following recommendations:

**1. Differentiated Relief Framework:**
    *   Extend the 30-day automatic relief period for Hours-of-Service (HOS) and equipment-related regulations as proposed, but limit the automatic suspension of driver qualification standards (specifically those under **49 CFR Part 391**) to 14 days, with a carrier-initiated opt-in mechanism for extensions requiring a documented showing of operational necessity [1], [4].
    *   Exempt private fleets that can demonstrate a current satisfactory safety rating and a fleet safety performance record above the industry average (e.g., below-average out-of-service rates over a rolling 24-month period) from the automatic suspension of ELDT and DQF requirements entirely, recognizing their demonstrated safety culture [9].

**2. Clear Guidance and Enforcement Policy:**
    *   Amend the proposed rule to explicitly include an enumerated list of the specific regulatory provisions covered by the automatic emergency relief—and those expressly excluded. In particular, the final rule should state clearly that **49 CFR Part 391** driver qualification standards (medical certification, CDL verification, annual review) are NOT suspended under the automatic emergency relief provision [1].
    *   Publish a plain-language compliance guidance document alongside the final rule that private fleet compliance managers can use as a reference during active emergency declarations, reducing the administrative burden of real-time regulatory interpretation during crisis conditions [1].
    *   Commit to publishing post-emergency enforcement guidance within 60 days of any major relief period's conclusion, giving private fleets a clear remediation timeline [1].

**3. Voluntary Compliance Check-in Mechanism:**
    *   Create a voluntary Emergency Operations Documentation Portal—or at minimum a standardized self-certification form—that carriers can use during a 30-day relief period to log: (1) which drivers operated under relief provisions, (2) the nature of emergency-related operations performed, and (3) any qualification or training activities deferred and their planned completion dates. This documentation should be treated as a mitigating factor in any post-emergency compliance review [1].

**4. Disaggregated Safety Data Analysis:**
    *   Publish a breakdown of safety incidents—crashes, HOS violations, and fatigue-related events—occurring specifically in the 14-to-30-day window of prior emergency declarations, stratified by carrier type (private vs. for-hire). If the data show that private fleet incident rates during this window remain at or below baseline, FMCSA should consider a tiered exemption framework that grants the full 30-day automatic relief only to carrier types whose historical emergency-period data support it, while maintaining the 14-day limit with an expedited extension process for carriers with demonstrated safety performance records [2], [4].

**5. Recordkeeping Clarity:**
    *   Issue standardized recordkeeping guidance specifying exactly which DQF entries must be made—and in what format—when a driver operates under an active regional emergency exemption, reducing the current interpretive burden on private fleet compliance officers [6].

[CONCLUSION]
NPTC supports the operational flexibility that a 30-day automatic emergency relief period could provide. However, we urge FMCSA to recognize the unique safety profile of private fleets and implement risk-based, differentiated regulatory approaches that preserve essential driver qualification and training standards while offering necessary operational flexibility during emergencies. By adopting these recommendations, FMCSA can extend relief effectively without inadvertently compromising safety or imposing undue compliance burdens on the private fleet industry.

### References

[9] https://learnhazmat.com/dot/fmcsa-clarifies-applicability-of-emergency-exemptions/
