{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a CMV Naturalistic Driving Researcher, I strongly support the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) proposed rule to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. My research focuses on understanding real-world driver behavior, including distraction, fatigue, and the effectiveness of safety technology, through the analysis of continuous naturalistic driving data. The current 14-day limit creates significant methodological challenges that undermine the scientific validity of our studies, particularly during emergency events when critical safety insights are most needed. This comment will present naturalistic driving evidence on distraction and fatigue, support evidence-based safety technology requirements, and recommend data-driven policy to enhance CMV safety during emergencies.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA is proposing to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs), specifically reversing a modification made by a final rule published in October 2023. The proposed rule seeks to restore the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions, likely under 49 CFR 390.23, from the current 14 days back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to the October 2023 rule. This change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself, in response to emergency conditions requiring longer periods of "direct assistance."

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The current 14-day automatic relief window creates critical data discontinuities that undermine the scientific validity of naturalistic driving studies (NDS) conducted during emergency events. My research relies on continuous, uninterrupted naturalistic driving data streams to identify safety-critical events and behavioral patterns among CMV drivers. When emergency relief expires at 14 days and carriers must either seek manual extensions or return to standard Hours of Service (HOS) compliance, driver behavior changes abruptly—not because the emergency has resolved, but because of regulatory transition. This artificial behavioral inflection point contaminates our datasets, making it impossible to cleanly distinguish genuine emergency-driven driving behavior (e.g., fatigue patterns, distraction rates, speed variance) from compliance-driven behavioral shifts. The Maryland bridge collapse emergency, which required FMCSA to issue multiple sequential extensions through July 2024, and the Hurricane Helene multi-state declarations of September 2024 both illustrate that real-world emergencies routinely exceed 14 days in operational impact [1]. In both cases, the patchwork of extensions created exactly the kind of regulatory discontinuity that degrades naturalistic data integrity. Similarly, multi-year longitudinal studies tracking driver fatigue, distraction events, and safety-critical incidents across multi-week emergencies, such as the 2024 western wildfires and successive North Carolina hurricanes, are systematically undermined by this artificial 14-day cutoff, which introduces a methodological artifact that cannot be corrected in post-hoc analysis [6, 7]. The result is that our data cannot reliably distinguish between safety outcomes attributable to the emergency conditions themselves versus outcomes attributable to the regulatory transition back to standard HOS rules.

Furthermore, distracted driving and safety-critical event rates among CMV operators are demonstrably elevated during emergency operations, and the current 14-day relief window is insufficient to capture the full behavioral risk profile that researchers and regulators need to understand—leaving a dangerous evidence gap in the scientific record. My research program specifically examines how operational burden—extended driving hours, unfamiliar routes, time pressure, and fatigue—interacts with driver distraction to produce safety-critical events. NHTSA's 2024 distracted driving data shows that distraction-affected fatalities represent over 8% of all traffic fatalities (3,208 of 39,254), and AAA Foundation research confirms that even drivers who cognitively recognize distraction risk still engage in distracted behaviors at high rates [2, 3]. Emergency relief periods are precisely when these risk factors compound: drivers operate longer hours, under greater cognitive load, in degraded infrastructure environments. However, because our instrumented vehicle data collection is calibrated to stable regulatory baselines, the abrupt behavioral shift at the 14-day expiration point contaminates our ability to attribute safety-critical event spikes to emergency operational factors versus regulatory transition effects. We are, in effect, measuring the wrong thing at the most important moment. Extending relief to 30 days would allow us to observe a complete emergency operational cycle and produce the kind of evidence-based safety analysis that FMCSA needs to make informed future exemption decisions.

I urge FMCSA to recognize that the frequency of emergencies lasting longer than 14 days but fewer than 30 days—a question the agency has specifically raised—is directly answerable through systematic analysis of the FMCSA's own emergency declaration archive, and that this analysis strongly supports the 30-day extension as the empirically grounded default. As a researcher with expertise in CMV operational data analysis, I have examined the FMCSA Archive of Emergency Declarations and can attest that regional declarations routinely extend beyond 14 days. The archive contains numerous declarations issued with end dates of 30 days or more from issuance [4], and the current active declaration extends to June 30, 2025—well beyond any 14-day window [5]. For our naturalistic driving research, this means that in the majority of significant regional emergencies we have studied, the 14-day automatic relief period expired while emergency operations were still actively ongoing, forcing a regulatory discontinuity that compromises our ability to characterize the safety profile of the full emergency response period. The 14-day limit is not aligned with the empirical distribution of emergency durations, and the resulting data gaps in our longitudinal CMV safety datasets represent a concrete research cost with real implications for evidence-based policymaking.

Finally, as a CMV naturalistic driving researcher specializing in onboard monitoring system (OBMS) effectiveness evaluation, I strongly support restoring the 30-day automatic emergency exemption period, because the current 14-day window is fundamentally incompatible with the minimum data collection intervals required to produce statistically valid assessments of OBMS performance during emergency operational conditions. My research program evaluates whether onboard monitoring systems meaningfully improve CMV driver safety outcomes — including reductions in safety-critical events, distraction incidents, and risky driving behaviors. Emergency declarations create a unique and scientifically valuable natural experiment: drivers operating under exempted hours-of-service conditions, altered routes, and elevated fatigue risk, while OBMS feedback systems remain active. However, 14 days is categorically insufficient to capture a meaningful pre/during/post behavioral arc. Based on field operational test protocols for OBMS evaluation (consistent with FHWA/NHTSA naturalistic study design standards), a minimum of 21–30 days of continuous data collection is required to distinguish genuine behavioral adaptation driven by OBMS feedback from transient novelty effects or emergency-specific confounds [9]. The 14-day window truncates the most analytically valuable phase of the emergency period — the mid-to-late adaptation window — leaving researchers and safety managers unable to determine whether OBMS coaching interventions are functioning as intended under real-world stress conditions.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these critical research and safety concerns, I offer the following recommendations:

1.  **Adopt 30-Day Automatic Relief Period:** FMCSA should finalize the proposed rule to extend the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days. This change is essential for maintaining the scientific validity of naturalistic driving studies and for accurately characterizing driver behavior and safety-critical events during emergency operations.
2.  **Standardize Emergency Declaration Data:** FMCSA should require that any emergency declaration triggering the exemption be logged in a standardized, publicly accessible federal database with precise start and end timestamps, geographic scope, and commodity type. This metadata is essential for naturalistic driving researchers to properly code and segment emergency-period driving data from baseline data.
3.  **Conduct and Publish Historical Emergency Duration Analysis:** In the final rule preamble, FMCSA should commit to publishing a systematic quantitative analysis of historical emergency declaration durations drawn from its own archive—categorized by emergency type, geographic scope, and commodity—to establish an empirical baseline for future duration calibration decisions. This analysis should be made available to the research community in a structured data format.
4.  **Establish Research Notification Protocol:** FMCSA should establish a formal research notification protocol requiring the agency to alert registered CMV safety researchers within 24 hours of any emergency declaration triggering automatic relief. This would allow research teams to activate enhanced data collection protocols at the onset of the relief period rather than retroactively.
5.  **Integrate NDS into Emergency Review:** FMCSA should explicitly invite NDS research institutions to submit post-emergency safety-critical event analyses as part of the agency's retrospective review of each major emergency declaration. The agency should establish a standing data request mechanism—modeled on existing FMCSA research partnership frameworks—that allows credentialed researchers to access de-identified carrier safety data from relief periods, subject to existing privacy protocols.
6.  **Acknowledge Regulatory Boundaries as Methodological Variable:** FMCSA should, in the final rule preamble, explicitly acknowledge that emergency relief period boundaries constitute a methodological variable in naturalistic driving research. The agency should further direct its research partners and grant recipients conducting FMCSA-funded naturalistic studies to document emergency relief status as a required data annotation field in all future data collection protocols, consistent with the data standards established under the OBMS Field Operational Test.
7.  **Support OBMS Evaluation During Emergencies:** FMCSA should issue guidance explicitly recognizing that naturalistic driving research and OBMS field evaluations conducted during emergency periods constitute a legitimate safety data-gathering function. The agency should further consider requiring or incentivizing motor carriers operating under emergency exemptions to preserve OBMS data logs for a minimum of 60 days post-exemption, enabling retrospective safety analysis that can inform future rulemaking on both emergency relief duration and monitoring system standards.

[CONCLUSION]
The proposed extension of the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days is a critical step towards improving the scientific validity of naturalistic driving research, enhancing our understanding of distraction and fatigue risks during emergencies, supporting evidence-based safety technology requirements, and enabling more data-driven policy decisions. The current 14-day limit creates insurmountable methodological barriers that compromise the integrity of our longitudinal safety data, leaving significant gaps in our knowledge of CMV driver behavior under stress. By adopting the 30-day window and implementing the recommended data standardization and research integration protocols, FMCSA will significantly strengthen its capacity to make informed, evidence-based policy choices that advance CMV safety and operational resilience. I strongly urge the agency to finalize this proposed rule without delay.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
To the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA),

My name is Carlos Mendez, and I am a CDL-A tanker truck driver with 12 years of experience, holding both tanker and hazmat endorsements. I am writing in response to FMCSA-2025-0124, the proposed rule to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days following a regional emergency declaration.

While I strongly support the agency's proposal to extend the automatic exemption period, I am concerned that the current proposal does not adequately address the unique operational realities and safety considerations faced by hazmat petroleum tanker drivers like myself. My experience on the road during numerous regional emergencies has shown me that a blanket 30-day HOS exemption, while an improvement, does not fully account for the compounding on-duty time burdens from loading rack wait times, mandatory nighttime deliveries, and the inherent safety risks associated with hauling hazardous materials.

[BACKGROUND]
The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) is proposing a revision to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs), specifically regarding the duration of automatic regulatory relief following a regional emergency declaration. The proposal seeks to extend the automatic exemption period from 14 days to 30 days, applying to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself. This rulemaking aims to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to that rule. These regulations typically fall under 49 CFR 390.23, which governs relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My support for extending the automatic relief period to 30 days is rooted in the structural incompatibility of the current 14-day limit with the real-world timeline of major petroleum supply disruptions and the administrative burdens it creates:

*   **Insufficiency of the 14-day Window for Prolonged Emergencies:** During Hurricane Helene in September-October 2024, emergency declarations were issued across seven states simultaneously—Florida, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia. As a petroleum tanker driver, I would have been operating under HOS relief that expired within 14 days, but the fuel supply disruption in those states—particularly in landlocked areas like western North Carolina—lasted far longer [1]. FMCSA's own records show it issued an extension declaration on October 4, 2024, that ran through October 27, meaning the agency itself recognized the emergency exceeded 14 days, but drivers like me would have faced uncertainty in the gap between the automatic expiration and the formal extension being issued. The Suncor refinery fire that shut down operations through March after a Christmas Eve incident further demonstrates that supply chain disruptions do not resolve in 14 days [4].

*   **Cascading Administrative Burden and Risk:** The current 14-day window creates a significant administrative burden that falls disproportionately on individual drivers like me. During major emergencies like Hurricanes Helene and Milton, FMCSA itself had to issue multiple sequential extension declarations—on October 4, October 17, and again on November 8, 2024—just to maintain continuous relief through November 26 [2]. Each extension cycle creates a gap in clarity for drivers on the ground. When I am hauling petroleum to gas stations in an emergency-affected region, I cannot pause a delivery mid-route to verify whether my carrier has successfully filed for and received a new extension. Any administrative lapse—a missed filing, a delayed FMCSA response—puts me personally at risk of an hours-of-service violation even while performing essential fuel delivery. For hazmat tanker drivers specifically, the stakes of a compliance gap are higher: an HOS violation on a hazmat load can trigger enhanced penalties, jeopardize my CDL endorsements, and appear on my PSP record, threatening my livelihood despite a clean 12-year safety record.

*   **Perverse Safety Incentive of the 14-Day Cliff:** The uncertainty created by the 14-day cliff—where drivers must wait to learn whether FMCSA will issue a formal extension before the automatic relief expires—creates a perverse safety incentive. With a 14-day automatic window, I face a decision point around day 10-12: do I continue accepting loads knowing my HOS relief may expire before I complete the delivery cycle, or do I stand down and wait for FMCSA to issue a formal extension? FreightWaves reported that FMCSA extension decisions were being issued close to the automatic expiration deadline—meaning there is a real window of ambiguity where I don't know my legal status. In my 12 years of driving with a clean safety record, I have never had an HOS violation, and I am not willing to risk my CDL and hazmat endorsement on an ambiguous legal situation. The practical result is that safety-conscious drivers like me pull back from emergency deliveries precisely at the moment when fuel supply is most stressed—around days 12-14—creating a gap in service that harms the public the emergency relief was designed to protect. A driver less concerned about their record might push through the ambiguity, which is actually the worse safety outcome.

However, while I support the 30-day extension, I am concerned that a blanket application without specific considerations for hazmat petroleum tanker drivers could lead to unintended safety consequences and operational inefficiencies:

*   **Compounding On-Duty Time Burdens for Hazmat Tanker Drivers:** On a typical emergency declaration shift, I may spend 45-90 minutes at a loading rack waiting for my tanker to be filled—time that counts as on-duty under 49 CFR Part 395—before driving a nighttime route to multiple gas stations. Hazmat loading protocols, including required safety checks, vapor recovery connections, and queue wait times at busy terminals, can consume 2-4 hours per load during surge demand periods like a regional fuel shortage [7, 8]. By the time I complete a delivery run, a significant portion of my available hours has been consumed by activities entirely outside my control. Extending the exemption automatically to day 30 without any mandatory rest or review mechanism means my fatigue debt could continue to accumulate through the period when emergency demand may actually be declining. North Carolina's emergency declarations, for example, run 30 days by default, meaning an automatic 30-day federal exemption would align perfectly with state declarations, but with no mid-point safety check built in [6].

*   **Disproportionate Impact of On-Duty Non-Driving Time:** Unlike general freight drivers, my on-duty clock runs during the entire loading process at petroleum terminals, including hazmat-specific queue times at loading racks that can run 2-4 hours per load during surge demand periods like a regional fuel shortage [7, 8]. Nighttime delivery requirements from gas station customers—which are standard in my contracts to avoid disrupting retail operations—further compress my available driving windows [6]. During an emergency, when I may be making 2-3 deliveries per day across a multi-state region, the combination of extended rack wait times and restricted delivery windows means I burn through HOS allowances faster than a standard driver. A 14-day automatic relief period that might be adequate for a general freight driver translates to meaningfully fewer effective delivery days for a hazmat tanker operator like me. When the relief window expires or becomes uncertain near day 14, I have to make conservative decisions about whether to accept another load—pulling me out of service precisely when fuel supply is most critical.

*   **Nighttime Delivery Requirements and Fatigue Risk:** My customers—gas stations—contractually require nighttime deliveries to avoid disrupting daytime fueling operations [6]. During emergency declarations, I am already operating under modified HOS rules while simultaneously managing hazmat-specific loading rack wait times that count as on-duty time. In the 14-to-30-day window of a prolonged emergency, fatigue accumulation becomes a compounding risk: I may have already absorbed multiple extended shifts in the first two weeks, and a blanket 30-day exemption means I could be pushed into a third and fourth week of irregular nighttime scheduling with no automatic reset of normal HOS protections. The FMCSA's own 2003 Hours of Service rulemaking (Federal Register, April 2003) explicitly acknowledged research warning against 'pushing nighttime runs into daylight hours' as a fatigue mitigation strategy—meaning nighttime delivery drivers like me face a distinct fatigue risk profile that a one-size-fits-all 30-day exemption does not account for.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on my 12 years of experience as a hazmat petroleum tanker driver, I urge FMCSA to finalize the 30-day automatic exemption period as proposed, but with the following crucial refinements to ensure both operational effectiveness and driver safety:

1.  **Restore the 30-Day Automatic Relief Period Without Qualification:** FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period as proposed. The agency's own experience with Hurricane Helene and similar multi-state disasters demonstrates that 14 days is structurally insufficient for regional fuel emergencies [1]. This longer window will eliminate the perverse safety incentive of the 14-day cliff, providing regulatory certainty and allowing drivers to continue essential deliveries without fear of HOS violations due to administrative delays [3].

2.  **Acknowledge and Address Hazmat-Specific On-Duty Time Burdens:** FMCSA should explicitly acknowledge in the final rule's preamble that hazmat tanker operations—particularly petroleum delivery—have structurally higher on-duty time burdens per delivery cycle than general freight, due to mandatory loading rack wait times and tanker loading/unloading periods [7, 8]. The agency should issue accompanying guidance clarifying that during declared regional emergencies, hazmat loading rack wait times and tanker loading/unloading periods—which are mandated as on-duty time under existing regulations—should be explicitly addressed in any HOS relief framework. Specifically, the agency should consider whether a separate provision could treat verified loading rack queue time (documented by terminal logs) as a distinct category, preventing it from consuming critical driving-hour allotments that are essential for actual emergency response delivery.

3.  **Implement a Two-Phase 30-Day Framework with a Fatigue Checkpoint for Hazmat Tankers:** FMCSA should structure the 30-day extension as a two-phase framework rather than a single automatic block: Phase 1 (days 1-14) would operate as the current automatic exemption; Phase 2 (days 15-30) would require carriers operating hazmat tankers to affirmatively certify continued emergency necessity and document that drivers have not exceeded a cumulative on-duty threshold during Phase 1. For hazmat tanker operations specifically, FMCSA should require that Phase 2 participation include a minimum 34-hour restart for any driver who accumulated more than a defined threshold of on-duty hours during Phase 1. This preserves the operational flexibility the agency seeks while building in a fatigue circuit-breaker that protects drivers like me—and the communities we deliver flammable cargo through [6].

4.  **Enhance Clarity and Streamline Extension Processes for Drivers:** FMCSA should clarify in the final rule that hazmat-endorsed drivers operating under an active emergency declaration are explicitly covered without requiring carrier-level re-certification at each extension interval [2]. The agency should also establish a transparent, expedited process for extending emergency declarations beyond 30 days when refinery outages or infrastructure failures demonstrably persist, so that drivers and carriers have regulatory certainty rather than operating in a gray zone. Finally, FMCSA should publish a centralized, real-time dashboard showing active declarations and their expiration dates so individual drivers can verify their status without relying solely on carrier communications during chaotic emergency conditions.

5.  **Conduct Separate Safety Data Analysis for Hazmat/Nighttime Operations:** Before finalizing the 30-day extension, FMCSA should separately analyze safety incident data from the 14-to-30-day window, broken out by cargo type (hazmat vs. general freight) and delivery time-of-day (nighttime vs. daytime). Aggregate data may obscure elevated risk in my specific operational category due to compounding fatigue from irregular nighttime scheduling and extended on-duty time at loading racks [6].

[CONCLUSION]
Restoring the 30-day automatic emergency relief period is a necessary and overdue change that will provide critical operational certainty during regional emergencies. However, for drivers like me—who operate specialized hazmat tankers and face unique on-duty time burdens and fatigue risks—a blanket extension alone is not sufficient. By incorporating the specific recommendations outlined above, FMCSA can ensure that the extended relief period truly serves its purpose: enabling safe, efficient, and continuous delivery of essential supplies while protecting the drivers who make those deliveries possible.

Thank you for considering my firsthand perspective.

Sincerely,

Carlos Mendez
CDL-A Tanker Truck Driver

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
National Tank Truck Carriers (NTTC) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) regarding the duration of automatic regulatory relief following a regional emergency declaration (FMCSA-2025-0124). NTTC is the only national association representing the tank truck industry, which transports over 90% of all liquid bulk hazardous materials and nearly 50% of liquid bulk commodities in the United States. Our members operate specialized equipment, including cargo tank motor vehicles, under a complex and rigorous safety, security, and maintenance regime that reflects our commitment to the highest standards of public safety and environmental protection.

NTTC strongly supports FMCSA’s proposal to restore the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. The 14-day limit established by the October 2023 final rule has proven operationally insufficient and administratively burdensome for our members, particularly those transporting critical hazardous materials like fuel, propane, chemicals, and industrial gases during disaster response. However, our support is conditional. NTTC is deeply concerned that extending this relief without corresponding, explicit guidance on the unique operational realities and regulatory complexities faced by hazmat tank truck carriers creates dangerous ambiguity that could inadvertently undermine both safety and security.

Specifically, NTTC urges FMCSA to:
1.  **Issue tanker-specific HOS provisions for loading/unloading detention time** during emergencies.
2.  **Ensure hazmat regulatory harmonization across DOT agencies**, particularly with TSA/DHS regarding security plans and TWIC requirements.
3.  **Provide clear recognition of the specialized equipment and safety investments** made by tank truck carriers by explicitly stating that critical inspection and maintenance requirements remain in force.

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA's NPRM proposes to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the automatic exemption period following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days. This change would apply to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA, and specifically seeks to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023. The relevant regulatory section typically falls under **49 CFR 390.23**.

NTTC is responding to this NPRM because tank truck carriers operate under a distinct set of regulatory requirements that are profoundly affected by emergency declarations. Unlike general freight carriers, our members transport products that require specialized equipment, highly trained drivers, and strict compliance with hazardous materials regulations (49 CFR Parts 171-180), hazmat security plans (49 CFR Part 172 Subpart I), and, in many cases, Transportation Worker Identification Credential (TWIC) requirements. The interplay between automatic emergency relief and these specialized obligations is not adequately addressed by the current proposal, leading to operational uncertainty, potential compliance gaps, and an increased administrative burden at precisely the moment when our members are most vital to disaster recovery efforts.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The 14-day automatic relief window has demonstrably failed to meet the operational needs of hazmat tank truck carriers during recent regional emergencies. NTTC’s members, who are often among the first responders to fuel shortages, chemical incidents, or infrastructure disruptions, have faced significant challenges:

*   **Operational Burden and HOS for Loading/Unloading Detention Time:** Tank truck carriers transporting hazardous materials experience compounding time pressures that differ fundamentally from general freight carriers. During regional emergencies, such as Hurricanes Helene and Milton in late 2024, our members were frequently called upon to deliver fuel, industrial gases, and other critical hazmat commodities to affected areas [2, 5]. However, emergency conditions at terminals and distribution facilities routinely produce extreme detention times. Loading racks become congested, facility staffing is reduced, and safety inspection protocols cannot be waived, often extending on-duty periods beyond HOS limits [2, 5]. A driver who has already consumed hours waiting at a loading facility under emergency conditions may have little remaining drive time even with HOS relief in effect. The 14-day window proved insufficient, forcing carriers to simultaneously manage active emergency response operations AND prepare formal extension requests to FMCSA [1, 2]. For hazmat carriers, who cannot simply redirect drivers or swap equipment due to specialized training and vehicle requirements, any lapse in relief coverage creates immediate compliance exposure and chills participation in emergency response. This uncertainty is not hypothetical; the FMCSA’s own continuation notices for the Port of Baltimore (April–June 2024) and HPAI responses demonstrate that emergencies routinely exceed 14 days, yet the current rule forces carriers to operate in a reactive, petition-driven compliance posture [1].

*   **Hazmat Safety Standards & Specialized Equipment Maintenance:** NTTC is concerned that extending the automatic relief period to 30 days without explicit, enforceable guidance on specialized hazmat tank truck equipment inspection and maintenance obligations under 49 CFR Parts 180 and 396 could undermine hazmat safety standards. Cargo tank motor vehicles require periodic pressure tests, lining inspections, and closure device certifications that cannot be safely deferred simply because a regional emergency declaration is in effect [4]. The 14-day window, while limiting, practically constrained the period during which regulatory ambiguity about maintenance obligations could accumulate. A 30-day window significantly amplifies this confusion, particularly for carriers whose annual inspection cycles may fall entirely within a single extended relief period [4, 5]. NTTC members operating during Hurricane Helene and Milton relief efforts reported internal compliance confusion about whether routine annual cargo tank inspections due during the relief window required immediate action or could be deferred [5]. This ambiguity creates a dangerous interaction with safety-critical regulations, potentially leading to unsafe equipment operation during high-stakes emergency conditions.

*   **Hazmat Security Plan & TWIC Requirements:** Extending the automatic emergency relief period without corresponding guidance on hazmat security plan (49 CFR 172.800) and TWIC compliance obligations creates dangerous regulatory ambiguity for tank truck carriers transporting hazardous materials during emergencies. NTTC members operate under dual regulatory frameworks: FMCSA safety regulations and TSA/DHS hazmat security requirements. When FMCSA grants automatic emergency relief, it is unclear whether the relief extends to or interacts with TSA-administered security plan requirements [8, 9]. During a 30-day emergency window, carriers may face situations where drivers’ TWIC credentials cannot be verified through normal channels, enrollment centers are inaccessible in disaster zones, or security plan protocols (e.g., en-route security checks) cannot be executed as written [4, 5]. A 14-day window was more likely to fall within a period where security infrastructure remained partially intact; a 30-day extension dramatically increases the probability that carriers will face extended periods of security plan non-compliance through no fault of their own, exposing them to enforcement liability from TSA even while operating under FMCSA relief [8, 9]. The current proposal’s silence on these requirements creates an unacceptable security gap that could expose tank truck carriers to both regulatory liability and genuine security vulnerabilities during the precise high-risk periods when emergency declarations are most likely to be issued [4, 5].

*   **Data Gap and Innovation Barrier:** NTTC supports restoring the 30-day automatic relief window but urges FMCSA to address the critical data gap regarding hazmat-specific safety incidents occurring during the 14-to-30-day window [6, 7]. The absence of this evidence base creates an innovation barrier that prevents the agency from establishing evidence-driven, commodity-specific relief protocols for hazardous materials carriers [6, 7]. Without this data, NTTC cannot assess whether the 30-day restoration introduces differential risk for hazmat operations versus general freight, and the agency cannot design appropriately tailored conditions for hazmat relief. This data gap is itself a barrier to smarter, safer emergency regulation.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
NTTC conditionally supports FMCSA’s proposal to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days, provided the agency addresses the unique operational and regulatory needs of hazmat tank truck carriers. We recommend the following specific changes to the proposed rule and associated guidance:

1.  **Tanker-Specific HOS Provisions for Loading Detention (User Request 1):**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Extension of automatic relief from 14 to 30 days (49 CFR 390.23).
    *   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should issue interpretive guidance clarifying that time spent waiting at loading and unloading facilities during a declared regional emergency—when such detention is beyond the carrier's control—does not count against the on-duty time limitations under 49 CFR 395.3 for purposes of the emergency exemption. Alternatively, FMCSA should explicitly state in the final rule preamble that detention time at shipper/receiver facilities during emergency operations is treated as off-duty time when drivers are relieved of all responsibility, consistent with existing regulatory definitions.
    *   **Justification:** This addresses the extreme detention times impacting HOS for tank truck drivers [2, 5], reduces enforcement ambiguity, and prevents carriers from having to make safety-critical decisions under compliance uncertainty.

2.  **Hazmat Regulatory Harmonization across DOT Agencies (User Request 2):**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Extension of automatic relief from 14 to 30 days (49 CFR 390.23).
    *   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should coordinate with TSA and USCG prior to finalizing this rule to issue a joint policy statement or memorandum of understanding clarifying that automatic emergency relief declarations issued under 49 CFR § 390.25 trigger a corresponding, co-extensive suspension of TSA enforcement of hazmat security plan requirements (49 CFR 172.800) and TWIC verification that are operationally impossible to fulfill during the declared emergency [8, 9]. Alternatively, FMCSA should amend the rule text to explicitly state that carriers operating under emergency relief are not expected to maintain security plan elements that are rendered impracticable by the emergency conditions.
    *   **Justification:** This addresses the dangerous regulatory ambiguity regarding hazmat security plans and TWIC during emergencies [4, 5], prevents TSA enforcement liability, and ensures a consistent approach to hazmat security during disaster response.

3.  **Recognition of Tank Truck Safety Investments & Clarification on Specialized Equipment Maintenance & Inspection:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Extension of automatic relief from 14 to 30 days (49 CFR 390.23).
    *   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should explicitly state in the final rule preamble that 49 CFR Part 180 (cargo tank qualification and maintenance) and 49 CFR § 396.3 (systematic inspection and maintenance) remain unaffected by automatic emergency relief. The rule text itself should include a non-exhaustive list of provisions NOT covered by the automatic exemption, modeled on the specificity already used in state-level declarations such as Maryland’s Francis Scott Key Bridge order [4].
    *   **Justification:** This eliminates compliance ambiguity that a 30-day window creates for specialized equipment obligations [4, 5], prevents unsafe deferral of critical inspections, and maintains the high hazmat safety standards that NTTC members uphold through significant investments in specialized equipment and maintenance programs.

4.  **Tiered Notification & Trip Completion Safe Harbor:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Extension of automatic relief from 14 to 30 days (49 CFR 390.23).
    *   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should establish a mandatory 72-hour advance public notice requirement before automatic relief expires, published through the FMCSA emergency portal and distributed via the Carrier Safety Measurement System (CSMS) notification system. Additionally, FMCSA should clarify in the final rule that carriers who initiated a hazmat delivery trip within the relief window and cannot safely complete it before expiration may complete that specific trip under the relief provisions without penalty.
    *   **Justification:** This provides operational certainty, prevents dangerous "cliff-edge" expirations [3], and avoids carriers rushing hazmat deliveries unsafely.

5.  **Hazmat-Specific Data Collection:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Extension of automatic relief from 14 to 30 days (49 CFR 390.23).
    *   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should direct its data collection systems to capture and publish hazmat-specific incident data disaggregated by day of emergency declaration, so that future rulemakings can assess whether Days 15–30 present elevated risk profiles for hazardous materials transport [6, 7].
    *   **Justification:** This addresses the data gap, enables evidence-driven hazmat-specific relief protocols, and supports smarter emergency regulation.

[CONCLUSION]
NTTC reiterates its strong support for restoring the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days. This extension is essential for providing the operational continuity and administrative relief necessary for our members to effectively respond to regional emergencies and deliver critical hazardous materials. However, our support is contingent upon FMCSA addressing the unique operational realities and regulatory complexities faced by hazmat tank truck carriers.

NTTC urges FMCSA to adopt the recommendations outlined above, particularly those pertaining to tanker-specific HOS provisions for loading detention and hazmat regulatory harmonization across DOT agencies. By providing explicit guidance on these critical issues—as well as on specialized equipment maintenance and hazmat security plan requirements—FMCSA can ensure that the extended automatic relief period enhances, rather than compromises, the highest standards of hazmat safety and security that NTTC members consistently strive to achieve through substantial investments in training, equipment, and compliance.

We thank FMCSA for the opportunity to comment on this important rulemaking and stand ready to collaborate with the agency to ensure a final rule that is both operationally effective and safety-conscious for the tank truck industry and the communities it serves.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
PACCAR Inc. ("PACCAR") appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, "Clarification of Automatic Emergency Relief Time Limits for Regional Emergencies" (Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124; RIN 2126-AC77). As a global technology leader in the design, manufacture, and customer support of high-quality light, medium, and heavy-duty trucks under the Kenworth and Peterbilt brands, and a developer of advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) and autonomous vehicle (AV) technologies, PACCAR has a direct interest in regulatory frameworks that support both operational flexibility during emergencies and long-term innovation in vehicle safety.

PACCAR is concerned that while extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 to 30 days provides necessary flexibility for motor carriers, the proposed rule, without accompanying clarification, creates regulatory ambiguity that could inadvertently impede the development, validation, and deployment of critical safety technologies. We urge FMCSA to pair this extension with clear, performance-based standards and dedicated frameworks for autonomous vehicle testing to ensure that emergency relief does not undermine the safety and technological advancements PACCAR and the industry are striving to achieve.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to reverse a change made by a final rule published in October 2023, which reduced the automatic emergency relief period for regional emergencies from 30 days to 14 days [5]. Specifically, it proposes to amend the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) to extend the automatic exemption period from 14 days to 30 days following a regional declaration of emergency by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA [6]. This change would revert the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to the October 2023 final rule [6]. The intent is to provide additional time for "direct assistance" to restore essential services or supplies, recognizing that 14 days may be insufficient [6].

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
While PACCAR supports the principle of providing appropriate regulatory flexibility during genuine emergencies, we are concerned about the potential for unintended consequences related to the development and deployment of vehicle safety technologies and autonomous systems. Our analysis reveals several specific impacts:

**1. Regulatory Uncertainty for Autonomous Vehicle (AV) and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) Testing and Validation:**
PACCAR's Kenworth and Peterbilt divisions are at the forefront of developing and testing ADAS and AV technologies, which involve multi-year design cycles (typically 3-5 years) to ensure rigorous validation and safety compliance. When a Governor or FMCSA declares a regional emergency, the automatic 30-day relief from FMCSRs creates regulatory ambiguity regarding which specific rules are suspended. If ADAS-related operational requirements—such as data logging, disengagement reporting, or minimum safety performance standards—are inadvertently swept into the blanket relief, PACCAR's ongoing AV testing programs could operate in a legally uncertain environment [4, 5]. This ambiguity could invalidate months of test data, delay product development timelines by years, and expose PACCAR to unforeseen liability, thereby acting as a significant innovation barrier to the deployment of these safety-enhancing technologies. The Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse in March 2024, which triggered a Maryland emergency declaration, demonstrated the kind of multi-week, multi-jurisdictional scenario where telematics data interpretation and operational clarity for advanced systems become critical [2].

**2. Lack of Adequate Lead Time for Vehicle Safety Technology Mandates and Specifications:**
PACCAR's Class 8 heavy-duty trucks, including vocational configurations, are frequently among the first assets mobilized during regional emergencies [8, 9]. Under the current 14-day window, fleet operators and emergency managers have reported pressure to push vehicles beyond their designed duty cycles within a compressed timeframe, creating downstream warranty, liability, and safety concerns for PACCAR as the original equipment manufacturer. Extending the exemption to 30 days without corresponding OEM guidance on permissible operational deviations leaves PACCAR exposed to ambiguous liability when vehicles operated under exemption experience accelerated wear or component failure. Our 3-to-5-year vehicle design cycle means that if emergency-use operational parameters for safety-critical components are not clearly defined now, the next generation of Kenworth and Peterbilt platforms—currently in development—cannot be engineered to accommodate those parameters in a compliant and predictable way, impacting the lead time needed for safety technology integration.

**3. Operational Burden and Compliance Ambiguity for Connected Vehicle Telematics Data:**
PACCAR's Kenworth and Peterbilt trucks are equipped with sophisticated telematics and connected vehicle systems that continuously log Hours of Service (HOS) data, vehicle speed, location, and driver behavior metrics. During a 14-day emergency window, fleet operators and PACCAR's OEM systems operate under a defined—if brief—period of regulatory suspension. Extending that window to 30 days without clarifying how telematics data recorded during that period should be archived, flagged, or excluded from compliance analytics creates a significant operational burden. Our connected vehicle platforms are designed around FMCSA regulatory parameters; when those parameters are suspended for an indeterminate or ambiguously defined period, the data pipelines that feed compliance dashboards, safety scoring systems, and post-incident review tools generate conflicting or misleading outputs [2, 3, 6, 7]. Fleet managers relying on PACCAR-integrated telematics to manage driver risk face confusion about which data records are compliance-relevant and which reflect emergency-period exemptions.

**4. Potential for Over-Reliance on HOS Exemptions and Safety Risks:**
As both a manufacturer dependent on inbound freight and a producer of the very Class 8 vehicles operating under these exemptions, PACCAR has a dual stake in ensuring that HOS relief is calibrated to genuine emergency necessity. PACCAR's vehicle design and ADAS technology development — including advanced driver assistance systems integrated into Kenworth and Peterbilt platforms — is premised on a regulatory environment that maintains baseline driver fatigue standards. A blanket 30-day automatic exemption covering all carriers operating in a declared region, regardless of whether their specific routes or operations are materially affected by the emergency, could normalize extended HOS operations in ways that increase crash risk on the same highway corridors used by PACCAR's supplier network [1]. The 2023 Final Rule's revision of the 'emergency' definition to exclude purely economic conditions like labor strikes or inflation was a meaningful safeguard; PACCAR is concerned that restoring a longer automatic window without reinforcing that definitional boundary could erode the distinction between genuine emergency response and routine operational convenience.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
PACCAR urges FMCSA to adopt the 30-day automatic relief period but with critical safeguards and clarifications to support the responsible development and deployment of vehicle safety technologies and autonomous systems:

1.  **Establish Clear Frameworks for Autonomous Vehicle (AV) and ADAS Testing and Data Reporting:** FMCSA should amend the proposed rule to explicitly clarify—in regulatory text or accompanying guidance—that emergency relief under 49 CFR § 390.23 applies narrowly to hours-of-service and driver qualification rules (as historically intended for emergency logistics) and does not extend to technology performance standards, safety data collection mandates, ADAS/AV operational design domain restrictions, or technology validation frameworks. Additionally, FMCSA should convene a technical working group inclusive of connected vehicle OEMs to develop standardized data-tagging protocols for emergency-period data, ensuring consistency across platforms and facilitating accurate compliance analysis.
2.  **Provide Adequate Lead Time and Performance-Based Guidance for Vehicle Safety Technology Mandates:** FMCSA should extend the exemption period to 30 days as proposed, but simultaneously publish a companion guidance document that defines the specific Hours of Service, weight, and maintenance interval deviations that apply to Class 8 OEM-warranted vehicles during the exemption period. This guidance should prioritize performance outcomes and safety objectives rather than overly prescriptive design requirements, allowing for innovation in safety technology. FMCSA should also establish a safe harbor for OEMs when vehicles operated under emergency exemption experience accelerated wear or component failure, and require post-emergency reporting by motor carriers on vehicle utilization data. This provides the predictability and lead time needed for PACCAR's multi-year design cycles for safety-critical components.
3.  **Standardize ELD and Telematics Data Handling During Exemptions:** FMCSA should accompany the restoration of the 30-day standard with a formal technical bulletin or regulatory clarification specifying: (1) the precise trigger conditions under which ELD systems may lawfully reflect suspended HOS obligations; (2) a standardized data field or flag within the ELD technical standard (49 CFR Part 395, Appendix A) to denote active emergency exemption status; and (3) a safe harbor provision for carriers whose ELD systems log apparent HOS violations during a declared exemption period, provided the carrier can document the applicable declaration. This would allow PACCAR and other manufacturers to build compliant, automated exemption-recognition functionality into future ELD firmware updates.
4.  **Enhance Predictability and Scope Limitations for Emergency Relief Activation:** FMCSA should adopt the 30-day extension but simultaneously establish a mandatory, standardized public notification protocol requiring that: (1) FMCSA publish a clear, machine-readable registry of all active emergency declarations and their precise expiration dates in real time; (2) any extension beyond the automatic 30-day period be announced no fewer than 72 hours before expiration to allow shippers and manufacturers to adjust logistics plans; and (3) FMCSA develop guidance distinguishing between emergency declarations affecting interstate supply corridors versus localized last-mile delivery zones. Furthermore, FMCSA should explicitly reaffirm—in the final rule's regulatory text and preamble—that the revised 'emergency' definition from the October 2023 rule (excluding economic conditions absent immediate threat to human life) remains fully in force and is not weakened by this amendment.

[CONCLUSION]
PACCAR supports the intent of the proposed rule to provide greater flexibility during regional emergencies. However, as an OEM deeply invested in vehicle safety and technological innovation, we urge FMCSA to ensure that emergency relief provisions do not inadvertently create regulatory uncertainty or impede the responsible development and deployment of critical safety technologies, including ADAS and autonomous vehicles. By providing clear frameworks for AV testing, adequate lead time for safety technology mandates through performance-based guidance, and standardized data handling for telematics systems, FMCSA can achieve both operational flexibility during emergencies and long-term advancements in road safety. We stand ready to collaborate with FMCSA and other stakeholders to develop these essential clarifications.

### References

{"draft": "Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways
[CRASH Letterhead - Placeholder]
[CRASH Address - Placeholder]

[Date - Placeholder]

Submitted via Federal eRulemaking Portal
Docket ID: FMCSA-2025-0124

The Honorable Robin Hutcheson
Administrator
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE
Washington, DC 20590

**Re: Proposed Rule: Emergency Relief; Extension of Automatic Emergency Relief Duration (FMCSA-2025-0124)**

[INTRODUCTION]
Dear Administrator Hutcheson,

Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways (CRASH) is a national grassroots advocacy organization dedicated to improving highway safety and protecting critical transportation infrastructure. Our mission is rooted in preventing large truck crashes, reducing fatalities and injuries, and advocating for safe, sustainable transportation policies. We represent the voices of families, communities, and commuters who share our nation’s roads and bridges with commercial vehicles. CRASH has a long-standing commitment to ensuring that federal regulations adequately balance operational flexibility with public safety and infrastructure preservation.

We appreciate the opportunity to submit comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed rule (Docket ID: FMCSA-2025-0124) to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days following a regional emergency declaration. While CRASH fundamentally opposes any changes that would authorize generalized truck size and weight increases or undermine current federal limits without rigorous, evidence-based safety and infrastructure impact assessments, we understand the operational challenges presented by the current 14-day limit during prolonged regional emergencies. We believe that a *structured* 30-day relief window, *if paired with robust safeguards against unmonitored size and weight increases and infrastructure damage*, can be less dangerous and more transparent than the current system, which often leads to informal, unmonitored extensions or rushed operations.

Our comments will focus on how this proposed extension intersects with our core advocacy areas: preventing truck crashes, protecting bridge and road infrastructure, and ensuring effective enforcement of size and weight regulations.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA’s proposed rule seeks to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. This change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself. The proposal specifically aims to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state. These regulations typically fall under **49 CFR 390.23**, which addresses relief from regulations during emergencies.

While the primary focus of this proposed rule is on the *duration* of emergency relief, CRASH notes that emergency declarations frequently include exemptions from truck size and weight limits. These exemptions, even temporary ones, pose unique and significant risks to highway safety and critical infrastructure. Our organization’s interest in this rulemaking stems from the direct implications of extended emergency exemptions on the safety of motorists and the integrity of our nation’s bridges and roads. The current 14-day limit, rather than improving safety, may perversely incentivize carriers to rush operations into a compressed window, increasing crash risk and potentially exacerbating infrastructure damage. A 30-day window, *with structured oversight*, could mitigate this "deadline pressure" dynamic while allowing sufficient time for proper infrastructure assessment and safety monitoring.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
CRASH’s analysis of the proposed rule focuses on several critical intersections between emergency relief duration, public safety, and infrastructure resilience.

**1. Safety Risks of Unmonitored Weight Violations and Rushed Operations:**
The proposed **extension of automatic emergency relief from 14 days to 30 days** presents a critical opportunity to reduce the risk of unmonitored, ad hoc weight limit violations during emergencies, a period when large truck crash risks are already elevated. Our organization's advocacy work is grounded in crash fatality and injury data. In 2023, large truck crashes resulted in 5,078 fatalities and 86,842 injuries nationally [1]. While 2024 showed a modest 2.5% decrease in fatalities involving large trucks, injuries in traffic crashes involving large trucks actually increased by 5.0% [1]. Emergency periods — when roads are damaged, detour routes are used, and drivers face pressure to deliver relief supplies quickly — are precisely when these risks spike.

The current 14-day limit, rather than improving safety, may perversely incentivize carriers to rush operations into a compressed window, increasing crash risk. A compressed timeframe can lead to drivers pushing limits, operating fatigued, or making hasty decisions, all of which contribute to an elevated risk of crashes. Our members have observed anecdotal evidence of this "deadline pressure" dynamic in post-disaster trucking corridors. An extended 30-day window, *if paired with structured oversight*, could reduce this pressure and allow for more deliberate, safer planning and execution of relief operations, thereby potentially reducing crash rates.

**2. Inadequate Bridge and Road Infrastructure Data Assessment within 14 Days:**
Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways (CRASH) strongly supports extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 to 30 days because the current 14-day window is structurally incompatible with the time required to assess and protect bridge and road infrastructure during regional emergencies. This gap creates a real risk of overloaded vehicles operating on compromised infrastructure without adequate data-driven oversight.

CRASH's core mission depends on accurate, current bridge and road infrastructure data to advocate for appropriate truck size and weight limits. During a regional emergency — such as the catastrophic Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse in March 2024, which triggered a Maryland gubernatorial emergency declaration [2], [3] — infrastructure conditions change rapidly and unpredictably. The 14-day automatic relief window expires before transportation agencies, state DOTs, and advocacy organizations like ours can compile updated load-bearing assessments, detour route structural ratings, and bridge condition inventories for the affected region.

This forces carriers operating under emergency exemptions onto alternative routes whose infrastructure data may be outdated or incomplete, with no regulatory mechanism to ensure those routes can safely bear the weight of vehicles operating outside normal size and weight limits. Our organization, operating with limited resources against well-funded industry lobbying, cannot rapidly produce counter-data within a 14-day window to flag infrastructure vulnerabilities on improvised emergency corridors. A 30-day window would provide a more realistic timeframe for these critical assessments to be completed and disseminated, reducing the risk of infrastructure damage from overloaded vehicles.

**3. Risk of Misinterpreting Automatic Relief as Suspension of Bridge Posting Limits and Infrastructure-Specific Weight Restrictions:**
CRASH supports the 30-day automatic relief extension and urges FMCSA to pair it with a requirement that any automatic relief triggered by a **gubernatorial declaration** explicitly preserve — and not supersede — existing bridge posting limits and infrastructure-specific weight restrictions. This is crucial to ensure that the extended exemption period does not inadvertently authorize vehicle operations that damage the very infrastructure emergency response depends upon.

Our organization's advocacy work is grounded in the principle that truck size and weight limits exist because infrastructure has finite load tolerances, and that those tolerances are documented in bridge inspection records and state posting orders. During emergencies, the pressure to move goods and relief supplies quickly creates strong industry incentives to interpret regulatory relief as broadly as possible. A 30-day automatic relief window, without explicit carve-outs for infrastructure-specific restrictions, could be read by some carriers or enforcement agencies as suspending not just hours-of-service rules but also weight-based infrastructure protections. This misinterpretation risk is heightened during chaotic emergency conditions when enforcement resources are stretched thin [2], [3].

CRASH, with its limited resources, cannot litigate or contest individual carrier interpretations in real time — we depend on clear regulatory text to do the protective work. An explicit clarification would cost the agency nothing operationally, eliminate interpretive ambiguity, and ensure that the extended relief window strengthens emergency response without creating new infrastructure damage risk.

**4. Addressing the Dangerous Regulatory Vacuum and Informal Enforcement Lapses:**
Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways (CRASH) strongly supports extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days because the current 14-day window creates a dangerous regulatory vacuum during prolonged emergencies that effectively suspends truck size and weight enforcement at precisely the moment when overloaded vehicles pose the greatest risk to already-stressed infrastructure.

As a grassroots advocacy organization focused on truck size and weight limits, CRASH relies on the integrity of federal weight enforcement as the baseline from which we educate the public and policymakers. When emergency exemptions expire after only 14 days but the emergency itself continues — as is common with hurricanes, floods, and other regional disasters — carriers and states operate in an ambiguous gray zone where enforcement lapses informally even without legal authority. This informal non-enforcement is harder for our organization to track, document, and challenge than a formally authorized 30-day window with defined boundaries.

A clear, time-bounded 30-day relief period with explicit start and end dates is actually more enforceable and transparent than a 14-day period that gets repeatedly extended or ignored in practice. Our limited staff and resources are better deployed monitoring a single, clearly defined relief window than chasing piecemeal extensions and informal enforcement gaps. This extended window provides an opportunity for FMCSA to implement stronger mid-period safety checkpoints rather than treating the 30 days as a blanket suspension of oversight.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on our analysis of the proposed rule and its potential impacts on highway safety and infrastructure, CRASH offers the following specific recommendations to FMCSA:

1.  **Mandatory Mid-Period Safety Review:** FMCSA should amend the proposed rule to require that any automatic 30-day relief period include a mandatory safety review at the 15-day mark. At this point, the issuing Governor or FMCSA must affirmatively certify that: (a) the emergency conditions justifying size and weight relief remain active; (b) no significant bridge or pavement failures attributable to overweight loads have been reported; and (c) crash rates on affected corridors have not materially increased. This mid-period checkpoint would preserve the operational flexibility of a 30-day window while creating a structured accountability moment that our organization and other public interest groups can monitor and, if necessary, challenge. This recommendation addresses safety risks (Analysis Point 1) and the regulatory vacuum (Analysis Point 4).

2.  **Publicly Accessible Infrastructure Inventory:** FMCSA should finalize the proposed 30-day automatic relief period and additionally require that, as a condition of automatic relief triggered by a gubernatorial declaration, state emergency management agencies coordinate with state DOTs to publish a publicly accessible inventory of approved emergency route infrastructure ratings — including bridge sufficiency ratings and posted weight limits — within the first 72 hours of a gubernatorial emergency declaration. This would allow advocacy organizations and the public to monitor whether emergency vehicle operations are consistent with actual infrastructure capacity throughout the full 30-day window, not just the first 14 days. This recommendation addresses infrastructure data assessment (Analysis Point 2) and the preservation of limits (Analysis Point 3).

3.  **Explicit Preservation of Infrastructure-Specific Weight Restrictions:** FMCSA should finalize the 30-day extension as proposed but amend the regulatory text to include an explicit statement that automatic emergency relief does not modify, suspend, or supersede state or federally posted bridge weight limits, load restrictions, or infrastructure-specific size and weight postings. This clarification eliminates interpretive ambiguity and ensures that the extended relief window strengthens emergency response without creating new infrastructure damage risk. FMCSA should also require that any extension requests beyond 30 days include a state DOT certification that proposed emergency routes have been assessed for structural adequacy. This recommendation directly addresses the preservation of limits (Analysis Point 3).

4.  **Mandatory Public Reporting and Post-Relief Inspection Protocol:** FMCSA should adopt the proposed 30-day automatic relief period but pair it with two critical safeguards that the agency has not yet proposed: (1) a mandatory public reporting requirement obligating states to document which routes received relief, what weight and size limits were suspended, and what infrastructure assessments were conducted before and after the relief period; and (2) a post-relief bridge and pavement inspection protocol, particularly for structures already rated as structurally deficient, to detect damage caused by heavier loads during the emergency window. These additions would allow advocacy organizations like CRASH to hold agencies accountable using the infrastructure data we depend on, without opposing the operational flexibility that legitimate emergency response requires. This recommendation addresses the regulatory vacuum (Analysis Point 4) and infrastructure data (Analysis Point 2).

In addition to these specific recommendations regarding emergency relief, CRASH strongly urges FMCSA to reaffirm its commitment to the current federal limits on truck size and weight. While we recognize the need for temporary exemptions during emergencies, we fundamentally oppose any efforts to loosen these limits more broadly. The current federal limits are essential for protecting our nation's aging infrastructure, which is already under severe strain, and for ensuring the safety of all road users. Overweight vehicles accelerate pavement deterioration, increase bridge fatigue, and contribute to higher crash severity. Enhanced enforcement of these limits, particularly on vulnerable routes and infrastructure, remains a critical priority for CRASH.

[CONCLUSION]
Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways (CRASH) appreciates FMCSA’s proposal to extend the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days. We believe that this extension, *when coupled with the robust safeguards outlined above*, has the potential to enhance safety, improve infrastructure protection, and provide greater regulatory clarity during regional emergencies. However, we cannot support an extension that lacks critical oversight and explicit protections for our nation’s infrastructure against unmonitored size and weight increases.

We urge FMCSA to adopt our recommendations, which are designed to ensure that emergency relief serves its intended purpose of facilitating response and recovery without compromising public safety or accelerating infrastructure damage. By incorporating these safeguards, FMCSA can create a more effective, accountable, and safer framework for emergency regulatory relief.

Thank you for your consideration of our comments.

Sincerely,

[Signature - Placeholder]

[Name - Placeholder]
[Title - Placeholder]
Citizens for Reliable and Safe Highways (CRASH)

---
**Sources:**
[1] [PDF] Overview of Motor Vehicle Traffic Crashes In 2024. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA). URL: https://crashstats.nhtsa.dot.gov/Api/Public/ViewPublication/813791
[2] Regulatory Update - January 9, 2026. Commercial Vehicle Safety Alliance (CVSA). URL: https://cvsa.org/regulatory-updates/regulatory-update-january-9-2026/
[3] FMCSA Finalizes Changes to Emergency Trucking Exemptions. PrePass. URL: https://prepass.com/blog/fmcsa-finalizes-changes-to-emergency-trucking-exemptions/

### References
[1] https://crashstats.nhtsa.dot.gov/Api/Public/ViewPublication/813791
[2] https://cvsa.org/regulatory-updates/regulatory-update-january-9-2026/
[3] https://prepass.com/blog/fmcsa-finalizes-changes-to-emergency-trucking-exemptions/
