{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Honorable Amit Bose
Administrator
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590

**Re: Clarifications to the Duration of Regional Emergency Declarations (Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124)**

Dear Administrator Bose:

North American Van Lines (NAVL) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposal to extend the automatic exemption period for regional emergency declarations from 14 days to 30 days. As one of the largest and most experienced providers of interstate household goods moving services in the United States, NAVL operates through a nationwide network of independent agents and drivers, routinely handling multi-day, multi-state residential moves, including critical government and corporate relocations.

While we recognize the operational benefits of a longer emergency relief period for certain freight movements, NAVL is concerned that the proposed rule, without specific clarifications and safeguards tailored to the unique dynamics of the household goods moving industry, will create unintended compliance costs, operational disruptions, and capacity drain during our peak summer season. Our comments below are therefore conditioned on the agency incorporating explicit guidance that addresses the distinct challenges faced by household goods carriers.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA is proposing to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) to extend the automatic exemption period following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days. This change would apply to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself, effectively reversing a modification made by a final rule published in October 2023. The proposal seeks to restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, suggesting that the current 14-day limit may be insufficient for addressing logistical and safety needs during regional emergencies, particularly for "direct assistance" operations. While the specific CFR sections are not explicitly cited in the proposed rule text, these regulations typically fall under 49 CFR 390.23 regarding relief from regulations during emergencies, and potentially 49 CFR 390.25 concerning extensions.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
NAVL's concerns stem from several unique aspects of household goods moving operations that are not adequately addressed by a blanket 30-day automatic exemption:

**Operational Burden of Multi-Day, Multi-State Household Goods Moves:**
Unlike freight carriers executing single-day point-to-point hauls, NAVL's interstate residential moves involve multi-day operations: packing on Day 1, loading on Day 2, transit across multiple states on Days 3–5, and delivery and unloading on Days 6–7 or later [4], [6]. When a regional emergency declaration is issued mid-move, our drivers may be operating under the Hours of Service (HOS) exemption in one state while the shipment's origin or destination state has not declared an emergency, or vice versa. The 14-day window established by the October 2023 final rule proved dangerously short for moves affected by Hurricane Helene in September 2024, when seven states simultaneously issued declarations [1]. Drivers hauling household goods into western North Carolina or coastal Georgia faced road closures, mandatory evacuation orders, and customer inaccessibility that extended well beyond 14 days, yet the exemption lapsed before the operational disruption resolved [1]. This creates significant compliance ambiguity and operational burden for our agent-based driver network [3].

**Compliance Costs and Contractual Conflicts with Government Contracts:**
As a major Transportation Service Provider (TSP) operating under U.S. Transportation Command (TRANSCOM) and General Services Administration (GSA) household goods contracts, NAVL is contractually obligated to meet specific pickup and delivery windows, often measured in days, regardless of regional emergency conditions [2], [3]. When a Governor or FMCSA issues a regional emergency declaration, our drivers may lawfully operate under extended HOS relief, but our government contracts do not automatically mirror that relief. This creates a compliance gap: drivers are legally permitted to operate differently under FMCSA rules, yet NAVL simultaneously faces contract penalties, performance score degradation, and potential loss of future contract awards if deliveries fall outside contractually mandated windows [2], [3]. During Hurricanes Helene and Milton in fall 2024, FMCSA issued and extended emergency declarations through late November 2024—well beyond 14 days—demonstrating that real-world emergencies routinely exceed the prior limit. However, TRANSCOM's Defense Personal Property Program (DP3) performance metrics continued to accrue during those periods, creating asymmetric regulatory and contractual obligations for TSPs like NAVL operating in affected regions [3]. This imposes significant compliance costs not acknowledged in the proposed rule's analysis.

**Driver Diversion and Summer Peak Season Capacity Drain:**
NAVL operates through an agent-based driver network where driver availability is pre-committed weeks or months in advance for multi-day interstate residential moves. Summer peak season—June through August—represents approximately 40-50% of annual household goods volume, with corporate relocation clients and government contracts carrying strict delivery windows and liquidated damages clauses [5]. When a regional emergency declaration triggers a 30-day automatic exemption, drivers and owner-operators within our agent network face strong financial incentives to redirect to emergency freight (e.g., disaster relief, fuel, utilities), which often pays premium spot rates [5]. A 30-day window—more than double the current 14-day period—dramatically increases the probability that this driver diversion will overlap with peak booking periods. With the American Trucking Associations estimating an 80,000-driver shortage at peak season in 2023, representing a 2% nationwide capacity loss, and ATRI's 2024 data showing 45% of carriers already short-staffed for regional routes, our agent network has virtually no surge capacity to absorb defections lasting a full month [8]. A single regional emergency declaration covering a major corridor (e.g., Gulf Coast, Southeast, or Mid-Atlantic) during July could render us unable to fulfill dozens of committed government and corporate moves simultaneously, creating cascading service failures and economic harm [5], [9].

**Ambiguous Definition of "Regional" and Dual-Tracking Compliance Burden:**
Our agent-based driver network regularly executes multi-day, multi-state household goods moves that originate, transit, or terminate in regions that may be subject to a Governor's emergency declaration [2], [3], [5]. A single long-haul government relocation move—for example, a military family relocating from a base in North Carolina to one in Virginia during a hurricane emergency—may involve a driver who is subject to emergency HOS relief for one portion of the route but not another. Under the current and proposed rules, the burden falls on the carrier to document, in real time, which portions of a trip qualify for relief and which do not [2], [3], [5]. Extending the automatic relief period to 30 days increases the duration over which this dual-tracking compliance burden applies. During peak summer relocation season, when NAVL may have hundreds of active government moves simultaneously, the administrative cost of maintaining compliant ELD records that accurately reflect emergency relief eligibility across shifting regional boundaries is substantial and not acknowledged in the proposed rule's cost-benefit analysis [2], [3], [5].

**Lack of Empirical Basis for 30-Day Extension and Unintended Costs:**
NAVL is concerned that the proposed rule's reversion to a 30-day automatic exemption period lacks an empirical basis demonstrating that the October 2023 reduction to 14 days caused measurable harm to emergency response operations [5]. Since the October 2023 final rule took effect, NAVL has not observed evidence that 14-day regional exemptions have failed to provide adequate carrier capacity for emergency response [5]. Our agent network has successfully navigated regional weather events and localized emergencies under the 14-day framework without being called upon to extend operations beyond that window. Meanwhile, the summer peak season driver availability problem is concrete and recurring: we face documented shortfalls every June through August, and a 30-day exemption window materially increases the duration during which our pre-committed drivers face competing financial incentives to redirect to emergency freight [5], [8]. The agency is proposing to reverse a rule that has been in effect for less than two years without presenting data showing the 14-day window was operationally insufficient [5].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these concerns and ensure that the 30-day automatic relief period is both effective for emergency response and sustainable for the household goods moving industry, NAVL offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Clarify HOS Exemption Applicability for Multi-State Household Goods Moves:**
    FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period as proposed, but should simultaneously issue supplemental guidance—either in the final rule preamble or as a companion enforcement policy—clarifying that:
    *   A driver who began a multi-day interstate household goods move under normal HOS rules and subsequently enters a declared emergency zone mid-transit is covered by the exemption for the duration of that shipment's delivery, not merely for 30 calendar days from the declaration date [1], [4], [6].
    *   The exemption applies when the driver is operating in any state that has issued a qualifying declaration, even if the shipment originated in a non-declared state [1], [4], [6].
    *   *Justification:* This addresses the operational burden of multi-day, multi-state moves (Impact 1) and the ambiguous definition of "regional" (Impact 4), ensuring compliance certainty and reducing administrative burden.

2.  **Coordinate with Federal Contracting Agencies to Pause Timelines:**
    FMCSA should coordinate with the Department of Defense (TRANSCOM), GSA, and other federal contracting agencies to issue companion guidance—concurrent with any emergency declaration—that formally suspends or tolls government moving contract performance timelines for TSPs operating in the declared emergency region [2], [3]. The rule should include explicit language directing FMCSA to notify relevant federal contracting authorities within 24 hours of any regional emergency declaration so that contract officers can issue corresponding stop-work or performance-pause notices [2], [3].
    *   *Justification:* This eliminates the compliance gap between FMCSA regulatory relief and federal contract performance obligations (Impact 2), protecting carriers from penalties. NAVL also recommends incorporating a good-faith safe harbor provision stating that carriers operating under a documented federal government task order or contract directive within a declared emergency region shall not be subject to HOS enforcement action for the duration of the federally directed operation, provided the carrier maintains records of the government directive [2], [3], [7].

3.  **Establish Clear Administrative Protocols for Extensions:**
    FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic exemption period as proposed, but should additionally commit to issuing formal extension or termination notices no later than day 21 of any active exemption period [4].
    *   *Justification:* This provides carriers like NAVL a minimum 9-day operational planning window before any change in HOS relief status takes effect, reducing uncertainty for our agent network during peak season (Impact 3).

4.  **Implement a Tiered Relief Duration Framework with Safeguards for Peak Season:**
    FMCSA should adopt a tiered relief duration framework rather than a blanket 30-day extension, specifically:
    *   Retain the 14-day automatic exemption as the default for regional declarations [5].
    *   Allow extension to 30 days only upon a secondary finding by the FMCSA Administrator that the emergency scope demonstrably requires additional carrier capacity beyond what 14 days provides [5].
    *   Require that carriers invoking the exemption beyond 14 days provide notice to FMCSA of the specific operations covered, enabling the agency to monitor whether pre-committed household goods moves are being abandoned [5].
    *   *Justification:* This addresses the critical issue of driver diversion and summer peak season capacity drain (Impact 3) and the lack of empirical basis for a universal 30-day extension (Impact 5), preserving emergency response flexibility while protecting the integrity of the pre-committed interstate moving market.

[CONCLUSION]
North American Van Lines supports the intent of extending HOS flexibility during regional emergencies. However, for this proposal to be effective and sustainable for the household goods moving industry, it must acknowledge and address our unique operational realities, including multi-day, multi-state moves, peak season capacity constraints, and the intersection with federal government contracts. We urge FMCSA to incorporate the specific clarifications and safeguards outlined above, particularly those related to HOS applicability, federal contract coordination, and peak season driver retention.

We appreciate FMCSA's consideration of these comments and look forward to continued collaboration to ensure safe, compliant, and efficient emergency response while maintaining the critical flow of household goods across the nation.

Sincerely,

[Signature omitted for privacy]

### References

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The National Association of Small Trucking Companies (NASTC) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) seeking to extend the automatic emergency relief period for regional declarations from 14 days to 30 days. NASTC represents over 11,000 small and mid-sized motor carriers, owner-operators, and private fleets across the United States, advocating for policies that promote safety, efficiency, and competitive fairness within the trucking industry. Our members, typically operating between 5 and 100 trucks with limited administrative staff, are directly and disproportionately affected by changes to emergency regulatory relief provisions.

NASTC strongly opposes the proposed extension of the automatic emergency relief period for regional declarations from 14 days to 30 days. While emergency relief is crucial for disaster response, a blanket 30-day automatic window creates unnecessary compliance ambiguity and disproportionate administrative burdens for small carriers, without demonstrated safety benefits. We urge FMCSA to retain the 14-day automatic relief period established in October 2023, and instead focus on streamlining the extension request process for small carriers when longer relief is genuinely needed.

[BACKGROUND]
The current regulatory framework, established by a final rule published in October 2023, limits the automatic regulatory relief period following a regional declaration of emergency by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA, to 14 days [4, 8]. Prior to this change, the automatic relief period was 30 days. Presidential declarations of emergency continue to trigger a 30-day exemption [8]. The NPRM proposes to reverse the 2023 modification and revert the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to 30 days. This change would primarily affect 49 CFR 390.23, which governs relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
NASTC’s opposition to the proposed 30-day automatic extension is rooted in several key concerns regarding its impacts on small carriers and owner-operators:

1.  **Disruption to Small Carrier Compliance and Operations:**
    The October 2023 final rule, while initially requiring adjustment, established a clear and manageable 14-day automatic relief period for regional emergencies [4]. Our member carriers, averaging 5 to 20 trucks with no in-house compliance staff, invested significant time and resources after October 2023 to retrain drivers, update internal dispatch protocols, and recalibrate emergency response procedures around this 14-day window [5]. Many relied on our association's compliance assistance programs to implement these changes. Reverting to a 30-day automatic window now forces another round of retraining, policy rewrites, and driver communications at direct cost to small operators who cannot absorb repeated regulatory pivots. Unlike large carriers with dedicated safety departments, our members depend on external compliance assistance that is finite in capacity and costly to access repeatedly. Each regulatory reversal consumes that capacity without delivering net safety benefit.

2.  **Increased Compliance Ambiguity and Liability Risks:**
    A blanket 30-day automatic relief window creates significant compliance ambiguity and liability risks for small carriers. While emergency relief is intended to help them respond to disasters, an overly broad 30-day automatic window creates its own compliance trap. When relief is automatically triggered for 30 days, small carriers must independently track whether the exemption still applies to their specific load, route, and commodity, because not all operations during an emergency qualify [7]. Without in-house legal or compliance staff, our members have historically received citations and faced liability for misapplying the scope of relief—believing they were covered when they were not, or failing to document their operations adequately for the full 30-day window [8]. The 14-day window, paired with clearer scope limitations established in the October 2023 rule, gave small carriers a more defined and manageable compliance horizon. Reverting to 30 days re-exposes our members to that ambiguity for twice as long.

    Furthermore, when an emergency exemption is in effect, drivers and dispatchers operate under relaxed hours-of-service rules, but the safety data generated during that window—incidents, near-misses, log deviations—still flows into FMCSA's Safety Measurement System (SMS) and can affect a carrier's CSA scores and safety fitness determinations [7]. Doubling the exemption window from 14 to 30 days doubles the period during which our members accumulate data points that may later be scrutinized without the contextual flag that emergency conditions existed. Small carriers lack the legal staff to contest adverse SMS entries or to document, after the fact, that a violation occurred during a declared emergency. Large carriers have compliance teams that proactively annotate records and challenge erroneous data; our members do not. This creates disproportionate compliance and liability risks that large carriers can absorb but small fleets cannot.

3.  **Lack of Evidentiary Justification and Regulatory Instability:**
    FMCSA has not presented sufficient new evidence to justify extending the automatic relief period back to 30 days for regional emergencies. The agency previously acknowledged it lacked data to justify reducing the relief period from 30 to 14 days in 2023 [3], yet finalized that reduction. Now, it proposes to restore the 30-day period, again without addressing the underlying data gap—creating regulatory instability that imposes recurring, uncompensated compliance costs on small carriers who must repeatedly retrain drivers and update internal policies each time the standard changes. Each time FMCSA changes the automatic emergency relief duration, our member carriers must update their driver handbooks, retrain dispatchers on Hours of Service exemption tracking, revise their emergency response protocols, and communicate changes to drivers who may be operating across multiple states under different declaration timelines. For a carrier with 10 trucks and no compliance officer, this retraining and policy revision process costs an estimated 8–15 hours of owner-operator time per regulatory change—time that directly competes with revenue-generating operations [9]. Regulatory ping-pong between 14 and 30 days is not a neutral administrative exercise; it is a recurring, uncompensated compliance tax on small businesses that large carriers absorb through dedicated staff.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address the concerns outlined above and to promote safety while supporting small business regulatory relief and tiered compliance, NASTC offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Retain the 14-Day Automatic Relief Period:** FMCSA should withdraw the proposed rule and maintain the 14-day automatic emergency relief period for regional declarations, as established by the October 2023 final rule. This period strikes a reasonable balance, providing initial relief without creating prolonged ambiguity or administrative burden.

2.  **Streamline Extension Requests for Small Carriers:** If FMCSA determines that longer relief periods are operationally necessary in specific disasters, it should create a formal, carrier-initiated extension request process—similar to the continuation mechanism used in the April–June 2024 Maryland/Port of Baltimore emergency [6]—that is specifically tailored for small carriers.
    *   **Simplified Form:** FMCSA should develop a simplified, one-page electronic extension request form—distinct from the full exemption petition process—that small carriers (defined as those with fewer than 25 power units) can submit with minimal documentation burden.
    *   **Presumption of Approval:** Extensions requested by small carriers should be presumed approved when a Governor's emergency declaration remains active and the carrier is actively engaged in emergency relief operations. This targeted approach ensures that relief is extended only to carriers actually performing emergency-related operations, reducing the compliance ambiguity that blanket 30-day automatic windows create for small operators.

3.  **Commitment to Regulatory Stability and Small Business Impact Review:** FMCSA should accompany the retention of the 14-day automatic relief period with a formal commitment—codified in the regulatory text or preamble—to maintain this standard absent specific, quantified safety data demonstrating harm attributable to the shorter window. The agency should also establish a small business impact review process, consistent with the Regulatory Flexibility Act, that specifically evaluates the per-carrier compliance cost of any future changes to § 390.23 emergency relief durations before proposing modifications.

[CONCLUSION]
NASTC appreciates FMCSA's efforts to facilitate emergency response, but respectfully submits that the proposed extension of the automatic relief period to 30 days for regional declarations will inadvertently create more compliance challenges and liability risks for small trucking companies without clear safety benefits. We urge the agency to retain the 14-day automatic relief period and instead implement a streamlined, tiered extension process that supports small carriers while ensuring safety and accountability. Thank you for considering our comments.

### References
[3] https://landline.media/truck-drivers-support-changing-back-to-30-day-emergency-window
[4] https://www.feesmith.com?p=5667
[5] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008
[6] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/fmcsa-continuation-extension-emergency-declaration-under-49-cfr-ss-39025-no-2024-002-0
[7] https://freedomheavyhaul.com/we-explain-why-emergency-declarations-impact-trucking-regulations/
[8] https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/10/13/2023-22538/clarification-to-the-applicability-of-emergency-exemptions
[9] https://www.ams.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media/FMMO_NMPF_53A.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Daimler Truck North America (DTNA), including its Freightliner, Western Star, Detroit Diesel, and Torc Robotics brands, respectfully submits these comments in response to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, proposing to extend the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from 14 days to 30 days. As the largest manufacturer of commercial vehicles in North America, DTNA deeply understands the operational complexities, safety imperatives, and technological advancements shaping the trucking industry. While we conditionally support the proposed extension for conventional motor carrier operations, we are profoundly concerned that this rulemaking, as currently framed, fails to address critical, broader policy needs that are essential for fostering a stable, innovative, and sustainable commercial vehicle ecosystem.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA's proposal seeks to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) by extending the automatic exemption period following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days, thereby restoring the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state. This change is intended to address the logistical and safety needs inherent in regional emergencies, which often require longer periods of "direct assistance" to restore essential services or supplies. While we acknowledge the agency's intent to provide greater operational runway for conventional motor carriers during emergencies, DTNA urges FMCSA to use this rulemaking opportunity to concurrently address three fundamental policy areas that are critical for the industry's future and FMCSA's own safety mission: coordinated EPA-FMCSA vehicle standards, autonomous CMV operational design domain (ODD) clarity, and zero-emission truck (ZET) weight exemptions. Failure to address these interconnected issues in a holistic manner will undermine the very resilience and safety FMCSA aims to enhance.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Autonomous CMV Operational Design Domain (ODD) Clarity and Emergency Relief Framework Incompatibility**
DTNA's autonomous trucking subsidiary, Torc Robotics, is actively developing and testing Level 4 autonomous commercial vehicles. Torc's operations are governed by rigorous safety cases and defined ODDs, specifying the conditions (e.g., road types, weather, speed limits, geographic boundaries) under which the AV system is validated to operate safely. The proposed expansion of automatic emergency relief to 30 days, while beneficial for conventional operations, creates a structural incompatibility with the safety architecture of autonomous vehicle testing and limited deployment programs.

Applying a blanket 30-day relief period without distinguishing between conventional and autonomous operations establishes prolonged periods during which the regulatory baseline for CMV operations is undefined. Emergency declarations frequently alter the operational environment: they may open roads to overweight vehicles, change traffic control patterns, or route freight through corridors outside Torc's approved ODD [4], [5]. More fundamentally, the presence of emergency responders, debris, and non-standard traffic patterns in a declared emergency zone creates exactly the kind of edge-case operational environment that AV systems are still being validated to handle. DTNA is concerned that this one-size-fits-all approach creates pressure—from shippers, logistics partners, and internal business units—to deploy AV assets in conditions for which they have not been fully validated, creating genuine safety risk. The current framework acts as a significant innovation barrier, forcing AV testing operators to suspend operations out of an abundance of caution, resulting in lost data collection windows, delayed safety validation milestones, and competitive disadvantage.

**2. Coordinated EPA-FMCSA Vehicle Standards**
As a manufacturer of both conventional and zero-emission trucks, DTNA faces the challenge of complying with a complex and often uncoordinated regulatory landscape. We are concerned that the current approach to vehicle standards, with EPA focused primarily on environmental performance and FMCSA on safety and operational parameters, can create conflicting requirements or operational inefficiencies for motor carriers and OEMs. For instance, EPA's increasingly stringent emissions and ZET mandates, while critical for environmental protection, can have profound implications for vehicle weight, configuration, and maintenance requirements, which fall under FMCSA's purview. Uncoordinated regulations can lead to:
*   **Increased Compliance Costs:** OEMs and motor carriers must navigate distinct and sometimes overlapping compliance processes, leading to higher administrative and engineering costs [3].
*   **Operational Conflicts:** Environmental mandates may inadvertently compromise safety or operational feasibility if not harmonized with FMCSA's safety standards (e.g., increased weight from batteries impacting braking performance or payload capacity).
*   **Innovation Barriers:** The lack of a unified regulatory vision can deter investment in advanced vehicle technologies, as manufacturers face uncertainty about the long-term regulatory stability and operational viability of new designs.

**3. Zero-Emission Truck (ZET) Weight Exemptions**
DTNA is at the forefront of developing and deploying electric and hydrogen fuel cell trucks. These ZETs are inherently heavier than their diesel counterparts due to the weight of batteries, fuel cells, and associated powertrain components. Current federal and state weight limits, which were designed for diesel vehicles, often restrict the payload capacity of ZETs, making them less competitive for certain applications and hindering their broader adoption. We are concerned that, while the proposed rule addresses emergency relief, FMCSA should concurrently address broader policy barriers to ZET adoption, such as weight limits, to facilitate the transition to cleaner fleets. The current emergency relief framework, even with a 30-day window, does not account for the unique operational needs of ZETs, such as charging/refueling infrastructure availability and payload constraints.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
DTNA urges FMCSA to adopt a more holistic and forward-looking approach to commercial vehicle regulation, addressing the proposed emergency relief period in conjunction with these critical, broader policy needs.

**1. Autonomous CMV ODD Clarity and Emergency Operations Protocol:**
*   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should amend the proposed rule to include a new subsection specifically addressing AV testing and limited deployment operations during emergency periods.
*   **Justification:** The current one-size-fits-all emergency relief framework is structurally incompatible with the safety architecture of AV testing programs, creating safety risks and innovation barriers for entities like Torc Robotics.
*   **Specific Provisions:** This subsection should: (1) require AV testing operators to affirmatively opt in to any emergency relief provisions that would alter their approved ODD or exemption conditions, rather than having relief apply automatically; (2) establish a streamlined 24-hour emergency ODD modification request process for AV operators who wish to expand operations to support disaster relief; and (3) create a safe harbor provision confirming that AV testing operators who suspend operations during an emergency period due to ODD incompatibility are not in violation of their testing exemption terms.

**2. Coordinated EPA-FMCSA Vehicle Standards:**
*   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should establish formal inter-agency coordination mechanisms with EPA to align vehicle standards, ensuring that environmental regulations do not inadvertently compromise safety or create operational conflicts for motor carriers and OEMs.
*   **Justification:** Uncoordinated regulations lead to increased compliance costs, operational conflicts, and innovation barriers for the commercial vehicle industry [3].
*   **Specific Provisions:** FMCSA should commit to: (1) regular, structured consultations with EPA on proposed vehicle standards that impact both safety and environmental performance; (2) joint development of technical guidance or best practices where standards intersect; and (3) a process for identifying and resolving potential conflicts between EPA and FMCSA regulations before they are finalized.

**3. Zero-Emission Truck (ZET) Weight Exemptions:**
*   **Recommendation:** FMCSA should advocate for or establish federal weight exemptions for zero-emission commercial vehicles to support their adoption and competitive viability.
*   **Justification:** ZETs are inherently heavier, and current weight limits restrict their payload capacity, hindering their broader adoption and acting as an innovation barrier for electric and hydrogen truck infrastructure deployment.
*   **Specific Provisions:** FMCSA should: (1) support legislation or regulatory changes that provide a minimum federal weight exemption (e.g., at least an additional 2,000 lbs) for ZETs; (2) encourage states to adopt similar exemptions; and (3) conduct a study on the impact of ZET weight on bridge infrastructure and road wear to inform future policy decisions.

[CONCLUSION]
Daimler Truck North America appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on FMCSA Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124. While we conditionally support the proposed extension of automatic emergency relief to 30 days for conventional operations, we strongly urge FMCSA to use this rulemaking as a catalyst for addressing the broader policy challenges impacting the future of commercial vehicle safety, innovation, and sustainability. By establishing clear ODD protocols for autonomous CMVs, coordinating vehicle standards with EPA, and providing weight exemptions for zero-emission trucks, FMCSA can ensure that the commercial vehicle industry remains resilient, efficient, and technologically advanced, capable of serving the nation's needs both during emergencies and in the years to come. We stand ready to collaborate with FMCSA on these critical issues.

[END OF COMMENT]

### References
[3] https://ftrebbi.com/research/Note%20on%20State%20Regulatory%20Compliance%20Costs%20Dec_3_2024.pdf
[4] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/fmcsa-continuation-extension-emergency-declaration-under-49-cfr-ss-39025-no-2024-002-0
[5] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/archive-emergency-declarations-2024

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Bus Association (ABA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) proposed rule to extend the automatic regulatory relief period following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days. As the leading national trade organization for the intercity bus and motorcoach industry, representing approximately 3,400 members—90% of whom are charter and tour bus operators—ABA brings unique expertise in the operational realities, safety considerations, and financial landscape of passenger transportation during regional emergencies.

ABA conditionally supports the proposed extension to 30 days, recognizing that it addresses a critical operational deficiency identified by both the industry and FMCSA itself, particularly regarding the uncertainty created by late extension decisions under the previous 14-day limit [3]. However, the effectiveness and safety of this extension for motorcoach operators will depend on how comprehensively FMCSA addresses several interrelated issues: establishing clear, time-bound processes for extensions beyond 30 days; explicitly clarifying the scope of relief for passenger carriers, including driver qualification standards and the full range of motorcoach-specific regulations; providing bus-specific safety technology implementation timelines; offering motorcoach driver recruitment support; and formally recognizing the significant financial challenges facing the motorcoach industry post-COVID.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, which narrowed the automatic emergency relief period to 14 days and restricted its scope primarily to Hours-of-Service (HOS) rules under §§ 395.3 and 395.5 [4], [5]. This change, intended to enhance safety and reduce regulatory ambiguity, inadvertently created significant operational and compliance challenges for motorcoach operators engaged in emergency response. Regional emergencies, such as hurricanes, wildfires, and severe winter storms, frequently exceed two weeks in operational impact, requiring sustained "direct assistance" to restore essential services or evacuate populations [1], [6]. Under the 14-day limit, motorcoach operators were often forced to choose between breaching their emergency service contracts or operating out of compliance with federal regulations while awaiting FMCSA's extension decisions, which sometimes were not issued until close to the expiration deadline [3].

The current proposal to extend the automatic exemption period from 14 days to 30 days aims to restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, thereby reducing uncertainty and administrative burden for carriers responding to longer-duration emergencies [5]. This change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA itself.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The 14-day automatic relief window has proven operationally insufficient for motorcoach operators, creating significant burdens and risks:

*   **Operational Burden and Contractual Uncertainty:** Charter and tour bus operators frequently enter multi-week emergency relief contracts with state emergency management agencies, FEMA, or private clients to evacuate residents, transport displaced populations, and provide shelter-in-place support [1], [6]. These contracts involve substantial upfront costs, including driver scheduling, fuel procurement, maintenance positioning, and equipment deployment. The previous system, where FMCSA extension decisions were sometimes issued close to the 14-day expiration, created a contractual and financial cliff for motorcoach operators [3]. For example, a September 2024 regional emergency declaration covering seven states in response to Hurricane Helene required sustained motorcoach deployments [1]. If relief expired mid-contract without a timely extension decision, operators faced an impossible choice: breach their emergency service contract or operate out of compliance with federal safety regulations. This uncertainty is particularly acute for smaller ABA member carriers operating on thin post-COVID margins.

*   **Compliance Costs for Small Operators:** The motorcoach industry is composed predominantly of small businesses—many operating fewer than 10 vehicles—that lack dedicated compliance departments. When an automatic exemption expires at 14 days and an extension must be filed under 49 CFR §390.25, these small operators must divert owner-operator time to regulatory paperwork during active emergency operations [3]. FMCSA's own archive shows multiple 2024 emergency declarations that required formal extension actions (e.g., an October 2024 regional declaration that expired within 23 days), confirming that the 14-day window routinely triggers extension filings [2]. Each such filing represents hours of administrative time that small motorcoach companies—already strained by post-COVID recovery and a persistent driver shortage—cannot easily absorb.

*   **Ambiguity on Scope of Relief:** The 2023 final rule narrowed the scope of automatic relief to primarily HOS rules, leaving ambiguity about whether driver qualification file requirements, medical certificate currency checks, and pre-employment screening obligations were also suspended [4], [5]. This ambiguity compounds the problem: regional disasters such as severe winter storms or wildfires routinely exceed two weeks in operational impact. Our members cannot safely and legally deploy full motorcoach capacity under a 14-day ceiling when road clearance, shelter-in-place orders, and displaced-population transport needs persist for three to four weeks [5].

*   **Need for Bus-Specific Safety Technology Implementation Timelines:** Motorcoach operators have invested significantly in safety technologies such as electronic logging devices (ELDs), collision mitigation systems, and driver monitoring tools [9]. During regional emergencies, drivers may operate under extreme fatigue, on unfamiliar routes, and with reduced oversight. A blanket 30-day exemption that suspends HOS and driver qualification standards without any safety floor could expose our members to liability, undermine their safety culture investments, and—critically—put passengers at risk during the most vulnerable moments of a disaster [8]. Post-COVID, our industry is still recovering financially and cannot absorb the reputational or legal consequences of a high-profile motorcoach incident during an emergency deployment.

*   **Challenges in Motorcoach Driver Recruitment:** The motorcoach industry faces a persistent driver shortage [3]. During regional emergencies, ABA members must rapidly mobilize drivers who may be sourced from neighboring states or who hold qualifications that require administrative verification under normal conditions. The 2023 rule's narrowing of automatic relief to primarily HOS rules left ambiguity about whether driver qualification file requirements, medical certificate currency checks, and pre-employment screening obligations were also suspended [4], [5]. A 14-day window compounds this problem, making it difficult to recruit and deploy qualified drivers for extended emergency operations.

*   **Financial Challenges of the Motorcoach Industry:** The motorcoach industry is composed predominantly of small businesses—many operating fewer than 10 vehicles—that lack dedicated compliance departments. ABA members operating in the post-COVID recovery environment are running leaner fleets and thinner margins [3]. A compliance misstep during an emergency deployment—such as a vehicle inspection interval violation or a driver qualification file gap triggered by rapid crew mobilization—can result in DOT penalties now exceeding $18,760 per highway safety violation as of 2024 [6], [7]. This financial risk deters participation in disaster response contracts, even when motorcoaches are among the most critical assets for evacuation and relief efforts [6].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the proposed 30-day extension effectively supports the motorcoach industry's vital role in emergency response while maintaining robust safety standards, ABA offers the following recommendations:

*   **Establish a Transparent, Time-Bound Process for Extensions Beyond 30 Days:** FMCSA should codify a procedural requirement that any decision on extending emergency relief beyond the automatic 30-day period must be issued no later than day 21 of the initial relief period—providing at least 9 days of advance notice to carriers before the automatic exemption expires [1]. This procedural safeguard would allow motorcoach operators to make informed contractual and operational commitments without facing mid-deployment regulatory uncertainty.

*   **Explicitly Clarify Coverage for Motorcoach Operators:** FMCSA should adopt the proposed 30-day restoration AND issue accompanying guidance clarifying that passenger-carrying motorcoach operators providing evacuation, shelter, or emergency transportation services are fully covered under 49 CFR §390.23 relief, with the same automatic extension as freight carriers [1]. Additionally, FMCSA should create a simplified, online notification mechanism for motorcoach carriers to self-certify emergency operations status, reducing the administrative compliance burden on small operators during active disaster response.

*   **Restore Broad Scope of Regulatory Relief for Motorcoach Operators:** FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic exemption window AND simultaneously clarify—through accompanying guidance or regulatory text—that emergency relief for motorcoach operators encompasses the full range of applicable FMCSRs under parts 390–399, consistent with the pre-2023 regulatory posture [6], [7]. The agency should publish a clear matrix of which regulatory provisions are automatically suspended under a regional emergency declaration so that operators, drivers, and enforcement personnel have unambiguous guidance during fast-moving disaster scenarios.

*   **Explicitly Cover Motorcoach Driver Qualification Standards:** FMCSA should (1) adopt the proposed 30-day extension, (2) publish accompanying regulatory guidance that explicitly lists which driver qualification standards under 49 CFR Part 391—including medical certificate verification, road test documentation, and pre-employment drug testing timelines—are suspended alongside HOS rules during the automatic relief window, and (3) create a streamlined, publicly accessible notification mechanism so that ABA member operators can confirm in real time which specific regulations are covered under a given Governor's emergency declaration, reducing compliance uncertainty during fast-moving disaster deployments [4], [5].

*   **Address Interaction with Successive Governor Declarations:** In the final rule, FMCSA should (1) explicitly address how the 30-day clock interacts with amended, extended, or successive Governor declarations covering the same disaster event; (2) require that FMCSA's public emergency declaration portal be updated within 24 hours of any triggering declaration and include a plain-language summary of which specific regulations are suspended, the precise start and end dates of relief, and whether the declaration covers passenger-carrying motor carriers, freight carriers, or both; and (3) establish a direct notification channel—such as an email or SMS alert system—through which registered motor carriers, including ABA members, receive automatic notice when relief is triggered or expires in states where they hold operating authority [4], [5].

*   **Incorporate Bus-Specific Safety Technology Implementation Timelines:** FMCSA should extend the exemption window to 30 days as proposed, but simultaneously codify a tiered relief framework that distinguishes between administrative/paperwork requirements (which may be fully suspended) and core safety-critical standards (which should remain in effect or be subject only to limited, documented waivers) [8], [9]. Specifically, the agency should clarify that driver medical qualification standards, minimum rest requirements of no less than 8 consecutive hours in any 24-hour period, and functional operation of required safety technology (e.g., ELDs, automatic emergency braking where installed) are not automatically suspended under the extended window. The agency should also require carriers invoking the exemption to maintain an internal log of driver hours and assignments for post-emergency audit.

*   **Support Motorcoach Driver Recruitment:** Beyond HOS relief, FMCSA should explore developing programs or resources to support motorcoach driver recruitment, acknowledging the persistent shortage [3]. This could include streamlined, temporary qualification processes for emergency responders, or collaborating with industry stakeholders to promote careers in motorcoach driving.

*   **Recognize Motorcoach Industry Financial Challenges:** FMCSA should include in the final rule's regulatory impact analysis a disaggregated cost-benefit assessment that separately accounts for passenger carrier compliance costs versus freight carrier compliance costs [3]. The agency should also commit to publishing, within 180 days of the final rule's effective date, a plain-language compliance guide specifically for small motorcoach operators explaining how the 30-day automatic exemption interacts with state emergency declarations, FMCSA regional declarations, and the extension process under §390.25 [3].

[CONCLUSION]
ABA strongly supports the principle of extending the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days. This change is a necessary step to align regulatory flexibility with the operational realities and safety needs of motorcoach operators during regional emergencies. However, the full benefit of this extension for passenger transportation will only be realized if FMCSA adopts a more comprehensive and nuanced approach that explicitly addresses the unique challenges faced by the motorcoach industry. By clarifying the scope of relief, ensuring timely notification, establishing safety floors, supporting driver recruitment, and recognizing financial constraints, FMCSA can transform the 30-day extension from a numerical adjustment into a genuinely workable and safe framework for the charter and tour bus industry.

ABA appreciates the opportunity to comment on this important rulemaking and stands ready to collaborate with FMCSA to develop practical, safe, and effective solutions for emergency transportation.

### References
[1] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]
[Docket Number: FMCSA-2025-0124]

The Honorable Administrator Robin Hutcheson
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE
Washington, DC 20590

**Re: Comments on FMCSA-2025-0124: Automatic Emergency Relief Period**

Dear Administrator Hutcheson,

The Indiana State Police Commercial Vehicle Enforcement (ISP-CVE) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) regarding FMCSA-2025-0124, which proposes to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days.

As the primary agency responsible for commercial vehicle enforcement on Indiana's critical transportation network, ISP-CVE is intimately familiar with the operational complexities and safety challenges inherent in managing commercial motor vehicle (CMV) traffic on high-volume interstate corridors, including I-65, I-70, and I-80. While this NPRM specifically addresses the duration of emergency relief, ISP-CVE takes this opportunity to highlight three fundamental, related needs that directly impact our ability to effectively enforce federal motor carrier safety regulations (FMCSRs) and ensure public safety on these vital arteries of commerce: increased MCSAP funding, inspector training standardization, and the deployment of technology-assisted inspection tools. These needs are critical regardless of the specific length of automatic emergency relief.

[BACKGROUND]
ISP-CVE operates under the framework of the Motor Carrier Safety Assistance Program (MCSAP), authorized by 49 CFR Part 350. MCSAP funding is essential for supporting the personnel, training, and equipment necessary to conduct roadside inspections, enforce Hours-of-Service (HOS) regulations, ensure compliance with hazardous materials transportation requirements, and perform critical post-crash drug and alcohol testing under 49 CFR Part 382.

Indiana's geographic position at the "crossroads of America" means our interstate system carries an exceptionally high volume of CMV traffic, including significant rerouted freight during national or regional emergencies [1, 2]. This unique operational environment places immense demands on our enforcement resources, requiring robust MCSAP support and consistent, high-quality inspector training to maintain effective safety oversight.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Increased MCSAP Funding**
ISP-CVE relies heavily on MCSAP funding to support inspector salaries, training, equipment, and outreach programs. Current funding levels are insufficient to meet the operational demands of Indiana's interstate system, which carries some of the nation's highest CMV volumes. This funding shortfall directly impacts our capacity for proactive enforcement and our ability to recruit and retain qualified inspectors.

*   **Impact on Operational Burden:** On high-volume corridors like I-65, I-70, and I-80, our inspectors face immense pressure to conduct efficient and thorough inspections while managing significant traffic flow. Limited MCSAP funding restricts our ability to staff inspection facilities and deploy mobile enforcement teams at optimal levels. This leads to understaffing and reduced capacity for proactive enforcement, meaning fewer inspections are conducted, and fewer safety-critical violations are identified before they contribute to crashes.
*   **Impact on Inspector Recruitment and Retention:** Inadequate MCSAP funding directly affects our ability to recruit and retain qualified inspectors. The demands of roadside enforcement, particularly on high-volume corridors, are significant. New recruits require extensive initial training and ongoing professional development, which are costly. Experienced inspectors face high workloads and constant exposure to hazardous conditions, contributing to attrition. The high-stakes environment where inspectors must make real-time regulatory determinations [5] further compounds this challenge. Increased funding is essential to offer competitive salaries, enhance training programs, and provide better equipment, all of which are vital for attracting and retaining the skilled personnel needed to maintain effective CMV safety oversight [6].

**2. Inspector Training Standardization**
While FMCSA provides core training, there is a critical need for enhanced standardization and regular updates for all MCSAP inspectors, particularly in complex regulatory areas. Inconsistent training leads to varied interpretation and application of regulations at roadside, which can undermine enforcement credibility and safety outcomes.

*   **Impact on Inconsistent Enforcement and Safety Outcomes:** On high-volume corridors, inspectors frequently encounter CMVs operating under a patchwork of emergency exemptions (state and federal), HOS regulations, and hazardous materials requirements. Without highly standardized and regularly updated training, there is a risk of inconsistent enforcement. For example, during emergency relief periods, our inspectors must make rapid determinations about the validity and scope of exemptions [5]. Similarly, post-crash drug and alcohol testing under 49 CFR Part 382 requires strict adherence to specific protocols [3, 4]. Inconsistent training in these areas can lead to errors that compromise the integrity of safety data, create legal challenges, and ultimately, undermine our ability to ensure public safety.
*   **Impact on Operational Burden and Legal Risk:** Inspectors making real-time regulatory determinations on high-volume corridors require robust, standardized training to avoid errors that could lead to wrongful citations or undermine enforcement credibility. The complexity of interpreting various state and federal emergency declarations [1, 2, 8] demands a consistent, defensible standard, which can only be achieved through comprehensive and uniform training programs. This standardization would reduce the ambiguity that burdens frontline inspectors and contributes to attrition, as noted in the context of the original NPRM's impact on inspector recruitment and retention [8].

**3. Technology-Assisted Inspection Tools**
The deployment of advanced, technology-assisted inspection tools is essential to enhance efficiency, safety, and data-driven enforcement capabilities on Indiana's major freight corridors. Manual processes are time-consuming and limit our ability to conduct targeted, risk-based inspections.

*   **Impact on Inefficient Operations and Data Collection:** Manual processes for inspecting CMVs, verifying credentials, and documenting violations are inherently less efficient and more prone to error than technology-assisted methods. On high-volume corridors, this inefficiency directly reduces the number of inspections that can be conducted and can delay traffic flow. Our MCSAP grant-funded enforcement programs operate on structured deployment schedules tied to inspection targets and safety priorities [6]. Time spent on manual verification tasks consumes valuable inspector hours that should be dedicated to safety-critical inspections.
*   **Impact on Limited Data-Driven Enforcement:** Better technology, such as automated license plate readers, weigh-in-motion systems, and integrated electronic logging device (ELD) verification tools, would allow for more targeted, risk-based enforcement. Currently, our ability to leverage real-time data effectively is limited. Enhanced technology would improve data input and utilization for systems like the Motor Carrier Management Information System (MCMIS) [7], which underpins our enforcement strategies. For example, real-time access to carrier safety performance data would enable inspectors to prioritize high-risk vehicles, thereby maximizing the safety impact of limited enforcement resources.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
ISP-CVE urges FMCSA to consider the following recommendations to enhance CMV enforcement, safety, and operational efficiency on high-volume interstate corridors:

1.  **Increase MCSAP Funding:** FMCSA should advocate for and secure increased, stable MCSAP funding levels that accurately reflect the operational demands on high-volume interstate corridors like Indiana's. This funding should explicitly support inspector salaries, equipment upgrades, and ongoing professional development to attract and retain qualified personnel.
2.  **Standardize Inspector Training:** FMCSA should develop and implement enhanced, standardized training curricula for all MCSAP inspectors, with a particular focus on complex regulatory areas (e.g., HOS, hazmat, drug and alcohol testing) and the consistent application of enforcement protocols across state lines. This should include regular refresher courses and modules on emerging technologies and regulatory changes to ensure inspectors are equipped to navigate evolving operational environments, including emergency relief periods.
3.  **Deploy Technology-Assisted Inspection Tools:** FMCSA should invest in and facilitate the deployment of advanced inspection technologies, including automated pre-screening tools, integrated ELD verification systems, and real-time data analytics platforms. These tools are critical for enhancing efficiency, safety, and data-driven enforcement capabilities on major freight corridors.

[CONCLUSION]
ISP-CVE supports the proposed rule to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days, as it aligns with the operational realities of managing CMV traffic during extended regional emergencies. However, the effectiveness of any regulatory framework, including emergency relief provisions, is contingent upon the strength of the underlying enforcement infrastructure. Increased MCSAP funding, standardized inspector training, and the deployment of technology-assisted inspection tools are indispensable for enhancing CMV enforcement, safety, and operational efficiency on Indiana's critical transportation network.

ISP-CVE remains committed to collaborating with FMCSA to achieve our shared goal of improving CMV safety on our nation's highways.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

[Name and Title of ISP-CVE Representative]
Indiana State Police Commercial Vehicle Enforcement
[Address]

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Honorable Meera Joshi
Administrator
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, D.C. 20590

Re: FMCSA-2025-0124: Duration of Automatic Regulatory Relief Following a Regional Emergency Declaration

Dear Administrator Joshi,

As a CMV driver fatigue researcher, I am writing to express my strong support for the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) proposed rule to extend the duration of automatic regulatory relief following a regional emergency declaration from 14 days to 30 days. My research focuses on understanding and mitigating driver fatigue through advanced monitoring technologies and fatigue risk management systems (FRMS). The current 14-day automatic exemption period creates significant challenges for both safety oversight and scientific inquiry into driver performance during extended emergency operations. Restoring the 30-day window is not only operationally necessary but also scientifically critical for evaluating the effectiveness of fatigue detection technologies and FRMS under real-world, high-stress conditions.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA is proposing a revision to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs) regarding the duration of automatic regulatory relief. Specifically, the proposal seeks to extend the automatic exemption period from 14 days to 30 days, applying to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself. This rulemaking aims to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby reverting the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to that issuance.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The 14-day automatic relief window, while intended to provide immediate flexibility, has created an artificial research blind spot precisely when fatigue risk is highest and most scientifically consequential – the extended operational surge that follows major regional disasters.

First, my research on driver drowsiness and alertness monitoring depends on capturing comprehensive fatigue data across the full arc of emergency response operations. Emergency deployments rarely resolve within 14 days. For example, during Hurricane Helene between September 23–27, 2024, seven states simultaneously issued emergency declarations, triggering relief periods that, under the 14-day rule, would have expired while drivers were still operating under elevated stress, disrupted sleep schedules, and extended duty cycles [1]. Similarly, the FMCSA itself had to issue multiple continuation orders during the 2024 Port of Baltimore crisis, extending relief well beyond the initial 14-day window [2]. When the automatic exemption expires at 14 days, my monitoring systems – which require sustained data collection to distinguish acute fatigue from cumulative sleep debt – lose regulatory context precisely when fatigue accumulation is most pronounced. A 30-day window aligns with the biological reality that cumulative fatigue effects compound over weeks, not days, and allows my research instruments to capture the full fatigue trajectory rather than an artificially truncated snapshot [3]. The truncated 14-day automatic relief period prevents researchers from studying the most safety-critical phase of emergency operations – the period between days 15 and 30 – when cumulative fatigue effects are most pronounced and when in-cab monitoring technologies are most needed.

Second, the current asymmetry between state-declared (14-day) and federally-declared (30-day) emergency relief periods creates a methodologically incoherent research environment. Under the current framework, a driver operating under a Governor's declaration faces a different HOS compliance clock than one operating under an FMCSA national declaration – even when both are responding to the same disaster event [1]. This inconsistency forces my research team to apply different analytical frameworks to drivers who are experiencing functionally identical operational conditions, introducing confounding variables that weaken statistical conclusions.

Third, the 14-day limit creates a critical data gap in longitudinal fatigue monitoring studies. My research program conducts longitudinal in-cab driver monitoring assessments that require continuous, uninterrupted data streams to establish statistically valid fatigue baselines and detect cumulative alertness degradation patterns. When emergency relief expires at 14 days, carriers operating under exemption revert to standard HOS compliance postures, fundamentally altering driver behavior and invalidating ongoing study protocols [7]. Industry data from Seeing Machines' Guardian system, for instance, captured over 11.6 million confirmed risky driving events across 3.6 billion kilometers globally between October 2023 and September 2024, demonstrating the prevalence of risky driving behaviors that can be monitored by in-cab technology [7]. The 14-day cutoff means my team loses the ability to study driver fatigue trajectories during the second and third weeks of emergency operations, which is precisely when cumulative fatigue risk compounds most significantly [7]. The AASM has commented on the utility of DMS as a tool for road safety, and this rulemaking offers an opportunity to gather real-world data [8].

Finally, while I strongly support the 30-day extension, I must address an unintended consequence the agency may not have fully considered: without accompanying guidance on driver monitoring technology standards during extended relief periods, the longer exemption window could inadvertently increase fatigue-related crash risk. My research consistently demonstrates that population-level HOS rules are blunt instruments—some drivers exhibit significant alertness degradation well within standard HOS limits, while others maintain adequate alertness beyond them. During a 30-day emergency relief period, this variability becomes a critical safety concern because carriers lack the regulatory structure that normally constrains scheduling decisions. Without in-cab monitoring requirements or FRMS standards tied to the extended relief period, the 30-day window could expose the most fatigue-susceptible drivers to unacceptable risk levels during weeks three and four of emergency operations [7]. The Commercial Vehicle Safety Alliance's support for the 30-day extension, while appropriate, should not be read as endorsement of unmonitored extended operations [6].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To maximize the safety benefits and research opportunities presented by the proposed 30-day automatic relief period, I recommend the following:

1.  **Restore 30-Day Automatic Relief Period with Enhanced Monitoring Requirements:** FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period AND simultaneously require that carriers operating under emergency exemptions maintain enhanced fatigue monitoring data throughout the entire exemption period. This data should include ELD records, actigraph data where available, and alertness monitoring system logs. This data should be made available to FMCSA-approved researchers under appropriate privacy protections. This would transform the extended exemption period from a pure safety tradeoff into a scientifically productive opportunity to build the evidence base for fatigue risk management during emergencies [1, 2, 3, 7].
2.  **Standardized Data Reporting Requirements:** FMCSA should publish standardized data reporting requirements for carriers operating under emergency exemptions, specifically documenting duty hours, rest periods, and any fatigue-related incidents during the exemption window using a format compatible with existing ELD data architecture. This would allow researchers to build a longitudinal dataset of emergency-period fatigue outcomes that currently does not exist in any systematic form [1].
3.  **Tiered Safety Requirement for Extended Operations:** FMCSA should adopt the 30-day extension as proposed but pair it with a tiered safety requirement: carriers operating beyond 14 days under automatic emergency relief should be required to either (1) implement a documented FRMS with driver alertness monitoring, or (2) demonstrate that drivers are utilizing validated in-cab fatigue detection technology [6, 7]. This two-track approach preserves the operational flexibility that emergency responders need while ensuring that the additional 16 days of relief are accompanied by compensatory safety measures proportionate to the extended fatigue risk exposure [6, 7].
4.  **Solicit Public Comment on Technology Validation Standards:** FMCSA should solicit public comment on appropriate technology validation standards for driver monitoring systems used for fatigue detection. This would ensure the reliability and effectiveness of these critical safety technologies [7, 8].
5.  **Research Partnerships:** FMCSA should establish a formal mechanism – potentially through its research program – to partner with academic researchers to instrument a sample of emergency-response fleets during future extended declarations. Alternatively, FMCSA should establish a voluntary data-sharing framework for DMS and alertness data collected during emergency relief periods. This would address key research gaps identified by FMCSA and convert an operational necessity into a safety research opportunity [1, 2, 3].

[CONCLUSION]
Extending the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days is a crucial step towards aligning regulatory flexibility with the realities of emergency response operations. However, this extension must be accompanied by proactive measures to leverage fatigue monitoring technologies and FRMS to ensure driver safety and generate much-needed empirical data. By adopting the recommendations outlined above, FMCSA can support both operational efficiency and evidence-based safety policy, ultimately enhancing the well-being of CMV drivers and the communities they serve during critical times.

Thank you for considering these recommendations. I welcome the opportunity to discuss them further and contribute my expertise to this important rulemaking.

Sincerely,

Dr. Susan Soccolich
CMV Driver Fatigue Researcher

### References
[1] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008
[2] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/fmcsa-continuation-extension-emergency-declaration-under-49-cfr-ss-39025-no-2024-002-0
[3] https://downloads.regulations.gov/FMCSA-2018-0248-8319/attachment_1.pdf
[6] https://landline.media/truck-drivers-support-changing-back-to-30-day-emergency-window/
[7] https://seeingmachines.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Guardian-Insights-Report-2023-24.pdf
[8] https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-2024-05-06/pdf/2024-09776.pdf
