{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed rule FMCSA-2025-0124, which seeks to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. As the leading national association representing manufacturers of reversing alarms and other audible backing safety devices, NARAM is deeply committed to enhancing commercial motor vehicle (CMV) visibility, eliminating blind spots, and promoting the use of effective safety technologies to protect workers, pedestrians, and the public. We strongly support the FMCSA's proposal to restore the 30-day automatic emergency relief window, as this change is critical for ensuring consistent safety equipment standards, facilitating robust incident data collection, and supporting the future development and implementation of advanced CMV safety mandates, including camera and sensor systems.

[BACKGROUND]
The FMCSA’s proposed rule specifically addresses the duration of automatic regulatory relief following regional emergency declarations issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or the FMCSA itself. The proposal seeks to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, which reduced the automatic exemption period from 30 days to 14 days. By extending the window back to 30 days, the FMCSA aims to restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, recognizing that the current 14-day limit may be insufficient for addressing the logistical and safety needs inherent in regional emergencies, which often require longer periods of "direct assistance" to restore essential services or supplies. While the provided text does not explicitly cite specific CFR sections, these regulations typically fall under **49 CFR 390.23** regarding relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
NARAM supports the proposed 30-day restoration because the pattern of repeated FMCSA extensions following the 2023 rule change—necessitated by emergencies that consistently outlasted the 14-day window—demonstrates that the shorter period creates administrative fragmentation that distorts safety incident reporting timelines and imposes unnecessary operational burden on the cost-benefit demonstration process for safety equipment mandates. Each time FMCSA issues a piecemeal extension beyond the initial 14-day period (as occurred with Hurricane Helene, Hurricane Milton, and the Maryland Port of Baltimore emergency [1, 2]), a new regulatory clock resets. This fragmentation means that incident data is tagged to multiple overlapping declaration periods rather than a single continuous emergency window, making it analytically difficult for NARAM to aggregate and present clean longitudinal safety data. When NARAM petitions for or defends reversing alarm standards, we must demonstrate that backing incidents occur at statistically significant rates under specific operational conditions. Fragmented emergency declaration data undermines the integrity of that analysis and increases the cost and complexity of preparing compliant cost-benefit submissions [8, 9].

The National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM) strongly supports extending the automatic emergency exemption window from 14 to 30 days, because the truncated 14-day window created by the 2023 rule has undermined the evidentiary foundation needed to conduct credible cost-benefit analyses for safety equipment mandates—including reversing alarm requirements, camera systems, and proximity sensors—by artificially compressing the incident data collection period during emergencies when backing accidents are most likely to occur. NARAM members rely on FMCSA incident and crash data to demonstrate the safety value of reversing alarms, cameras, and sensors in cost-benefit proceedings. When emergency exemptions expire at 14 days, commercial motor vehicle operators may continue operating in degraded, post-disaster environments—congested staging areas, debris-strewn worksites, improvised loading zones—but without the regulatory relief framework that would otherwise ensure consistent data capture and reporting. The result is a data gap in precisely the high-risk operational window (days 15–30 of a declared emergency) where backing incidents are most likely to spike due to non-standard operating conditions. This gap directly weakens NARAM's ability to present statistically robust incident data when advocating for or defending reversing alarm, camera, and sensor mandates before the agency [1].

The National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM) strongly supports restoring the 30-day automatic emergency exemption window because the compressed 14-day period has created a documented evidentiary gap that undermines the agency's ability to conduct rigorous cost-benefit analyses for safety equipment mandates—including those governing reversing alarms, cameras, and backup warning systems on commercial motor vehicles. NARAM's core operational mission involves developing and defending safety equipment standards, which requires robust incident data from real-world emergency operations. When the automatic exemption window was shortened to 14 days in October 2023, safety incidents occurring between Day 15 and Day 30 of emergency operations fell into a regulatory gray zone: carriers operating under extension requests faced administrative uncertainty, and incident reporting during that window became inconsistent. This data gap directly impairs our ability to demonstrate the cost-benefit case for reversing alarm, camera, and sensor mandates on vehicles operating in disaster-response contexts—precisely the high-risk, high-congestion environments where backing alarm and camera effectiveness is most demonstrable. Without clean, continuous incident data across the full arc of emergency operations, our standards development work is compromised [8, 9].

Furthermore, the National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM) supports extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days because emergency conditions routinely persist beyond 14 days, and the truncated relief window creates perverse pressure on fleet operators to bypass or disable safety-critical backup alarm systems, cameras, and sensors in order to comply with noise ordinances or avoid operational disruption during extended disaster response operations—undermining the very worker and pedestrian safety protections our members' products are designed to provide. During extended regional emergencies such as Hurricanes Helene and Milton—where FMCSA itself issued multiple successive emergency declarations and extensions across several states well beyond 14 days [2, 3]—fleet operators managing disaster relief logistics face simultaneous pressure from local noise ordinances targeting backup alarms and from FMCSA safety compliance requirements. When the automatic relief window expires at 14 days but emergency conditions persist, operators in our members' customer base report informal pressure to disable or muffle backup alarms to avoid noise complaints in affected communities, creating serious occupational safety hazards. NARAM's standards development work is directly impaired when the regulatory environment incentivizes circumvention of alarm systems rather than adoption of our newer, adaptive-tone technologies designed to minimize noise pollution while maintaining safety performance [2, 3].

The National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM) strongly supports extending the automatic emergency exemption period from 14 to 30 days, because the current 14-day window creates disruptive mid-emergency regulatory uncertainty that forces fleet operators to make hasty, inconsistent decisions about safety equipment configurations—including reversing alarm systems, cameras, and proximity sensors—across mixed vehicle fleets during the most critical phases of disaster response. NARAM members manufacture and certify reversing alarm systems to specific federal safety standards. During regional emergencies, fleet operators frequently deploy vehicles across state lines and across vehicle classes (commercial trucks, utility vehicles, construction equipment) in configurations that were not pre-certified together. When the exemption window is only 14 days, operators face a hard deadline mid-emergency that forces them to either halt operations or rapidly reconfigure equipment to restore full regulatory compliance—often without access to certified technicians or replacement components in disaster-affected areas. FMCSA's own emergency declaration extension history (as documented in NEFI's reporting on FMCSA extending emergency declarations well beyond initial windows [4]) demonstrates that real-world emergencies routinely exceed 14 days. A 14-day cliff creates a compliance crisis precisely when fleet operators are most stretched, incentivizing shortcuts that may result in reversing alarms being disabled, bypassed, or improperly substituted across vehicle types—undermining the very safety standardization our members work to achieve [4, 7]. This operational uncertainty also hinders the consistent deployment and data collection from other vital safety technologies, such as cameras and sensors, which rely on stable operating conditions to inform future blind spot elimination mandates [5, 6].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on the analysis above, NARAM offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Finalize the Proposed 30-Day Automatic Emergency Relief Extension:** We urge the FMCSA to finalize the proposed rule extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days, as this change is essential for restoring a stable regulatory environment during extended regional emergencies and preventing administrative fragmentation of safety incident data.
2.  **Establish a Formal Protocol for Safety Equipment Incident Data Collection:** FMCSA should adopt the proposed 30-day automatic window and further clarify in the final rule that incident data collected across the full 30-day period—including any extension periods—will be treated as a continuous evidentiary record for purposes of safety equipment cost-benefit analyses. Specifically, NARAM recommends the agency amend the extension request process to include a standardized safety incident reporting field covering the full exemption period, ensuring that future cost-benefit analyses for safety equipment mandates (including reversing alarm, camera, and sensor requirements) are not distorted by administrative gaps in the data record [1, 8, 9]. The agency should also publish aggregated emergency-period incident statistics in a standardized format following each major declaration, enabling safety equipment manufacturers to incorporate this data into future rulemaking submissions without duplicative data-cleaning efforts.
3.  **Clarify that Emergency Relief Does Not Supersede Federal Occupational Safety Requirements for Alarms, Cameras, and Sensors:** FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period as proposed. Additionally, FMCSA should include explicit language in the final rule clarifying that backup alarm, camera, and sensor safety requirements under applicable OSHA and FMCSA standards remain in full force during emergency relief periods, and that noise ordinance compliance relief extends to safety-mandated audible warning devices. This will eliminate the ambiguity that currently drives operators toward unsafe workarounds during prolonged emergencies [2, 3]. NARAM further recommends that FMCSA publish guidance specifying that the exemption applies to hours-of-service and administrative documentation requirements, not to active safety device operability standards, to prevent ambiguity in the field. This would allow our members to maintain a stable standards baseline while still providing carriers the operational flexibility they need during extended emergencies.
4.  **Leverage the Stable 30-Day Window for Future CMV Safety Mandates:** The restored 30-day automatic relief window provides a more robust foundation for future rulemaking initiatives aimed at enhancing CMV visibility, eliminating blind spots through camera and sensor mandates, and updating backup alarm standards. By ensuring a consistent and uninterrupted period for emergency operations, the FMCSA can gather more reliable, longitudinal incident data that will be crucial for conducting rigorous cost-benefit analyses and demonstrating the effectiveness of these vital safety technologies. NARAM supports the 30-day automatic relief extension and urges FMCSA to use this rulemaking to clarify that emergency relief provisions do not and cannot supersede federal occupational safety requirements for backup alarms, cameras, and sensors on commercial motor vehicles, thereby preventing the unintended consequence of the extended relief period being misread by state and local authorities as authorization to enforce noise ordinances against federally-mandated safety equipment during disaster response. NARAM supports the proposed rule and specifically urges FMCSA to use the restored 30-day window as an opportunity to establish a formal protocol for capturing safety equipment incident data during the full duration of emergency exemptions, which would directly strengthen the cost-benefit record for reversing alarm, camera, and backing safety technology mandates on commercial vehicles operating in disaster-response scenarios.

[CONCLUSION]
NARAM strongly supports the FMCSA’s proposal to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. This change is not merely an administrative adjustment; it is a critical step towards improving CMV safety, ensuring consistent enforcement of safety equipment standards, and enabling the robust data collection necessary to inform future mandates for enhanced visibility, camera and sensor systems, and updated backup alarm technologies. We urge the FMCSA to finalize this proposed rule and to incorporate our recommendations to further strengthen its positive impact on public and occupational safety.

Respectfully submitted,

The National Association of Reversing Alarm Manufacturers (NARAM)

### References

[7] https://www.trucksafe.com/post/2023-regulatory-year-in-review-2024-outlook

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The AAA Foundation for Traffic Safety (AAAFTS) is a publicly funded, 501(c)(3) nonprofit research and education organization dedicated to saving lives by preventing traffic crashes and reducing injuries. For over 75 years, the AAA Foundation has been a leader in conducting rigorous, evidence-based research on critical traffic safety issues, including drowsy driving, distracted driving, and commercial motor vehicle (CMV) safety. Our mission is underpinned by a commitment to data-driven policy recommendations and public education campaigns that promote safer roads for all users. We appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed rule to extend the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from 14 days to 30 days (Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124).

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA is proposing to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, which narrowed the automatic exemption period for regional emergency declarations to 14 days [1]. The current proposal seeks to restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state by extending the automatic exemption period back to 30 days, applicable to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA itself. This change primarily affects regulations under **49 CFR 390.23**, which governs relief from regulations during emergencies. FMCSA suggests that the current 14-day limit may be insufficient for addressing the logistical and safety needs inherent in regional emergencies, which often require longer periods of "direct assistance" [PROPOSED RULE TEXT].

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The AAA Foundation supports extending the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days, provided FMCSA pairs this extension with robust fatigue risk monitoring requirements, enhanced data collection, and clear public education campaigns on sharing roads safely with trucks during emergencies. Our research consistently demonstrates that emergency operations lasting beyond 14 days present compounding drowsy driving hazards and create critical data gaps that hinder effective safety analysis and public outreach.

**Drowsy Driving Research Data:**
The AAA Foundation's own studies, including our landmark research on the prevalence and impact of drowsy driving, demonstrate that fatigue-related crash risk escalates non-linearly with cumulative sleep deprivation. When commercial drivers operate under extended hours-of-service (HOS) exemptions during multi-week emergencies, they enter precisely the high-risk fatigue profiles our research has characterized [1]. A blanket 30-day exemption without embedded safety checkpoints undermines the scientific basis of our public education campaigns, which rely on regulatory frameworks that reflect the known physiology of driver fatigue. Our ability to credibly communicate drowsy driving risks to the public is weakened when federal policy appears to treat extended fatigued driving as an acceptable norm during emergencies [1].

Furthermore, the 14-day exemption window creates a dangerous data gap that prevents our researchers from capturing the full arc of fatigue-related and distracted driving crash risk during extended disaster response operations—precisely the period when CMV driver safety risk is highest and least studied [2]. When the exemption window is only 14 days but actual emergency operations extend well beyond that—as occurred during Hurricane Helene in September–October 2024, when seven states issued declarations and FMCSA subsequently had to issue continuation extensions—our researchers face a fragmented dataset [2, 4]. The first 14 days fall under automatic relief, but days 15–30 often involve a patchwork of extension requests [3, 4], creating inconsistent regulatory conditions across carriers and states that make it methodologically impossible to isolate the safety effects of HOS relief on crash rates. This undermines the validity of our longitudinal studies on driver fatigue during disaster response [2].

As an organization whose mission is grounded in evidence-based traffic safety research, the AAA Foundation is directly constrained by the absence of reliable, emergency-period-specific commercial vehicle safety data [3]. FMCSA's own data resources, including the 2024 Pocket Guide to Large Truck and Bus Statistics [5], provide robust baseline crash and inspection data but do not disaggregate incidents by emergency declaration status or day-within-declaration. This means that neither FMCSA nor independent researchers can currently answer the agency's own implicit question: what is the actual safety record of commercial vehicle operations during the 14-to-30-day window of emergency declarations? Without this data, the agency is making a consequential regulatory decision—one that affects driver hours, fatigue exposure, and public road safety—without the empirical foundation that sound rulemaking requires [3].

Existing CMV crash data—including FMCSA's own crash statistics showing Class 8 vehicles account for approximately **66–69% of CMV fatalities**—indicates that the safety risk profile during extended emergency operations is not adequately captured or mitigated under the current 14-day framework [7]. When emergency relief periods are administratively fragmented—with some carriers operating under automatic 14-day relief, others under extension, and others having reverted to full HOS compliance—the resulting crash data is confounded and cannot be used to draw reliable conclusions about whether HOS relief increases or decreases crash risk during emergencies [7].

**Fatigue Countermeasure Requirements:**
The current 14-day limit creates a fragmented and operationally uncertain relief environment that undermines the safe, coordinated delivery of emergency supplies across all transportation modes during extended disaster events [8]. Drivers operating under uncertainty about their HOS relief status—unsure whether an extension will be granted before the 14-day window closes—face compounded fatigue and decision-making stress [8]. Our drowsy driving research consistently shows that regulatory ambiguity and unpredictable work schedules are independent risk factors for fatigue-related crashes. When carriers and drivers cannot plan multi-day relief operations with confidence, the safety benefits of the exemption framework are partially negated [8].

FMCSA's own emergency declarations for Hurricanes Helene and Milton (Declarations 2024-008 and 2024-010, issued October 4 and 17, 2024, with extensions running through November 26, 2024) demonstrate that real-world disaster response operations routinely span six to eight weeks—far exceeding the current 14-day automatic relief window [4]. Our researchers studying drowsy driving and HOS fatigue among commercial drivers cannot draw valid conclusions about emergency-period safety performance when the regulatory framework creates an artificial discontinuity at day 14 that distorts both carrier behavior and data reporting [4]. The 2023 reduction to 14 days was itself a safety-motivated policy response, and simply reversing it without addressing the underlying fatigue science creates an unintended consequence: normalizing extended fatigued driving as an emergency management tool [6].

**Public Education Campaigns on Sharing Roads with Trucks:**
The AAA Foundation's public education programs on drowsy and distracted driving depend on consistent, science-aligned regulatory signals [6]. When FMCSA oscillates between 14-day and 30-day windows based on industry petitions rather than fatigue research, it creates confusion in our educational messaging and undermines public trust in the regulatory framework [6]. Our teen driver and aging driver safety programs specifically teach that HOS rules exist because fatigue impairs driving as severely as alcohol—a message that is harder to convey when those rules are suspended for extended periods without clear safety rationale [6]. Additionally, our multi-modal safety perspective requires us to consider not just commercial truck drivers but also the emergency responders, passenger vehicle drivers, and vulnerable road users who share corridors with emergency freight during disaster recovery [3]. A patchwork of expiring and re-issued exemptions creates inconsistent traffic management conditions that elevate risk for all road users [8].

The AAA Foundation's research mission depends on consistent, complete crash and incident data from commercial motor vehicle operations during emergency periods. When the exemption window is artificially capped at 14 days, carriers operating under informal or ad hoc arrangements in days 15–30 of an emergency may not be accurately captured in FMCSA's reporting systems [4]. This creates a systematic blind spot in our research datasets. Our review of FMCSA's 2023 and 2024 emergency declaration archives reveals that state-level declarations frequently align with 14-day or 30-day windows based on the underlying state executive order—for example, North Carolina’s EO288 was explicitly set for 30 days, while Nebraska’s flooding orders were set for 14 days [2, 3]. This inconsistency means that the federal automatic relief window may be misaligned with actual state operational timelines, creating periods where drivers believe they are operating under relief but are not, or vice versa—a confusion that our driver education research identifies as a significant contributor to non-compliance and crash risk [4].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
The AAA Foundation supports extending the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days, but we strongly urge FMCSA to pair this extension with robust safety measures, enhanced data collection, and clear public communication to mitigate drowsy driving risks and enhance overall traffic safety during emergencies.

1.  **Implement Tiered Relief with Fatigue Countermeasure Requirements:**
    *   FMCSA should adopt the 30-day extension but structure it as a tiered relief framework rather than a flat exemption [1].
    *   Specifically, days 1–14 should carry the current automatic full relief [1].
    *   Days 15–30 should require carriers to implement a documented Fatigue Risk Management Plan (FRMP) as a condition of continued relief, including mandatory rest minimums and driver self-reporting protocols [1]. This should include mandatory rest stops every 8 hours and prohibition on driving between 2:00–6:00 AM, consistent with AAA Foundation research on drowsy driving crash risk windows [7].
    *   FMCSA should pair the 30-day automatic exemption period with a mandatory mid-period safety monitoring checkpoint at day 14—not as a re-authorization trigger, but as a data collection and driver wellness review mechanism [8].

2.  **Enhance Drowsy Driving Research Data Collection and Transparency:**
    *   FMCSA should require post-emergency data collection from carriers operating under the extended window—including any crash, near-miss, or hours-of-service violation data—and share that data with research organizations like the AAA Foundation to enable evidence-based evaluation of whether the 30-day window is appropriately calibrated [1].
    *   The agency should also establish a standardized post-emergency safety data reporting protocol, submitted to FMCSA within 60 days of exemption expiration, to build a national evidence base on CMV safety during emergency operations [2].
    *   FMCSA should adopt the 30-day extension and simultaneously publish a data collection plan requiring that all carriers invoking emergency relief beyond 14 days report any crashes, near-misses, or hours-of-service deviations to FMCSA within 72 hours of occurrence [3]. FMCSA should then publish an annual Emergency Operations Safety Report aggregating this data, disaggregated by disaster type, declaration day, and geographic region [3].
    *   We recommend that FMCSA engage external research partners—including the Foundation—to conduct independent analysis of this data to assess whether the 30-day window is appropriately calibrated or whether future adjustments are warranted [3]. This evidence-based feedback loop would transform a currently data-poor regulatory area into one grounded in the same rigorous standards that govern other aspects of commercial vehicle safety regulation [3].
    *   FMCSA should commission or formally partner with independent traffic safety research organizations—including the AAA Foundation—to conduct a prospective study of crash rates, fatigue indicators, and hours-of-service patterns during future emergency relief periods under the restored 30-day window. This study should be built into the rulemaking as a condition of the extension, with findings used to inform a future data-driven determination of the optimal relief duration [6].
    *   FMCSA should direct its data collection partners to flag and separately code crash and inspection records occurring during days 15–30 of emergency declarations, enabling researchers like the AAA Foundation to isolate and analyze the safety profile of extended emergency operations [4].

3.  **Implement Public Education Campaigns on Sharing Roads with Trucks During Emergencies:**
    *   FMCSA should clarify in the final rule's preamble that the 30-day window is an operational ceiling for extraordinary circumstances, not a routine expectation, to preserve the integrity of hours-of-service regulations as a safety tool [6].
    *   FMCSA should issue accompanying guidance requiring state emergency management agencies to align their CMV-related executive order durations with the federal relief period [4].
    *   Additionally, FMCSA should create a real-time public dashboard tracking active emergency declarations, their geographic scope, applicable relief provisions, and any reported safety incidents—enabling research organizations, insurers, and safety advocates to monitor safety conditions during relief periods and submit data-informed comments in future rulemakings [4].
    *   FMCSA should publish aggregated safety outcome data from each emergency declaration period (crashes, fatalities, HOS violations) in a publicly accessible format to enable independent research organizations like ours to evaluate the safety tradeoffs of extended relief [7].
    *   The agency should also clarify in the final rule that the 30-day clock applies uniformly across all transportation modes covered by the FMCSRs to avoid multi-modal coordination gaps during regional emergencies [8].

[CONCLUSION]
The AAA Foundation for Traffic Safety supports the proposed extension of the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. However, this support is contingent upon FMCSA's commitment to implementing robust fatigue countermeasure requirements, establishing comprehensive data collection protocols to inform future drowsy driving research, and developing clear public education campaigns on sharing roads safely with trucks during emergencies. By adopting a tiered relief framework, mandating fatigue management plans, and fostering data transparency, FMCSA can ensure that operational flexibility during emergencies does not compromise driver safety or public trust in HOS regulations. We urge FMCSA to incorporate these recommendations into the final rule to achieve a balanced approach that supports emergency response while upholding the highest standards of traffic safety.

### References

{"draft": "December 4, 2024

The Honorable Robin Hutcheson
Administrator
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE
Washington, DC 20590

**Re: Comment on Proposed Rule, Duration of Automatic Regulatory Relief Following Regional Emergency Declarations (Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124)**

[INTRODUCTION]
Dear Administrator Hutcheson,

Walmart Private Fleet appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) proposed rule (Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124) to extend the duration of automatic regulatory relief following regional emergency declarations from 14 days to 30 days. As the largest private truck fleet in America, operating over 10,000 drivers and a vast network of distribution centers serving stores across all 50 states, Walmart Private Fleet is deeply committed to ensuring road safety and supply chain resilience, particularly during emergencies.

We support the agency's objective of extending the automatic emergency relief window, as the current 14-day limit is often insufficient to address the complexities of prolonged regional emergencies. However, we are concerned that a simple durational extension, without corresponding clarifications and operational guidance, could inadvertently create significant compliance risks, operational burdens, and potential safety challenges for large, sophisticated fleets like ours. Our comments below are intended to help FMCSA craft a final rule that enhances emergency response effectiveness while upholding safety and incentivizing industry-leading investments.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to revise the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations (FMCSRs), specifically by extending the automatic exemption period for regional declarations from 14 days to 30 days. This change applies to regional declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA itself. As noted in the proposed rule text, this rulemaking is being proposed to reverse a specific modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the automatic time limit for regional emergency exemptions back to the 30-day limit that existed prior to that final rule. While the specific CFR sections are not explicitly cited in the proposed rule text, these regulations typically fall under 49 CFR 390.23, which governs relief from regulations during emergencies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
Walmart Private Fleet’s comprehensive emergency response operations do not begin at the disaster perimeter; they commence at regional distribution centers hundreds of miles away, moving essential goods continuously across multi-leg supply chains for weeks. Under the current 14-day window, our drivers and dispatchers face compliance uncertainty after day 14 when the emergency is still ongoing [1].

**1. Ambiguity of 'Direct Assistance' and Upstream Supply Chain Operations:**
The definition of 'direct assistance' to emergency relief efforts, particularly in the context of an extended 30-day duration, is critically important. If interpreted narrowly to cover only drivers physically operating within a declared emergency zone, the vast majority of our fleet supporting that emergency – moving water, shelf-stable food, and emergency supplies from distribution centers to stores serving affected communities – may fall outside the exemption entirely. This creates a perverse operational outcome: the supply chain backbone enabling emergency relief is penalized while only the final delivery leg receives regulatory flexibility. As the nation's largest private fleet, our ability to rapidly restock stores with essential goods, which are often depleted within hours of an emergency, is fundamental to community recovery [2].

**2. HOS Monitoring Complexity for Large, Multi-State Fleets:**
As the largest private truck fleet in America with 10,000+ drivers operating distribution-center-to-store routes across all 50 states, Walmart Private Fleet routinely has drivers operating in multiple states that may be under different emergency declaration statuses simultaneously. Under the current 14-day window, our compliance systems — including ELD monitoring, dispatch scheduling, and driver log auditing — are calibrated to a predictable, short-duration exemption cycle. Extending to 30 days without corresponding compliance infrastructure guidance means our fleet management systems must track which drivers are operating under exemption, in which states, for how long, and under what specific relief parameters, across a far longer and more variable window [3]. A single multi-state winter weather event, such as the January 2025 FMCSA emergency declaration covering Alabama, Arkansas, Colorado, Connecticut, and more than a dozen other states simultaneously [6], could place hundreds of our drivers under exemption status for a full month — creating audit exposure if our internal systems do not precisely mirror FMCSA's exemption boundaries.

**3. Disproportionate Burden of Retroactive Data Requests and Absence of Telematics Framework:**
Walmart has invested substantially in AI-enabled video telematics, ELD integration, and real-time safety scoring systems that generate far richer data than the industry average. However, the absence of a standardized federal framework for what constitutes a compliant telematics data submission means that each agency data request requires us to make independent legal and technical determinations about scope, format, and disclosure risk [4], [5]. Retroactively isolating and extracting safety incident data specifically from the 14-to-30-day window of prior emergency declarations—such as the multi-state Hurricane Helene declarations issued between September 23–27, 2024, across Florida, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia [4]—requires custom engineering of queries across multiple data systems, legal review, and significant IT resource allocation. For a fleet of our scale, even a single retroactive data request could consume hundreds of staff-hours and divert resources from our ongoing safety improvement programs. Smaller carriers with less sophisticated systems will submit incomplete or incomparable data, making any aggregate analysis unreliable, while large carriers like Walmart bear a disproportionate compliance cost for producing high-quality submissions.

**4. Safety Guardrails and Performance-Based Flexibility:**
Walmart's fleet operates under industry-leading safety standards, with driver compensation, training, and telematics-based coaching programs that consistently produce safety metrics exceeding industry averages. During emergency declarations, our drivers operate under extraordinary conditions—degraded road infrastructure, altered traffic patterns, increased load volumes, and fatigue risk—that are categorically different from normal operations. Our telematics data from periods like Hurricane Helene response would show elevated hard-braking events, route deviations, and extended drive times that, without proper contextual annotation, could appear as safety degradation rather than as the expected signature of emergency operations conducted responsibly [4], [5]. If FMCSA uses this raw data to evaluate whether the 14-to-30-day window is 'safe' to extend, it risks penalizing carriers like Walmart whose data is more complete and granular than smaller fleets, creating a perverse incentive against robust telematics investment. A blanket 30-day window, without mandatory mid-period safety reviews, could allow less safety-conscious carriers to operate fatigued drivers for extended periods without any regulatory touchpoint — ultimately harming the public trust and road safety environment in which our drivers operate [6], [7].

**5. Operational Planning Uncertainty and Electric Truck Integration:**
Walmart operates delivery scheduling systems optimized around Hours of Service constraints. During emergencies, our logistics technology platforms must make real-time routing and driver assignment decisions across thousands of active loads. The proposed 30-day extension, as currently drafted, does not address what happens when a state declaration lapses and is renewed, whether the 30-day clock resets, or how carriers operating across multiple overlapping state declarations (as occurred during the 2024 hurricane season) should account for HOS compliance [6]. For a fleet of our scale, even a 24-hour period of compliance ambiguity can affect hundreds of drivers and thousands of store deliveries. Our sustainability goals also require integrating electric trucks into emergency response rotations, and EV range and charging constraints make HOS flexibility even more operationally critical — a driver who cannot legally extend driving time may be stranded without a charging option, creating safety and service failures simultaneously [1], [2].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Walmart Private Fleet urges FMCSA to adopt the 30-day extension but incorporate the following critical clarifications and operational improvements:

1.  **Clarify Definition of 'Direct Assistance':** Adopt the proposed 30-day extension, which Walmart supports, and simultaneously issue clarifying regulatory text or guidance defining 'direct assistance' to expressly include carriers transporting essential goods (food, water, emergency supplies as defined under 49 CFR 390.23) through any leg of the supply chain that is causally connected to emergency relief efforts, regardless of whether the driver physically enters the declared emergency area. The agency should establish a tiered documentation framework allowing large private fleets to self-certify supply chain nexus to emergency relief operations, reducing administrative burden while maintaining accountability. (Addresses Point 1, "Performance-based regulatory flexibility").
2.  **Standardize Compliance Monitoring for Multi-State Operations:** Extend the period to 30 days as proposed, but simultaneously issue a companion guidance document specifying: (1) standardized ELD and fleet management system logging protocols for drivers operating under state-declared emergency exemptions, (2) a centralized, real-time public dashboard showing active state emergency declarations and their precise HOS relief parameters, and (3) safe harbor provisions for carriers who demonstrate good-faith compliance monitoring efforts during multi-state, overlapping emergency events. FMCSA should also clarify whether the 30-day clock resets upon a governor's renewal declaration. (Addresses Point 2, Point 6, Point 8).
3.  **Establish a Standardized Telematics Data-Sharing Framework:** FMCSA should: (1) publish a precise, standardized data specification—including field definitions, time-window calculation methodology, geographic boundary criteria, and acceptable file formats—before requesting any retroactive telematics or safety incident data from carriers; (2) provide a minimum 90-day response window for large fleets; (3) clarify whether the agency intends to use submitted data for rulemaking support, enforcement screening, or safety benchmarking; and (4) consider accepting aggregated, anonymized fleet-level safety metrics. Furthermore, FMCSA should initiate a parallel rulemaking or advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) to establish: (1) a standardized telematics data taxonomy, (2) clear safe-harbor provisions confirming that voluntarily submitted data will not be used for enforcement, and (3) a tiered reporting framework that scales data submission requirements to fleet size and telematics capability. (Addresses Point 3, Point 5, "Recognition of industry-leading safety investments").
4.  **Implement Safety Guardrails and Acknowledge Performance-Based Safety:** FMCSA should adopt the 30-day extension but require carriers operating under state-declared emergency relief beyond 14 days to submit a brief mid-period safety attestation (e.g., certification of adequate rest, no HOS-related incidents, and ongoing direct tie to relief efforts). FMCSA should also clarify in the final rule that relief applies only to operations 'directly supporting' the emergency, with explicit guidance for large multi-state fleets conducting routine restocking alongside emergency deliveries. The agency should require that any safety data submitted be accompanied by a carrier-defined contextual narrative, commit to publishing its analytical methodology, and weight data from carriers with demonstrated above-average safety records differently than data from carriers with prior compliance violations. (Addresses Point 4, Point 7, "Performance-based regulatory flexibility").
5.  **Provide Operational Planning Clarity and Support EV Integration:** FMCSA should amend the proposed rule to include: (1) explicit guidance on clock-reset procedures when a state declaration is renewed or a new declaration is issued for a continuing emergency; (2) a standardized carrier notification protocol allowing large fleets to register their emergency operations with FMCSA within 48 hours of invoking the exemption; and (3) a provision acknowledging that alternative-fuel vehicles (e.g., electric trucks) operating under emergency exemptions may require modified rest stop and routing accommodations that should not be treated as HOS violations, potentially including weight exemptions for electric trucks to ensure operational parity and prevent unintended HOS impacts due to increased battery weight. (Addresses Point 6, Point 8, "Electric truck weight exemptions").

[CONCLUSION]
Walmart Private Fleet strongly supports the policy goal of extending the automatic emergency relief window to 30 days. We believe this extension is essential for effectively responding to prolonged regional emergencies and maintaining critical supply chains for essential goods. However, to ensure the final rule is practical, enforceable, and genuinely enhances safety and resilience, FMCSA must address the operational complexities and compliance uncertainties inherent in an unstructured 30-day exemption. Our recommendations, informed by our extensive experience in emergency response and our industry-leading safety investments, are designed to help the agency achieve these objectives. We urge FMCSA to consider these comments as it finalizes this important rule.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

[Name/Title, Walmart Private Fleet]

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The International Brotherhood of Teamsters Highway Division respectfully submits these comments in response to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124, titled "Clarification of Automatic Extension of Regional Emergency Declarations." Representing tens of thousands of over-the-road and local truck drivers across the United States, our union possesses unique expertise in the operational impacts of Hours of Service (HOS) regulations, the challenges of carrier coercion, and the critical need for driver safety and economic security during emergency response operations. Our interest in this rulemaking is rooted in our commitment to protecting the health, safety, and economic well-being of our members, who are on the front lines of every regional emergency.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to revise **49 CFR § 390.23(a)(1)(ii)(B)** by extending the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days for regional emergency declarations issued by a Governor of a State, their authorized representative, or FMCSA itself. This revision aims to reverse a modification made by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby restoring the pre-October 2023 30-day limit for such declarations [6]. While the Teamsters Highway Division supports the principle of extending the automatic relief period to better align with the prolonged nature of regional emergencies, our support is critically conditioned upon the simultaneous implementation of robust anti-coercion enforcement mechanisms, mandatory driver health and fatigue monitoring requirements, and effective whistleblower protections. Without these concurrent safeguards, the extended exemption window risks exacerbating existing safety liabilities and undermining the well-being of the drivers our union represents.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
*   **The 14-Day Window's Insufficiency and Amplified Coercion Risk for Local Drivers:** The current 14-day automatic relief period is structurally insufficient to cover the full operational arc of regional emergencies, particularly for our local truck driver members who are often the last responders standing when interstate carriers have departed the disaster zone [3]. When a Governor declares a regional emergency, such as the Florida hurricane declarations documented in FMCSA's 2023 archive (e.g., Emergency Order 082823, effective 08/28/2023, expiring 09/25/2023), local drivers hauling fuel, water, construction materials, and debris removal loads remain operationally engaged well beyond the initial 14-day window. Under the current 14-day automatic trigger, our members' carriers face a compliance cliff that creates pressure on drivers to either stop critical relief hauling or continue under ambiguous legal status. This ambiguity is precisely the environment in which carrier coercion to violate Hours of Service (HOS) rules flourishes [2]. When relief expires automatically at 14 days and a formal extension has not yet been processed through the email-based request process under **49 CFR § 390.25**, dispatchers and fleet managers pressure drivers to keep moving under the fiction that the emergency 'still applies,' exposing drivers to both safety risk and regulatory liability without the legal protection of a valid exemption.

*   **"Deadline Coercion" and the 14-Day Cliff:** The 14-day automatic relief period creates an operational cliff that perversely incentivizes carriers to pressure drivers to maximize hours in the final days before the exemption expires, creating a concentrated coercion spike [1]. Our members report that as emergency exemption deadlines approach, dispatcher pressure intensifies sharply. Under the 14-day window, carriers facing supply chain obligations tied to emergency response—fuel, food, medical supplies—compress maximum-hour demands into a shrinking timeline. This 'deadline coercion' dynamic is particularly acute for over-the-road drivers on multi-day hauls who may be mid-route when the exemption expires, creating ambiguous compliance situations that carriers exploit. A 30-day window, paired with the Section 390.25 extension request process, provides a more rational planning horizon that reduces the frantic end-of-exemption pressure our members currently experience [1].

*   **Long-Term Health and Safety Impacts of Extended HOS Relief Without Oversight:** Our members who drive over-the-road routes are disproportionately affected during emergency declarations because extended HOS exemptions remove the primary federal guardrail against carrier coercion [4, 5]. When the exemption window was 30 days prior to October 2023, Teamsters representatives documented recurring patterns of carriers pressuring drivers to run beyond safe limits under the cover of emergency relief, with no corresponding obligation on carriers to track or report driver health outcomes. A 30-day window without a health-and-safety data collection requirement means that fatigue-related incidents, stress-induced health deterioration, and lapses in employer-sponsored health insurance during extended emergency deployments go entirely unrecorded. This directly undermines our ability to advocate for members' long-term health and retirement security, because there is no evidentiary record linking extended emergency operations to downstream health costs borne by drivers and their families [4]. Furthermore, local drivers, unlike over-the-road counterparts who can rotate out of emergency zones, have no such option. They live and work in the same emergency zone, often dealing with personal displacement, family stress, and community trauma while simultaneously being asked to work extended hours under HOS relief. A 30-day automatic exemption, without accompanying fatigue mitigation requirements, means a local driver could theoretically operate under relaxed HOS rules for the entire month. FMCSA's own crash data research (presented at the 2024 Safety Research Forum) underscores that fatigue remains a leading causal factor in commercial vehicle crashes [2]. Extending the relief window without addressing cumulative fatigue risk for local drivers creates a safety liability that falls disproportionately on the workers our union represents [2].

*   **Administrative Vacuum and Ambiguity Driving Coercion:** Over-the-road Teamster drivers operating during regional emergencies, such as Hurricanes Helene and Milton (FMCSA Declarations 2024-008 and 2024-010, which required multiple extensions stretching from October 4 through November 26, 2024—a period of over 50 days), face particularly acute coercion risk during the gap between the 14-day automatic exemption expiring and a formal extension being granted [8, 9]. Our contract enforcement experience shows that during these administrative gaps, dispatchers and fleet managers pressure drivers to 'keep rolling' regardless of HOS status, knowing that enforcement ambiguity provides cover. Drivers who refuse face retaliation, load assignment penalties, or threats to their independent contractor status—a misclassification vulnerability we already combat. The 30-day automatic window eliminates this coercion pressure point for the most critical initial phase of emergency response [8]. Drivers have reported uncertainty about when the 14-day automatic exemption expires and whether a renewal or extension has been formally issued. This ambiguity creates a compliance gray zone in which drivers are unsure whether they are operating legally, and carriers have exploited this uncertainty to pressure drivers to continue operating without confirming the legal status of the exemption [6, 7]. A clear, automatic 30-day window gives our members a definitive legal basis to understand their rights and obligations. The October 2023 final rule's reduction to 14 days, while well-intentioned in limiting the scope of HOS relief, inadvertently created an administrative burden that falls disproportionately on drivers—not carriers—because drivers bear personal liability for HOS violations regardless of what their dispatcher told them about exemption status [6, 7].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
The International Brotherhood of Teamsters Highway Division strongly supports the proposed revision to **49 CFR § 390.23(a)(1)(ii)(B)** to extend the automatic emergency relief period from 14 days to 30 days. However, this support is contingent upon the simultaneous adoption of robust driver protection measures to prevent carrier coercion, monitor driver health and safety, and ensure accountability during extended emergency operations. Without these concurrent safeguards, the extended exemption window risks exacerbating existing safety liabilities and undermining the well-being of the drivers our union represents.

Accordingly, we recommend that FMCSA adopt the 30-day extension in conjunction with the following critical provisions:

1.  **Strengthened Anti-Coercion Enforcement and Whistleblower Protections:**
    *   **Mandatory Driver Notification:** Require carriers invoking emergency HOS relief to affirmatively notify drivers in writing or electronically of the specific declaration number, geographic scope, effective date, and 30-day expiration date of the relief being claimed. This notification must also explicitly inform drivers of their right to refuse unsafe dispatch or to take rest periods within the exemption window without fear of retaliation, load assignment penalties, or adverse impacts on their employment or independent contractor status [1, 8]. This addresses the ambiguity that enables coercion (impacts 1, 6, 8).
    *   **Expedited Coercion Complaint Review:** Establish an expedited coercion complaint review track—separate from the standard National Consumer Complaint Database (NCCDB) process—with a 15-day agency response target for complaints filed during active emergency exemption periods. This addresses the slowness of current complaint mechanisms and provides real-time protection for drivers during vulnerability windows (impact 1).
    *   **Dedicated Emergency Coercion Hotline/Portal:** Create a dedicated, publicly accessible emergency-period coercion hotline or online portal for drivers to report pressure to violate HOS rules without fear of retaliation. This provides an alternative reporting channel and supports whistleblower protections (impact 5).
    *   **Clarification of Anti-Coercion Regulations:** Clearly state in the final rule's preamble and accompanying guidance that the anti-coercion regulations at **49 CFR § 390.6** apply with full force during emergency exemption periods and that carriers cannot use the exemption as a shield against coercion complaints [2, 5, 6]. This addresses the misinterpretation and exploitation of exemptions by carriers (impacts 2, 5).
    *   **Carrier Logging and Voluntary Compliance:** Require carriers invoking emergency HOS relief to maintain and submit driver logs demonstrating voluntary compliance rather than dispatcher-directed violations during the exemption period. This creates an auditable record and supports enforcement (impact 5).

2.  **Mandatory Driver Health and Fatigue Monitoring:**
    *   **Incident Documentation and Reporting:** Condition the automatic 30-day exemption on a carrier obligation to: (1) maintain a daily log of all drivers operating under the exemption; (2) report any safety incidents, driver health complaints, or fatigue-related near-misses occurring during the 14-to-30-day window to FMCSA within 72 hours; and (3) certify that drivers operating beyond day 14 retain uninterrupted access to employer-sponsored health insurance or equivalent coverage [4]. This addresses the lack of long-term health data and safety oversight (impact 4).
    *   **Fatigue Risk Management Protocol:** Amend the proposed rule to require that carriers utilizing the 30-day automatic emergency relief for local and short-haul operations implement a documented fatigue risk management protocol after day 14 of continuous emergency operations. This protocol should include mandatory rest opportunities of no less than 10 consecutive hours every 24-hour period for drivers who have been operating under emergency relief for more than 14 consecutive days. Carriers should also be required to retain records of driver hours during the full 30-day period for post-emergency audit [2]. This addresses the unique fatigue risks for local drivers (impact 7).
    *   **Aggregated Safety Data Reporting:** FMCSA should commit to publishing an aggregated safety data report following each regional emergency declaration that utilized the full 30-day window, so that future rulemakings are grounded in actual incident data rather than assumptions [4]. This improves future rulemaking and oversight.

3.  **Enhanced Transparency and Driver Verification:**
    *   **Publicly Accessible Dashboard:** Require FMCSA to publish a publicly accessible, real-time dashboard or registry identifying the precise geographic scope, effective date, expiration date, and applicable regulatory relief provisions for each active regional emergency declaration. This transparency mechanism would reduce the information asymmetry that enables carrier coercion of drivers and allow drivers to independently verify their legal status [2, 8]. This addresses the ambiguity and administrative burden (impacts 2, 6, 8).

[CONCLUSION]
The International Brotherhood of Teamsters Highway Division believes that extending the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days is a necessary and practical adjustment to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations, reflecting the operational realities of regional emergencies. However, for this extension to genuinely serve public safety and the well-being of the trucking workforce, it must be paired with robust, concurrent safeguards against carrier coercion, comprehensive driver health and safety monitoring, and clear whistleblower protections. Without these essential measures, the extended HOS relief window will inadvertently create a longer period of vulnerability for our members, exposing them to intensified pressure, cumulative fatigue, and long-term health risks. We urge FMCSA to adopt the proposed 30-day extension in conjunction with the specific recommendations outlined above, ensuring that emergency relief does not come at the cost of driver safety and regulatory integrity.

### References
[1] https://truckx.com/new-fmcsa-rules-2024/
[2] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/research-and-analysis/2024-safety-research-forum-crash-data-overview
[3] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008
[4] https://landline.media/is-fmcsas-emergency-relief-window-increasing/
[5] https://www.freightwaves.com/news/fmcsa-to-restore-truckings-emergency-relief-window
[6] https://public-inspection.federalregister.gov/2026-00268.pdf
[7] https://prepass.com/blog/fmcsa-finalizes-changes-to-emergency-trucking-exemptions/
[8] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/ssc-extension-emergency-declarations-hurricanes-helene-and-milton-11-22-2024
[9] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/archive-emergency-declarations-2024

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]
The Honorable Meera Joshi
Administrator
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE
Washington, DC 20590

Re: Docket No. FMCSA-2025-0124: Clarification to the Applicability of Emergency Exemptions

Dear Administrator Joshi,

The Truck Renting and Leasing Association (TRALA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) seeking to extend the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from 14 days to 30 days following a regional declaration of emergency. TRALA represents over 500 truck renting and leasing companies, comprising the full spectrum of the industry, from the largest international leasing systems to independent single-location companies. Our members own and maintain a fleet of over 750,000 commercial trucks, tractors, and trailers, which are leased to motor carriers for both short-term rentals and long-term full-service leases. These vehicles are critical to maintaining the supply chain, particularly during regional emergencies.

TRALA supports FMCSA’s principle of restoring the 30-day automatic relief period, recognizing that regional emergencies often require more than 14 days to restore essential services or supplies [1]. However, we are deeply concerned that the current proposal does not adequately address the unique operational and compliance challenges faced by the truck renting and leasing industry, particularly regarding the allocation of safety compliance responsibilities between lessors and lessees during the extended relief window. Without explicit clarification, the 30-day automatic exemption could create dangerous ambiguity, leaving leased commercial vehicles operating under suspended inspection and maintenance standards without clear contractual or regulatory guidance on which party bears compliance obligations [5].

[BACKGROUND]
FMCSA's NPRM proposes to reverse a modification established by a final rule published in October 2023, thereby extending the automatic emergency regulatory relief period from the current 14 days to 30 days. This relief is triggered by a regional declaration of emergency issued by a Governor, their authorized representative, or FMCSA [1]. The stated purpose is to restore the regulatory landscape to its pre-October 2023 state, suggesting that the 14-day limit may be insufficient for logistical and safety needs during regional emergencies [1]. This change primarily affects operational requirements such as hours-of-service (HOS) under 49 CFR §§ 395.3 and 395.5, and potentially other regulations such as electronic logging device (ELD) use and vehicle inspection standards under 49 CFR Part 396. TRALA members’ operations are uniquely impacted by these changes due to their dual role as vehicle owners/lessors and their contractual and regulatory obligations under 49 CFR Part 376 regarding leased vehicles.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The proposed extension of the automatic relief period from 14 to 30 days, while generally positive for operational flexibility during emergencies, creates several critical unintended consequences and ambiguities for truck renting and leasing companies that FMCSA appears not to have fully considered.

First, a significant concern is the potential for the 30-day automatic exemption to be interpreted as suspending not just HOS requirements but also the broader vehicle maintenance and inspection standards that govern leased commercial motor vehicles under 49 CFR Part 376. TRALA members have affirmative contractual and regulatory obligations to ensure leased vehicles meet Federal safety standards [1]. If a lessee operates a leased vehicle for 30 days under emergency relief and an accident occurs involving a safety technology failure, TRALA members could face liability claims arguing that the emergency exemption somehow modified the lessor's maintenance obligations [1]. FreightWaves reporting on this rulemaking confirms that the 14-day period already created uncertainty about when and how exemptions applied; a 30-day period with no additional clarity will compound this problem for the leasing industry specifically [2].

Second, truck rental and leasing companies face a unique compliance challenge during emergencies with short-term rental vehicles. Customers (lessees) take physical custody of vehicles for days or weeks, yet lessors retain regulatory responsibility for vehicle condition under the FMCSRs [3]. When a 14-day automatic relief window expires mid-rental during a prolonged regional emergency—such as the North Carolina Executive Order 288 that ran a full 30 days (September 22, 2023 – October 22, 2023) [3]—TRALA members face an impossible situation. They cannot physically retrieve, inspect, or service vehicles that are actively deployed in disaster-response operations, yet they remain legally exposed for any maintenance deficiency that emerges after day 14 [3]. The 2023 final rule that shortened the window to 14 days directly worsened this exposure without providing any mechanism for lessors to transfer or suspend compliance obligations during the gap period. Extending this period to 30 days without clarification amplifies this operational burden and safety risk [5].

Third, when a regional emergency declaration triggers automatic regulatory relief, TRALA member companies face an immediate and unresolved question: who bears responsibility for ELD compliance and safety technology standards on leased vehicles during the exemption period? Under a 14-day window, this ambiguity was manageable because the period was short, and carriers typically resolved compliance questions quickly [1]. Extending the window to 30 days doubles the duration of this legal gray zone. For short-term rental vehicles in particular—where the lessee may change multiple times within a single 30-day emergency period—TRALA members could face situations where multiple successive lessees operate under the exemption using equipment that the lessor (TRALA member) installed and is contractually responsible for maintaining, yet the lessee controls day-to-day operation [1]. If an ELD malfunctions or a safety technology system fails during this extended period, it is unclear whether the lessor's maintenance obligations are suspended, modified, or unchanged by the emergency relief [1]. Moreover, extending HOS suspension to 30 days increases the window during which lessees may prefer to operate vehicles without active ELD monitoring—not for safety reasons, but to avoid HOS accountability. This creates a market distortion where TRALA members who have fully equipped their fleets may find their compliant vehicles less attractive to emergency lessees than older, less-equipped units from competitors who have not yet achieved full compliance [3].

Fourth, TRALA member companies lease and rent commercial trucks on short-term bases, often for periods of days or weeks. When a regional emergency declaration is issued, our members' vehicles may be operated by lessee motor carriers who are the regulated party for HOS and ELD compliance, while the lessor (TRALA member) retains responsibility for vehicle maintenance and inspection standards [6]. The current 14-day rule already creates ambiguity about which party must act—and within what timeframe—to bring vehicles back into compliance after relief expires [6]. Extending the period to 30 days without clarifying this lessor-lessee responsibility allocation compounds the problem: a short-term rental vehicle returned to the fleet after a 30-day emergency deployment may have deferred maintenance, altered ELD configurations, or inspection gaps that the lessor discovers only after the lessee's relief period has ended, leaving the lessor exposed to violations for conditions that arose during the lessee's emergency operation [6].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these critical concerns and ensure that the extension of automatic emergency relief does not inadvertently compromise safety or create undue regulatory burdens on the truck renting and leasing industry, TRALA offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Clarify Lessor vs. Lessee Safety Compliance Obligations:** In the preamble to the final rule, FMCSA should explicitly state that the automatic emergency relief period—whether 14 or 30 days—applies exclusively to operational requirements such as hours-of-service and does not modify, suspend, or otherwise affect the vehicle maintenance and inspection obligations of motor vehicle lessors under 49 CFR Part 376 or applicable state law [1]. FMCSA should further clarify that during the exemption period, the lessee (as the operating carrier) assumes full operational compliance responsibility, while the lessor retains its pre-existing maintenance and inspection obligations on the same schedule as if no emergency had been declared [1]. This clarification costs FMCSA nothing and would eliminate a significant source of legal uncertainty for TRALA members.

2.  **Establish Clear Guidance for Short-Term Rental Vehicle Compliance:** For short-term rental vehicles, FMCSA should restore the 30-day automatic relief period AND issue accompanying guidance specifying that, during the relief window, the lessee/motor carrier operating the short-term rental vehicle assumes primary responsibility for roadworthiness inspections under 49 CFR Part 396 [3]. This mirrors the existing lessor-lessee allocation framework and prevents TRALA members from bearing regulatory liability for vehicle conditions they cannot practically monitor or correct during active emergency deployments [3]. FMCSA should also clarify that the 30-day clock resets if a Governor issues a renewed or extended emergency declaration, consistent with how state emergency orders themselves operate [3].

3.  **Delineate ELD and Safety Technology Responsibilities:** FMCSA should accompany the final rule extending the relief period to 30 days with a specific regulatory clarification—either in the rule text or an accompanying guidance document—that explicitly addresses how ELD and safety technology installation and maintenance responsibilities are allocated between lessors and lessees during the automatic emergency exemption period [1]. Specifically, FMCSA should clarify: (1) whether lessors remain liable for ELD hardware functionality during the exemption period even if HOS recording requirements are suspended; (2) whether short-term rental vehicles cycling through multiple lessees during a 30-day emergency period require any documentation of the exemption's applicability; and (3) whether lessors must affirmatively notify lessees of the exemption's scope and limitations regarding onboard safety technology [1]. FMCSA should also clarify that the 30-day automatic relief applies exclusively to HOS hour-counting obligations under §§ 395.3 and 395.5, and does not suspend the requirement that ELD devices remain installed, powered, and functional on vehicles for which they are otherwise required [3]. This preserves the safety data record for post-emergency analysis, protects TRALA members' compliance investments, and prevents the relief period from becoming a de facto ELD exemption [3].

4.  **Implement Post-Emergency Vehicle Inspection Protocols:** FMCSA should accompany the 30-day extension with explicit regulatory guidance—or a supplemental notice—clarifying that: (1) during the automatic relief period, the lessee motor carrier bears primary compliance responsibility for operational regulations (HOS, ELD); (2) the lessor's vehicle maintenance and inspection obligations under 49 CFR Part 396 are tolled for the duration of the emergency relief period when the vehicle is in the lessee's possession and operating under the declaration; and (3) a defined post-return inspection window (e.g., 72 hours) is established for lessors to bring returned vehicles back into full compliance without penalty [6]. This would align the extended relief period with the practical realities of the truck rental and leasing business model.

[CONCLUSION]
TRALA supports the extension of the automatic emergency relief period to 30 days, as it provides crucial flexibility for motor carriers responding to regional emergencies. However, as vehicle owners and lessors, our members face unique compliance challenges that are not adequately addressed by the current proposal. Without clear guidance on the allocation of safety compliance responsibilities, particularly for vehicle maintenance, inspection, ELD functionality, and short-term rentals, the extended relief period could create dangerous ambiguities, compromise safety, and expose lessors to undue liability. We urge FMCSA to incorporate our recommendations to ensure that the final rule effectively supports emergency response while maintaining the highest standards of safety and regulatory clarity for all stakeholders.

Thank you for considering our comments. We look forward to continuing to work with FMCSA to achieve these shared goals.

Sincerely,

[Signature removed for privacy]
[Name removed for privacy]
President and CEO
Truck Renting and Leasing Association (TRALA)

### References
[1] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/regulations/federal-register-documents/2026-00268
[2] https://www.freightwaves.com/news/fmcsa-to-restore-truckings-emergency-relief-window
[3] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/archive-emergency-declarations-2023
[5] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/regional-emergency-declaration-and-extension-emergency-declarations-no-2024-008
[6] https://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/emergency/fmcsa-continuation-extension-emergency-declaration-under-49-cfr-ss-39025-no-2024-002-0
